1849 III (1849a). 
Discovered by Schweizer (Moscow) near fB Coronae 
Borealis in the evening of 11 April. 
Very clearly visible through a comet- 
seeker, though difficult to detect in moonlight (7 to 8™). 
After a few hours, 
discovered independently in Cambridge (U.S.A.). 
14 April, discovered in- 
dependently by Graham (Markree). 
Quite a bright nucleus, 
poorly shaped; 
avery blurred coma. 
Moved to the southwest; located in Virgo in the end 
of April. 
Before the perihelion passage last observed on 9 May (Marseilles) 
in Hydra. 
Observed also in Kazan', Bonn, Berlin and other places. 
From 
17 April to 1 May, observed by O. Struve (Pulkovo), who reported that on 
27Apriland28April, inspite of the low celestial position, 
the twilight and the 
bright moonlight, 
the comet was easily visible to the naked eye. 
29 
April and 1 May, comet equal in brightness to t Leonis (5™.16); no tail; a 
quite circular nebulosity with a fairly well defined nucleus. 
6 May and 7 May, 
observers in Kremsmunster reported the comet as faint due to its low posi- 
tion. 
After the perihelion passage, 
detected at the end of August by Bond 
(Cambridge, U.S.A.) and Challis (Cambridge, England); observed till 26 
Aug. (l0ptoed 122)

1850 I (1850a). 
Discovered by Petersen (Altona) on 1 May as a faint 
telescopic object. 
Located in Draco moving toward Ursa Major; in June 
passed through Draco into Bodtes; 
in July moved to the southern celestial 
hemisphere. 
In the north, observed for the last time in Virgo in the twilight 
of 28 July (Marseilles). 
5 Sept., detected in the southern sky and measured till 16 Oct. (Cape of 
Good Hope). 
In early May, Galle (Berlin) reported a very faint nebulosity 
of substantial extension (8 to 9™); 8 May, Schmidt (Bonn) gave D=3'. 
At 
that time Petersen described the granular appearance of the central con- 
densation as similar to a cluster of faint stars. 
Reliable determinations of 
the nucleus brightness were obtained by Schmidt with an 8-foot heliometer: 
14 May, 10™; 30 May, 9 to 10™;

At first the nucleus was glaringly white, but

tail was reliably measured by Schmidt: 
30 May, first faint trace of a tail; 
4 June, c=4'; 28Juneand 30June, c=28'; 2Julyto9 July, c= 1°14' to 1°38!. 
Schmidt reported variations in the visibility of the tail which could not be 
explained by variations in the atmospheric transparency or by other observa- 
tional circumstances. 
No tail reported on 10, 12, 13 and 26 June; hardly 
visible on 11, 13 and 21 July. On other days, the tail was clearly seen. 
2 July, Secchi (Rome) reported ''a magnificent tail'' 2° long, appearing as 
two parallel rays. He also reported variations in the tail; considerable 
activity in the comet's head near the nucleus was reported by Lassell 
(Liverpool), Schmidt (Bonn) and observers at the Washington Observatory. 
Petersen (Altona), Reslhuber (Kremsmunster) and others reported the 
comet as visible to the naked eye in July. 
4 July, according to Bond 
(Cambridge, U.S.A.) the nucleus was 11™ bright; 
8 July, the nucleus not 
as compact and as starlike 
as previously. 
10 July, a tail 5° long visible 
through a comet-seeker, 
curving noticeably with its convexity facing the 
zenith; 
the brightest part of the comet situated near the nucleus to the north 
of the tail axis (cloud formation ?). 
17 July, a starlike nucleus. 
After the rediscovery on 6 Sept., the comet was observed till 15 Oct. 
6 Sept., according to Maclear (Cape of Good Hope), the comet appeared as 
a round-shaped nebulosity about 5' in diameter with considerable central 
condensation. 
As the comet grew fainter, 
it increased in size. 
15 Oct. 
no central condensation; observations made with an 8.5-foot equatorial.

1850 II (1850b). 
Discovered by Bond Jr. (Cambridge, U.S.A.) in 
Camelopardus in the evening of 29 Aug. A faint object appearing as an in- 
significant concentration of luminosity through a 15-inch refractor; central 
condensation 1',.5 in diameter (8 to 9™). Moved to the east, mainly in right 
ascension. 
Discovered independently in Europe by Brorsen (Senftenberg) 
on 5 Sept. as a bright object with blurred edges (6 to 7™). 
9 Sept., dis- 
covered by Mauvais (Paris) as a whitish nebulosity 2 to 3' in diameter; no 
bright center or tail (6™ ?). On the same day detected by Robertson (Markree) 
as a cluster visible at small magnification: 
a very faint nebulosity occupying 
almost the entire field of vision of a 25-foot telescope. 
13 Sept., discovered 
independently by Clausen (Dorpat) in Lynx.

At the end of September, located in Cancer; 
in October, 
in Crater. 
17 Sept., Petersen (Altona) reported the comet as excellently visible with 
a sharp central nucleus. 
Traces of a tail sometimes appeared through a 
comet-seeker. 
General appearance similar to Herschel's nebula GC 1968 
(NGC 3060). 
Madler (Dorpat) observed the comet througha 5-inch refractor 
and on 15 Sept. reported it to be rather bright with faint nucleus and no tail.

faint, round-shaped nebulosity.''

in twilight (6 to 7™).

to bright twilight.

than in October, but still visible with no difficulty; 
last measured on 13 Nov.

1851 1 (1850c). 
The second recorded observation of Faye's short-period 
Comet. 
28 Nov. 1850, detected by Challis and co-workers (Cambridge, 
England) in Aquarius near the position calculated by Stratfordfrom Leverrier's 
elements. 
According to observations made on 28 Nov. and29 Nov. witha 12-inch 
equatorial, comet exceptionally faint even in an absolutely clear sky. 
When 15° above the horizon, couldnot have been fainter than10to11™. 6 Dec. 
and 7 Dec., hardly visible through haze. 
1 Jan. and 5Jan. 
1851, observed 
by Bond (Cambridge, U.S.A.) as a small nebulosity elongated towards the 
Sun (10 to 11™). 
22 Jan., Challis described the comet as a very faint object 
with a nucleus appearing intermittently (10 to 11™). 
24 Jan., hardly visible 
(11™). 
27 Jan., brighter and easily observed (9 to 10™) with a slightly elon- 
gated coma in the south-eastern direction and a nucleus (9 to 10™). 
O. Struve (Pulkovo) observing the comet from 24 Jan. to 7 Feb. 
1851 
described it as clearly visible and predicted possibility of observation for at 
least one more month (9 to 10™). 
26 Feb., comet near the horizon, but 
satisfactorily measured by Challis, who last measured it on 4 March in 
spite of its location in front of the zodiacal light (10° above the horizon) and 
its extreme faintness (9 to 10™).

1851 I1 (185la). 
The first recorded observation of the short-period 
d'Arrest Comet. 
Discovered by d'Arrest (Leipzig) in Pisces on 27 June. 
Moving east; located in Cetus in the end of July; between Taurus and 
Eridanus in September and October. 
Very faint (10™) on27Juneand29 June. 
Observed at Bonn, Berlin, Cambridge, Durham, Kremsmiunster, 
and other 
observatories. 
In August and September, 
a faint circular blurred object, 
no nucleus or tail (10™). Washington Observatory in August estimated the 
coma as 3! in diameter. 
Observed and measured for the last time on 6 Oct. 
by Galle (Berlin) with a 15-inch refractor (not fainter than 10™.5). 
References: A.N., 32-34; M.N., 11,.42; A.J., 2; Obs. Paris 19; 
Holetschek V, p. 37. 
Orbit: Leveau. —Ann. Obs. Paris 14:21.

m=11™.0.

1851 Ill (1851b). Discovered by Brorsen (Senftenberg) near the horizon 
in Canes Venatici on 1 Aug. (nearly full moon). 
A telescopic object; very 
bright in spite of the small size (7 to 8™). Passed through Bootes in August; 
located in Draco in September. 
12 Aug., in full moon, Plantamour (Geneva) noted a very faint comet. 
15 Aug., Secchi observed the comet easily (7™?). 
Till 23 Aug., observed 
in Konigsberg through a heliometer (6 to 7). 
11 Aug., 
Reslhuber 
(Kremsmunster) spotted the comet in full moon and reported that measure- 
ments entailed difficulties. 
20 Aug., slightly brighter; 
a small nucleus. 
Located again only on 22 Sept. as an exceptionally faint object vanishing 
from time to time (11™). Observed till 27 Sept. in Hamburg, and also 
measured in Liverpool, Durham, Altona, and other places. 
30 Sept., last 
position estimate made by Brorsen (10 to 11™) in clear sky. 
Brorsen 
looked for the comet with a 4-inch telescope near the pole, yet could find 
no trace of the object (fainter than 10-11™), notwithstanding the small dis- 
tance A=0.51. 
Apparently the comet rapidly exhausted itself and grew faint 
after the perihelion passage.

1851 IV (1851c). 
Discovered by Brorsen (Senftenberg) in Canes Venatici 
on the evening of 22 Oct. 
Bright, with glittering nucleus and bright tail 1° 
long (4™). As reported by Brorsen, was similar to 18471. 
Two tails; a 
large one directed away from the Sun and a small one toward the Sun. 
Passed through Bootes; 22 Nov., located in the western part of Hercules. 
24 Oct., Littrow (Vienna) reported two tails arranged almost along a 
straight line: large, very bright tail (up to 30' long and 5' wide) directed 
away from the Sun; small, 
dull tail (8' long) directed toward the Sun. 
Littrow observed the comet with the naked eye; through a refractor, nucleus 
was 6™ bright; glittered like a star. 
14 Nov., Argelander (Bonn) reported

nucleus appeared as a small star sometimes growing very bright within

2! wide at its most conspicuous part.

1852 1 (1852a). 
The fourteenth recorded apparition 
of the Encke- 
Backlund Comet. 
Detected 
by Vogel (London) on 9 Jan. as a very faint 
blurred nebulosity (11 to 12™). 
11 Jan., spotted in Liverpool, 
12 Jan., 
in 
Kremsmunster and 13 Jan. at the Harvard Observatory (U.S.A.). 
Observed 
in the mornings below Pegasus. 
By the end of January, considerable in- 
crease in brightness. 
While Hartnup (Liverpool) noticed the comet on 11 Jan. 
as a mere faint, luminous spot with 
D=1 to 2', on 25 Jan. the comet was 
much brighter, notwithstanding the proximity of the Moon. 
In February 
brightness continued to increase attaining 
a maximum during the first third 
of March; later, was invisible in bright evening twilight. 
8 Feb. , Carrington 
(Durham), Reslhuber (Kremsmiunster) and Ferguson (Washington) reported 
an increase in brightness. 
The comet rapidly increased in brightness be- 
coming an excellently visible object with a white central condensation (10™); 
a circular nebulosity without a nucleus. 
According to various observers, 
the condensation looked like a cluster of 12™ stars. 
D~2!. 
24 Feb. to 26 Feb., Galle (Berlin) described the comet as a bright, 
condensed nebula equal in brightness to a 7™ star. 
7 March, Reslhuber 
reported that had the comet not been located near the horizon, 
it would have 
been visible to the naked eye. 
A faint tail directed away from the Sun (5™). 
Schaub (Trieste) reported that on 7, 8 and 9 March the comet was bright 
enough to be easily located through a telescope, prior to the appearance of 
a 7 star (4 to 5™). 
8 March, Markree Observatory reported that the "comet 
has such a bright condensation as to be mistaken for a star!'' 
10 March, last 
measured in Washington; 
11 March, last observed in Konigsberg. 
12 March 
and 13 March, not detected in the bright twilight.

1852 II (1852b). 
Discovered by Chacornac (Marseilles) in Cepheus on 
15 May ap a very faint, diffuse nebula without a tail or a nucleus (9 to 10™). 
Moved to the pole. 
Could not be fainter than 10™, since discovered independ- 
ently on 17 May by Petersen (Altona) and on 18 May by Bond (Cambridge, 
U.S.A.), who reported the comet as a faint telescopic object (8 to 9™). 
Passed near the pole; 24 May, located at 1° from the pole. Observed in 
Berlin, Hamburg, 
Vienna, Cambridge, Bonn, Kremsmunster and other 
places. 
21 May to 24 May measured with the Konigsberg heliometer (not 
fainter than 8™), 
26 May, Littrow (Vienna) could not see the comet through 
a 5-foot telescope in moonlight and poor transparency (10 to 11™). 
In June, 
observed only in Vienna and Cambridge. 
Bond stated that the faintness 
made observations difficult (11"). Last measurement on 14 June (Cambridge).

1852 Ill (1852d). 
The last recorded apparition 
of the periodic Biela's 
Comet. 
Discovered by Secchi (Rome) in Gemini in the morning of 26 Aug. 
at a considerable distance from the precalculated position. 
Small in size. 
Secchi reported its precise, central occultation of a 9 to 10™ star on that 
day. During the occultation, 
the star appeared to be enveloped in light 
nebulosity; 
the comet did not disappear near the star (not fainter than 7 to 
8™). 
The second component (the comet split into two components in 1846) 
was not detected until 15 Sept. when it was located by Secchi not far from 
= Leonis as a very faint satellite elongated away from the sun, moving in 
front and to the north of the main, brighter part; no nucleus in satellite. 
The main part of the comet appeared "irregular" with two very faint "head- 
dresses"; the center brighter, but no nucleus. 
The most thorough observations were made from 18 Sept. to 28 Sept. by 
O. Struve (Pulkovo) with a 15-inch refractor. 
18 Sept., north component 
(the one moving in the front) appeared as a nebulosity brighter towards the 
center, 
no nucleus; intrinsic brightness equal to that of an 8 to 9™ star; 
20 Sept., the northern and the southern components located through a comet- 
seeker in the precalculated position. 
The two heads were of about the same 
magnitude, yet the northern head was slightly brighter with a starlike 
nucleus. 
Headofthenorthern component was round ~40"; head of the south- 
ern component elongated 1'40". A bright jet extending from the northern 
component in the approximate direction of the southern one. 
Struve advanced 
the assumption that this jet indicated interaction between the two bodies. 
23 Sept., the southern component, whose elongated shape was hardly dis- 
tinguishable, was fainter than the northern component and had no nucleus. 
The northern component perfectly concentric. 
25 Sept., the southern com- 
ponent considerably fainter than the northern; 
the northern component 
definitely observed through a comet-seeker, while the location of the south- 
ern one could only be sensed: 
The northern component 50 to 60" in diameter; 
the southern, 
30''. The northern component slightly elongated; the southern, 
circular. 
28 Sept., in bright moonlight and strong twilight, only the northern 
component visible, with difficulty. 
The comet disappeared below the horizon 
before the southern component could be observed.

reported the southern component to be much fainter than the northern one. 
19 Sept., the northern was fainter than the southern.

much fainter than the northern.

1852 IV (1852c). 
The first recorded apparition 
of the periodic Westphal 
Comet. 
Discovered on 24 July by Westphal (Gottingen) 2° to the south of 
Pisces. 
Appeared through a comet-seeker as a bright nebulosity several 
minutes in diameter (7 to 8™). 
Detected independently by Peters 
(Constantinople) 10 days later. 
Moving to the southeast; located in 
Andromeda in the end of August; passed through Andromeda and Cepheus 
in September; near the pole in the beginning of October; moved through 
Ursa Minor and Draco in Novembex; passed slowly through Ursa Major in 
February. 
Seen at various observatories. 
As reported by d'Arrest (Leipzig) in the second half of August, the comet 
appeared as a blurred nebulosity 2'.5 in diameter with a sharp nucleus. 
Beginning 17 Aug., traces of a tail (position angle 55°). 
2 Sept., Fearnly 
(Christiania) reported a 30" tail (position angle 225°). 
9 Oct., tail 5' long 
(5 to 10° angle). 
11 Oct., 9'(12°) with a jet 1' long extending from the nucleus. 
12 Nov., 
the nebulosity 3' in diameter. 
3 Dec., 
an 8! tail (0°). 
Visible till 
13 Dec., though too faint to be measured (9 to 10™). 
18 Aug., Reslhuber 
(Kremsmunster) estimated D=4 to 5!; 26 Aug., bright, with a definite fan- 
shaped short tail directed away from the Sun; bright nucleus (7 to 8™). 
10 Sept., location in the midst of the Perseus cluster caused a fainter ap- 
pearance. 
23 Sept., very bright in strong moonlight (6 to 7™). 
4 Oct., 
visible to the naked eye (5 to 6™). 
7 Oct., very bright andclearly visible to 
the naked eye (4 to 5™). Elongated 
nebulous envelope; eccentrically located 
nucleus; 
short, wide tail. 
14 Oct., brightness decreased. 
17 Nov., very 
faint. 
10 Dec., 
a faint trace of 
a comet (10™?). 
8 Jan. 
1853, measured in 
Liverpool; 
11 Jan., measured for the last time in Bonn (10 to 11™). 
Still 
visible on 9 Feb. (11™). 
Schonfeld (Bonn) saw the tail for the first time on 
4 Sept.; 
5 Sept., he saw the comet with the naked eye. 
19 Oct. and 20 Oct., 
the comet was as bright as the Andromeda Nebula, being then visible to the 
naked eye (4-5™).

1853 I (1853a). 
Discovered by Secchi (Rome) in Lepus on 6 March; 
discovered independently on 8 March by Schweizer (Moscow) and Tuttle 
(Cambridge, U.S.A.) and by Hartwig (Leipzig) on 10 March. 
Rather bright 
at discovery; 
5! (Secchi) to 8' (Schweizer) in diameter; 
no tail (6 to 7™). 
Moved north; located neara Orionis at the end of March, and to the south 
of Aldebaran in the middle of April. 
Observed for a short time at various European and American observa- 
tories. 
Secchi reported the absence of a single nucleus: 
the densest parts 
of the nebula seemed to consist of many bright points hampering the ob- 
servation. 
According to the observers in Bonn, where the comet's position 
was determined beginning 
/13 March, there were many bright points in the 
comet's head. 
Peters (Heidelberg), who observed the comet with a helio- 
meter, 
described it as very faint on 25 March (8 to 9™); on 1 April, quite 
bright; 
10 April, very faint; 
11 April, exceptionally faint, almost invisible. 
26 March, Reslhuber (Kremsmunster) reported a faint nebulosity without 
a nucleus; D=2'. 
Beginning 29 March, Reslhuber and Rumker (Hamburg) 
reported a rapid decrease in comet's brightness, 
so that on 7 April it 
could be detected only with considerable difficulty. 
Measured till 11 April 
(Rome, Heidelberg) (10™). 
Still observed on 18 April by Oudemans (Leiden) 
(10 to 11”). 
24 April, 
not measured in twilight due to considerable faintness. 
The brightness fluctuations are thus indisputable.

in

naked eye in the southern sky, yet was not discovered then. 
Absolute mag-

1853 II (1853b). 
Discovered by Schweizer (Moscow) near e Aquilae before 
the dawn of 5 April. A small telescopic comet, round-shaped, without a 
tail (7 to 8™), 
3' in diameter; 
a faint nucleus. 
Moved east; passed through 
Pegasus in April, then to the south; located in Eridanus at the end of April; 
at the beginning of May passed through Lepus into Canis Major. 
In April, 
observed in Hamburg and Berlin. 
16 April, Rumker reported the comet as 
quite bright with a nucleus and a small tail (6 to 7™). 
19 April, Bruhns 
reported the comet as glittering in bright moonlight, being equal to a 6™ star 
in brightness; 
a substantial tail directed away from the Sun (5™). 
The 
nucleus situated nearer to the sunward side of the nebulosity. 
28 April, 
Bruhns reported the comet to be very close to the Earth (A=0.09); should 
have been bright. 
After 24 April, the comet was not observed in Europe. 
Discovered independently in the southern sky with the naked eye as a 
bright luminary with a tail. 
30 April, spotted by Goodenaugh on board 
H.M.S. "Centaur" after sunset to the southwest of Orion; a bright nucleus 
(3 to 4™) and a tail about 4° long, extending almost to the zenith (2 to 3™). 
Rapidly grew faint; 
2 May, a 6™ nucleus; 
17 May, visible through a 2-foot 
telescope (6 to 7™). 
1 May, Constable (Melbourne) reported the comet to 
be a 1™ star, reddish in color with a bright 4° tail (M.N. 
14: 9) 
Bosquet (Port Louis) reported a 10° tail directed toward Orion. 
2 May, the 
comet grew considerably smaller and fainter. 
30 April, King (Parramatta) 
estimated the head at 1/4 of the lunar diameter. 
1 May, Maclear (Cape of 
189

Good Hope) estimated the nucleus at 4™; the tail 4° long. 30 April, Wright 
saw the comet on board "Lady Jocelyn"; a 1™ starlike, yellow-blue nucleus. 
Tail extending to Rigel, 
6° long and 20! wide at its end. 
1 May, a 3° tail; 
nucleus grew fainter. 
2 May, a 1°%5 tail; very faint nucleus, 
6 May, comet 
faint; telescopic appearance: 
an oval nebulosity surrounding a nucleus; 
major diameter 1°, minor diameter 30', Observed also in Buenos-Aires 
and Santiago. 
Last observations by Maclear (Cape Observatory) on 11 June.

1853 III (1853c). 
Discovered by Klinkerfues (Gottingen) in the night be- 
tween 10 June and 11 June; a telescopic object with a 3 to 4' tail. 
Moved 
from Ursa Major through Leo Minor; located in Leo in the end of August. 
Moved to the Southern Hemisphere in September; from 12 Sept. (located 
in Hydra) to 9 Jan. 1854 reported by southern observatories. 
During the 
first period, observed with 5-inch and 4-inch telescopes; 
quite faint (7 to 
8™?), 
21 June and 28 June, Oudemans (Leiden) estimated the nucleus at 
10 to 11™. 
17 June, Hornstein (Vienna) reported the tail to be 2 to 3!' long 
and up to 1'.5 wide at its end. He also reported that under high magnification 
the comet's head appeared to be a conglomerate of many bright nuclei; 
in 
the field of vision the comet appeared as an accumulation of bright parts 
rather than a continuous nebulous mass. 
In the end of July, a rapid increase 
in brightness. 
The position angles of the tail were measured in Leiden and 
Washington.

on 11 Aug. for the first time (5 to 6™), 
13 Aug., Reslhuber (Kremsmunster) 
reported the comet to be clearly visible in twilight on bright background;

1855 II (1855d). 
Discovered between Auriga and Gemini on 3 June by 
Donati (Florence) and independently on 4 July by Dien (Paris) and Klinkerfues 
(Gottingen). 
Donati described the comet as slightly fainter than the Hercules 
Nebula (NGC 6210; 9™.7 according to Vorontsov-Vel'yaminov). 
5 June, 
Bruhns (Berlin) reported a head 1' in diameter; equal in brightness to the 
Aquarius Nebula (apparently NGC 7009; 8™.4 according to Vorontsov- 
Vel'yaminov); no definite tail. 
Since the comet was observed in Hamburg, Gottingen, Paris, Florence, 
Padua, Rome, 
Berlin, Leiden and Liverpool, 
it must have been quite bright 
in the initial period (8 to 9™). Pape reported that by 14 June the comet had 
considerably decreased in brightness and thereafter the comet rapidly grew 
faint. 
Last measured in Cancer on 30 July by Lesser (Berlin) with a ring 
micrometer (11 to 12™). 
References: A.N., 41, 42; M.N., 
15, 16; A.J., 4; Berlin Beob. 
5: 202): 
Orbit: Van Biesbroeck.—A.J., 
29: 113. Discoveredandobserved after the 
perihelion passage; 
the assumed identity with 1362 not confirmed. 
From given estimates Hij=11™.3; 
5 July D, =0'.6. 
(r, 4): 3 June —(0.59, 0.62); 
15 June —(0.68, 1.07); 
30 June —(0.89, 1.59). 
1855 Ill (1855e). 
The fifteenth recorded apparition 
of the Encke- 
Backlund Comet. 
Observed in the southern sky in evenings. 
Discovered 
by Maclear (Cape of Good Hope) on 12 July after the perihelion passage and 
observed by Mann (Cape of Good Hope) till 16 Aug. Moved from Hydra 
to 
Sextans, and then to Crater, Curvus and near y Hydrae. 
13 July, appeared 
as a faint, almost circular nebulosity; D=1'.5 when 8 to 10° above horizon. 
1. 
16 July, clearly visible in bright twilight (micrometer reticle noticeable), 
while the comparison stars emerged much later (6 to 7™). Thereafter the 
comet rapidly decreased in brightness and observation became difficult. 
31 July, after full moon (29 July), comet more easily visible, though still 
considerably fainter than during the first period. 
2 Aug., perigee (A=0.74). 
Since the comet's envelope nearly disappeared during occultation of the star 
BD - 12°3481 (9™.3) on 1 Aug., itsintrinsic brightness could not have been 
less than 8 to 9™.

1855 IV (1855f). 
Discovered in Sextans by Bruhns (Berlin) in the morning 
of 12 Nov. 
A diffuse faint nebulosity moving rapidly west almost along the 
+2, +3° parallel. 
At the peginning of December, located in Monoceros; 
5 Dec. and6 Dec. passed through Orion; 
16 Dec., moved to PiscesseaatL Dec. 
discovered independently by Mitchell (Nantucket, U.S. A.)s 
Observed at Berlin, Hamburg, Leiden, Leipzig, Rome, Padua, 
Kremsmunster and other observatories. 
Schmidt (Olmutz) described the 
comet in November as a large bright nebulosity without any starlike nucleus 
or tail. 
Increased in brightness at the beginning of December; from 2 Dec. 
to 10 Dec., visible to the naked eye (6™). At perigee (6-7 Dec.), D,=15'. 
Clearly transparent, 
since the occulted stars did not diminish in brightness. 
D'Arrest (Leipzig) measured the apparent diameter of the central conden- 
sation (220 to 250" in the beginning of December). 
7 Dec., Reslhuber 
(Kremsminster) observed the comet on a meridian circle (not fainter than 
7™), 
30 Dec., visible only with difficulty; grew very faint near a 7 to 8™ 
star (9 to 10™). Beginning 10 Dec., Riimker (Hamburg) and Pape (Berlin) 
saw a faint starlike nucleus. 
Last measured on 3 Jan. 1856 by d'Arrest 
(Leipzig) and Winnecke (Berlin); very faint (10 to 11™).

1857.1 (1857a). 
Discovered near Pegasus on 22 Feb. by d'Arrest (Leipzig). 
26 March, independently discovered by van Arsdal (U.S.A.). 
Quite bright, 
11/2' in diameter (7 to 8™). 
In March, moved through Pegasus and 
Andromeda; passed through Perseus and Auriga in April; located in Orion 
at the beginning of May. 
Observed at almost all European observatories. 
2 March and 3 March, 
Winnecke (Bonn) reported a very bright comet with a nucleus of D=3'; no 
tail noticeable, though the coma extended slightly toward the Sun (6 to 7™). 
13 March and 18 March, 
Rumker (Hamburg) saw a clear nucleus which 
was not visible on 16 March. 
17 March, Fearnly (Christiania) estimated 
the comet's brightness at 7 to 8™ (central condensation?). 
D'Arrest des- 
cribed the comet as close in size and brightness to 1857 II. Schmidt (Olmutz) 
measured the head radius toward the Sun: 
17 March, 
2'.0; 18 April, 2'.64; 
17 March, Schmidt reported a bright comet with no nucleus or tail (6 to 7™). 
26 March, 
a faint trace of a tail 4 to 5' long; tail visible on 1 April and 
3 April. 
The tail was still visible on 11 April, when the comet was brighter 
than 1857 II (5 to 6™), 
which Schmidt repeatedly saw with the naked eye.

even when the comet was near the horizon.

decreased; comet considerably fainter than 1857 II. Last measured by 
Donati (Florence) and Plantamour (Geneva) on 2 May. Bruhns (Berlin) 
observed the comet with a Nicol prism and could detect no traces of polari-

1857 Il (1857b). 
The second recorded observation of the short-period 
Brorsen Comet (1846 III). 
First reported by Bruhns (Berlin) on 18 March 
as anew comet, 
since Van Galen (A.N., 
39: 22, 
et. seq.) had calculated 
that the comet would appear 3 months later in a different part of the sky. 
Located between Aries and Pisces; moved through Aries (March), Perseus 
and Auriga (April) and Camelopardus. 
Large and bright upon discovery; comparable with 1857 1(5 to 6™). 
According to Bruhns, brightness rapidly increased. 
A very dense conden- 
sation 1' in diameter; 
a 21/2' head; traces of a tail. 
Maximum brightness 
in the middle of April; grew considerably fainter in May, though observed 
till 22 June. 
Bruhns, using a Nicol prism, could detect no signs of polari- 
zation. 
1 April to 29 May, Schmidt (Olmiutz) measured the magnitude and 
the radius of the comet's head. 
1 April, in spite of low position and moon- 
light, the comet was equal in brightness to 1857 1(6 to 7™), though smaller 
in size; white color, round shape, no tail. 
Schmidt repeatedly observed 
the comet with the naked eye from 8 April to 12 April and on 20 April (5 to 
6™). 
11 April, a thin tail 11' long; a tail was visible on 14 April and 
20 April, but not 17, 18 and 20 April. 
1 May to 3 May, comet pale and 
blurred; beginning 9 May observed with difficulty. 
A periodic increase in 
brightness after 4 to 5 days. 
Same periodic variations observed by Schmidt 
in the size of the head. 
Radius of the head (measured from the central con- 
densation to the apex) varied from 1'.5 (11 April—1.5 (1 May) to 21.3 
(18 April) —2'.0 (22 May). 
In April, d'Arrest (Leipzig) could detect no trace of the tail. 
19 April, 
a 7 to 8™ central condensation (a later estimate, 
6 to 7™), but no intense 
condensation in May. 
21 May, a round, dull comet appearing as a nebulous 
disk of uniform brightness; D=170"; 22 May, D=140"'. 
Through the Leipzig 
instrument the comet appeared slightly brighter than Herschel's nebula 
h 908 (NGC 3718; 
107.5 according to Holetschek). 
18 May, Reslhuber 
(Kremsmunster) saw the comet near a 9™ star (not fainter than 9™); 22 May, 
the comet disappeared near a bright star. 
23 May, vaguely sensed near 
fp 
Ursae Majoris (10 to 11™). 
18 April, Forster (Berlin) saw a trace of a 
tail in position angle 250°, 
Donati (Florence) observed the comet's 
occultation of a 12™ star refractor aperture 28 cm, focal distance 5.2 m, 
x 250 magnification); the star grew gradually fainter, fully disappearing for 
30 sec, when occulted by the central condensation. 
Donati attributed this 
to the dullness of the condensation, 
since the star invariably remained 
brighter than the comet. 
In June, according to Bruhns and Forster (Berlin) 
the comet was very faint and extremely difficult to observe (10-11™).

1857 IV (1857d). Discovered by Peters (Albany, U.S.A.) on 25 July as 
a very faint diffuse nebulosity; no condensation. 
Discovered independently 
by Dien (Paris) on 28 July and Habitch (Gotha) on 30 July. Located in 
Camelopardus, moved south through Auriga and Gemini. 
Since the comet was observed at Berlin, Florence, Toulouse, Vienna, 
Leipzig, Bonn and other places, 
it must have been quite bright. 
In Altona 
the comet was not observed with small instruments. 
31 July, Secchi (Rome) 
estimated the diameter D=3'; an irregular nebula with an eccentric con- 
densation. 
2 Aug., oval shaped; the dense part elongated in the direction 
P=117°. 
Position determined on a 101/2-inch refractor with illuminated

1857 V (1857e). 
Discovered by Klinkerfues (Gottingen) in Camelopardus 
on 20 Aug. 
Passed near the pole moving south along the meridian 13545™; 
passed through Canes Venatici till 10 Sept.; 
in Bootesand Virgo at the 
beginning of October.

increase controverted the conventional formula.

the central condensation.

facing away from the Sun.

8 to 10' wide.

similar to that of 1854 III, remained 
constant throughout the observation period; brighter, however, than 1854 III.

still increasing.

nucleus.

a 3 to 4' envelope; 
a 2°5 tail.

head and tail even brighter.

1858 1 (1858a). 
The second recorded apparition 
of the periodic Tuttle's 
Comet. 
Discoveréd by Tuttle at the Harvard Observatory (Cambridge, 
U.S.A.) in Andromeda on 4 Jan. 
Since independently discovered by Bruhns 
(Berlin) on 11 Jan., was not fainter than 8™. 
Moved through Pisces and in 
the second half of February through Cetus; 
in March located in Eridanus. 
Observed at Bonn, Berlin, Cambridge (U.S.A.), Altona, Vienna, 
Liverpool, Konigsberg, Kremsmunster, Olmitz, Florence, 
and Copenhagen.

though exceptionally faint in bright moonlight (7 to 8™).

a. 
central condensation;

eter.

(full 
moon), visible on brightly illuminated field of vision (6 to 7™). Beginning 
20 March, the comet was invisible in twilight due to its low celestial position 
(8 to 9"), though on 10 March and 18 March it had been faintly visible under 
the same conditions. 
Schmidt (Olmutz) described the comet as clearly visible at the beginning of 
March; "granular" light.

comet in moonlight and bright aurora borealis; he too reported the granular 
structure of the disk.

21/2' long.

After the calculation of the first orbit, Tuttle and Pape positedthe identity 
of the comet with 1790 II; Bruhns assumed identity with 1785 I. Later, Pape 
and Bruhns gave a 13.7 year period; the comet having completed five revolu- 
tions since 1790. 
The catalogue gives Rahts' elements (A.N., 113: 194). 
Proximity to the Sun explains the omission of the 1803, 
1817 and 1844 ap- 
paritions, but in 1830 the comet should have been visible near dawn yet 
somehow was not observed.

1860 I (1860a). An exceptional case of a double comet. 
Discovered by 
Liais (Olinda, Brazil) near u Doradus on 26 Feb.; moved towards x Doradus. 
A double comet with the two components removed by 27" ina and 68" in 8.

1860 III (1860c). A bright comet. 
First discovered in Italy with the naked 
eye. 
18 June, measured by Camarri (Senigallia). 
19, 20 and 21 June de- 
tected in Athens on the north-western horizon, 
in Smyrna, and at Harvard 
Observatory (U.S.A.). 
Moved from Auriga to Lynx and then to Leo in the 
first half of July. 
At the end of July in Crater. 
As reported at Harvard 
Observatory, 
the comet at first hada 6 to 7° tail. 
24 June, Schmidt (Athens) 
estimated the reddish head at 3™; the straight thin tail appeared 7 to 10° 
long in twilight, and up to 20° long at night, remaining visible for nearly an 
hour after the nucleus had set. 
The comet sharply divided into a bright 
nucleus and a two-jet tail. 
27 June, a 1”.2 head. 
28 June, a 15° tail. 5 July 
and 6 July, a 9° tail; the head 2 to 3™ bright; the nucleus 4™.5. 
7 July, 
noticeable drop in brightness which continued to 11July when m= 3™ anda 
tail 1° long. 
Schmidt assigned the relative maxima of brightness and tail 
length to 27 June, 6 July, 
18 July and 23 July. 
18 July, comet still visible 
to the naked eye (4 to 5™). 
23 July, inspite of the low position, visible 
through a small 1-inch comet-seeker (6 to 7™).

28 June, Secchi (Rome) estimated nucleus brightness at 2™ and stated 
that the nucleus alternately occupied eccentric and concentric positions with 
respect to the cometary nebula, thus indicating a possible rotation of the 
nucleus. 
5 July, Liais (Rio de Janeiro) saw a 15° tail and estimated the 
nucleus at 2™,. Observers in Vienna described the gap between the two tail 
branches as absolutely dark and extending over a distance equal to that be- 
tweena and f Aurigae. 
This gap seemed to be a corpuscular shadow rather 
than a result of differences in the optical thickness of the tail conoid. 
Schmidt (Athens) measured the motion of the luminous matter in the comet's 
head.

discovered and observed in Rio de Janeiro and in Australia (Sydney, N.S.W.).

in brightness.

1861 II (1861b). 
A bright comet. 
Discovered by Tebbutt (Windsor, 
Australia) on 13 May. Visible to the naked eye (4 to 5™) while increasing 
in brightness. 
4 June, Ellery described the tail as 4° long and increasing 
in brightness. 
Beginning 27 May, observed in Sydney; independently at the 
Cape of Good Hope beginning 2 June. 4June, observed by Capelletti; 10 June, 
by Moesta (Santiago); 
11 June, by Liais (Brazil). 
11 June, comet located 
in Eridanus; 
Liais described the nucleus as 2 to 3™ bright, 
the tail over 
40° long. Moved almost directly north. 
At the end of June, seen throughout Europe in Auriga as a bright luminary 
with a long tail; moved through Lynx and Ursa Major. 
Galle described the 
nucleus as extremely bright; according to Secchi equal to Saturn in magnitude 
(0™.0). 
Schmidt described the head as yellow-reddish near the horizon. 
According to Brorsen and Secchi, the tail was 90 to 120° long, maximum 
width 6°; parabolic envelope. Williams reported 40 to 50° jets stretching to 
Cassiopeia and Ursa Major, 
in addition to the main tail extending all the 
way to Polaris. 
: 
29 June and 30 June, the Earth passed through the comet's tail at 2/3 of 
its length from the nucleus. 
1 July and 2 July, Secchi described the tail as 
118° long. Brightness exceeded that of Donati's Comet. 
Schmidt (Athens) 
easily saw the comet's nucleus in sunlight. 
30 June to 1 July, Schweizer 
(Moscow) reported a radical change in the structure of the head and in the 
features of the jet sector. 
Schmidt observed the motion of the fine details 
in the head, establishing a velocity of 252 toise* per second away from the 
Sun and 167 toise per second towards the Sun. 
5 July, Schmidt estimated 
* Toise, an old French measure roughly equal to 2 meters, 

===PAGE_0029 (book p.211)===
80 to 90° tail length. 
Head exceeded Polaris in brightness, though much 
fainter than Saturn. 
2 July, Littrow gave 1™ for nucleus brightness, 
97° 
for the main tail length; the second tail was straight. 
The main tail grew 
gradually shorter; 
8 July, only 31° long (57° according to Schmidt); the 
wide tail was faint.

1862 II (1862a). 
Discovered by Schmidt (Athens) on 2 July and a few hours 
later by Tempel (Marseilles); located near B Cassiopeiae, an excellent 4 to 
s™ object. 
3 July, discovered independently by Bond (Cambridge, U.S.A.) 
and Simons (Albany). 
Moved quite rapidly covering a distance of over 20° 
in one day between 2 July and 3 July; passed through Cepheus, Draco and 
Ursa Minor within 24 hours; at the end of July, located in Virgo. 
The most thorough observations made by Schmidt (Athens). 
2 July, he 
saw the comet with the naked eye, 22!' in diameter, half as bright as the 
Andromeda Nebula (5™,5); 
3 July, 4 July, 5 July, attained the maximum 
apparent size (up to 34'), equal in brightness to the Andromeda Nebula 
(47.5).

1862 III (1862b). 
First observed by L. Swift (Marathon, U.S.A.) 
in 
Camelopardus on 
15 July. 
His report was published after the comet 
had been discovered by Tuttle (Cambridge, U.S.A.) and Simons (Albany) on 
18 July. 
Discovered independently in Europe by Pocinotho (Florence) on 
22 July, Rosa (Rome) 25 July, Skjellerup (Copenhagen) 27 July. 
Moved north passing several degrees from the pole inthe middle of August. 
31 Aug., located in Corona Borealis, having moved to Scorpio by 16 Sept. 
Upon discovery in the U.S.A., the comet was a telescopic object; on 
24 July, Schiaparelli saw the comet with the naked eye, though with some 
difficulty, estimating the brightness of the head at 6™. A straight thin tail 
up to 11/2° long. 
Tuttle saw the comet with the naked eye beginning 25 July, 
describing it as equal in brightness to the Hercules cluster. 
25 July to 
27 July, Secchi (Rome) reported the nebula to be 3 to 4! in diameter with a 
point nucleus; some observers detected the comet with the naked eye (5 to 
6™). 
27 July, Secchi described the comet as a bright object witha 7™ nucleus. 
1 Aug., in Vienna visible to the naked eye; the nucleus appeared 6™ bright 
in a telescope. 
31 July and 2 Aug., Bruhns (Leipzig) saw the comet with 
the naked eye; traces of a 1° tail. 
2 Aug., Pocinotho and Toussaint 
(Florence) reported the comet to resemble a 4™ star. 
7 Aug., Schiaparelli 
estimated the head brightness at 4™; through binoculars, 
the tail was 5 to 
6° long. 
A distinguishing feature of the comet was the faint anomalous tail 30' long 
directed towards the Sun, as observed by Schiaparelli from 30 July to 6 Aug. 
In the beginning of August, various rapidly shifting ray-like jets were 
observed in the comet's head. Observed by d'Arrest (Copenhagen), Secchi 
(Rome), Romberg (London), Schwabe (Dessau), and mainly by Winnecke 
(Pulkovo) and Schmidt (Athens). 
Schmidt reported variations of up to 100° 
in the position angle of the jets and variations 
in the brightness of the nucleus 
from 11-12™ to 7-6™ within 3 days. 
Beginning 16 Aug., Schmidt measured 
the length of the straight tail; 27 Aug., maximum length of 25°. 
By that 
time (the perigee, A=0.35) the radius of the coma increased to 17'. Schmidt 
established periodic fluctuations in brightness, 
in the position of the jet 
sector and in the nature of the tail and jets with a period of 2.7 to 2.9 days. 
Extensive 
descriptions 
of the 
comet 
were 
given by Reslhuber 
(Kremsmunster), Bruhns (Leipzig), Tietjen (Berlin) and mainly by Winnecke 
(Pulkovo), who thoroughly measured the position of the tail, individual jets 
and rays. 
The position of the mid-line of the jet sector with respect to the 
Sun varied from 50 to 60°; the position of the tail deviated from the direction 
215 

===PAGE_0034 (book p.216)===
of the radius-vector within +5 to - 8°. According to Winnecke's observations 
(Pulkovo) the comet appeared to the naked eye at the end of August as 2732 
bright. 
7 Aug., brighter than 24 Camelopardalis. 
15 Aug., 
brighter than 
Draconis and slightly fainter than5 Ursae Majoris. 
21 Aug., slightly 
brighter than y Ursae Minoris. 
Tuttle assigned the maximum brightness 
to 2 Sept.; on that day the tail was tracked over 30°. 
17 Sept., 
still visible 
to the naked eye at 6° from Antares. 
Murmann (Vienna) observed the comet 
withaNicol prism. 
26 Aug. to 28 Aug., he reported considerable variation 
in the intensity of the tail when the prism was rotated, 
i.e., considerable 
polarization of the comet's light in the plane of the tail. 
In the southern 
celestial hemisphere the comet was observed by Tebbutt (Windsor, Australia) 
till 14 Oct. and Mann (Cape of Good Hope) till 27 Oct. 
The comet was of 
particular interest because of its relation to the Perseids meteor shower. 
References: A.N., 58-61; M.N., 22, 23; Mem. R.A.S., 32; Winnecke, 
Mém. Acad. Petersburg 7; Dorpat Beob., 
15:2; Ann. Mosc. Obs. Sly

According to F.A. Bredikhin (Ann. Mosc. Obs., 3(1) p. 17. 1877) the 
comet had a type I straight tail and a type III tail. 
The anomalous tail might 
have been produced by the explosion of 11-12 May 1862 at maximum initial 
velocity 1.2 km/sec (Vsekhsvyatskii, 
A. Zh. 9 (3-4) p. 166. 1932). According 
to G.A. Shain (Izv.

6(1). March,

in the Perseides shower amount to 4.10!" g. Kleiber and B.A. Vorontsov-

1863 II (1863a). 
Discovered in Aquarius by Klinkerfues (Gottingen) on 
11 April; moved north. 
14 April, discovered independently by Donati 
(Florence), in Delphinus. Bright upon discovery, traces of a tail (5 to 6™). 
Donati gave 1° for the tail length. 
15 April and 16 April, Galle observed 
the comet as a bright, poorly shaped nebula of granular structure with 
traces of a tail. InMay, moved through Draco and Ursa Minor, passing 
10° from the pole. Observed in Ursa Major from the second half of June. 
Measured in Vienna, Leipzig, London, Breslau, Cracow, Florence and 
Leiden. 
In May, measured in Lubeck, Vienna and Leiden on meridian 
circles, thus not fainter than 6 to 7™. Observed through refractors at 
Washington, Uppsala, Padua, Modena, Kremsmunster and other places. 
16 Aug. to 19 Aug., d'Arrest (Copenhagen) described the comet as a circular, 
second class nebula 30" in diameter, 
slightly brighter in the center, with an 
11to12™ nucleus. In September and on 1 Oct., d'Arrest described the comet 
as so well visible that had it not been for the approaching full moon it could 
have been seen for an additional 2 or 3 weeks (8 to 10™?). 
2 Sept. to 
14 Sept., observedby Schultz (Uppsala) as a nebula of class 2 or 3 (9 to 10™).

1863 V (1863e). 
(Provisional designation 1863 IV). 
Discovered by 
Respighi (Bologna) on 28 Dec. 1863 between Lyra and Hercules. 
Discovered 
independently by Backer (Nauen) on 1 Jan. 
1864. 
As reported by Bruhns 
(Leipzig), the comet was an almost bright circular nebula with a small tail 
and resembled a 6 to 7™star. 
9 Jan., Karlinski (Cracow) discovered the 
comet independently; nucleus 8™, coma 3 to 4! in diameter, 
tail 30' long 
(intrinsic brightness notless than 6™). Moved through Lyra and Cygnus; 
29 Jan., located in Cassiopeia, and at the end of January and the beginning 
of February rapidly traversed Perseus and Taurus; passed through Orion 
from 10 Feb. 
Encounter with the Earth, 
31 Jan., A = 0.182. 
10Jan. and 
11 Jan., Bruhns and Romberg reported a 30! tail and a sharp nucleus. 
Watson (Ann Arbor, U.S.A.), who discovered the comet independently 
on 9 Jan. and observed it on 10, 11 and 12 Jan., described it as a large, 
bright object with 30! tail and a nucleus strongly condensed towards the 
center. 
Stampfer, 
in his publication of the comet's ephemeris, 
stated that 
the comet should have been visible to the naked eye at the beginning of 
February. 
15 Jan., Bruhns saw the tail in moonlight. 
30 Jan., elongated nucleus; 
a jet sector visible in the direction of the tail. 
1 Feb., 
1°.5 tail visible 
through a seeker, 
sharp nucleus, ajet. 
Bruhns and Kruger (Helsingfors) 
measured the position angle of the comet's tail. 11 Feb., Karlinski (Cracow) 
described the comet as very faint (10 to 9™?). 
Position measured at several 
observatories: 
in Kremsmunster to 14 Feb., in Leyton (London) to 1 March. 
4 March, Schmidt (Athens) could not detect the comet (9 to 10™). 
References: A.N., 61-63, 65; M.N., 24-26; Leyton Obs. 
76. 
Orbit: 
Valentiner. —Dissertation, 
Berlin, 
1869.

H,)=7™. From these estimates we obtain Hjj)=8™.2.

1863 VI (1863c). 
Discovered in Leo by Backer (Nauen) on 9 Oct. and 
independently by Tempel (Marseilles) on 13 Oct. as a diffuse nebula with 
a slightly eccentric nucleus (7 to 8™). 
Passed through Canes Venatici; located in Bootes at the end of November, 
in Hercules in December, 
in Lyra in January; February, March and April, 
passed through Vulpecula, Sagitta and Aquila. It was remarkable that at the 
end of December and the beginning of January the comet passed only 4° 
from 1863 IV and both comets could be observed in the same field of a comet- 
seeker; 
the distance then between them was 0.14 a.u. 
According to Schultz (Uppsala), 
in November the comet's nucleus was 
quite large appearing as an 11™ and 10 to 11™ object; in December, nucleus 
9 to 10™ but not brighter than 9™ (7 to 8™?). Thenebula was 2 to 3' indiameter; 
almost central condensation. 
Schmidt (Athens) first located the comet on 
6 Nov. 
Easily visible through a seeker: D=5! (7 to 8™). 
13 Nov., refractors 
showed an 8! tail and an 11 to 12™ nucleus; coma radius 1'.53. 
During a 
long period, 
a 30! tail was easily noticed. 17 Nov., nucleus 11™; 
the

1864 II (1864a). 
Discovered by Tempel (Marseilles) on 4 July and by 
Respighi (Bologna) on 5 July. On the night of 12 July, spotted independently 
by Kowalczyk (Cracow); in Aries moving to the north-east. 
At the beginning 
of August, passed through Auriga, Gemini, Cancer and Leo; 
12 Aug., 
located in Virgo, descending south; 
at the end of September, 
in Libra. 
Upon discovery, 
a moderately bright object, faint in bright twilight; 
8 July, Schiaparelli described the comet as 
a circular object with ill- 
defined nucleus 
8 to 9™(6 to 7”); D=3 to 4', no trace ofa tail. 
Bright- 
ness rapidly increased. 
27 July, 
Rumker (Hamburg) described the comet 
as unexpectedly bright in spite of the moonlight; D=5 to 6'. 
28 July, 
Engelman (Leipzig) saw a bright comet; eccentric condensation, nebula 
elongated away from the Sun. 
30 July, circular nebula 5' in diameter, 
eccentric nucleus. 
31 July, somewhatbrighter; 
D=6'; visible to the naked 
eye and through opera glasses (5™); no tail. 
3 Aug., blurred nucleus and 
a 10' envelope; eccentric nucleus; 
no tail. 
4 Aug., large diffuse nebulous 
mass; through seeker a narrow 3° tail visible extending away from the Sun. 
5 Aug., tail brighter; envelope occupied entire field of vision (15'); nucleus 
appeared as a sharp condensation; jet directed away from the Sun. 
15 Aug., 
nebulous mass; D~3'; 
nucleus faint and blurred, yet easily observed (10™). 
8 Aug., encounter with the Earth (A=0.098). 
6 Aug., Schmidt (Athens) 
estimated the brightness at 2 to 3”, head 32' in diameter. 
Extremely faint,

nebula up to 1° in diameter; 
tail invisible to the naked eye, 
yet low-powered telescopes showed a 40! tail.

of a tail.

Qnaife and observed by Tebbutt (Windsor, Australia) with a 3 1/4-inch

Comet 1864 II was the first comet to be observed spectroscopically.

dark gaps dividing the comet's spectrum, which consisted of three bright

1864 III (1864b). 
Discovered by Donati and Toussaint (Florence) on 
23 July in Coma Berenices as a very faint object 2' in diameter. 
Moved 
south through Virgo in August, Corvus in September, Hydra and Centaurus 
in October. 
In November, passed near Crux andatthe beginning of December 
passed 8°5 from the south pole. 
In February and March, located in Cetus, 
moving north. 
30 July, Schiaparelli (Milan) reported observations difficult 
due to comet's faintness (9 to 10™). 
6 Aug., according to Oppolzer (Vienna) 
the comet was not exceedingly faint: equal to a lst class nebula of moderate 
brightness; 
a clear central condensation (8 to 9™?). 
3 Aug., Donati reported 
a 15! tail and a nucleus. 
Starlike nucleus reported also by Engelman (Leipzig). 
13 Aug., exceptionally faint due to low position (5° altitude). 
Visible in 
Leipzig on 15 Aug., though with considerable effort and too faintly for 
measurements (considering its altitude, the comet was not fainter than 9™). 
Moesta (Santiago) spotted the comet on 30 Aug. through a telescope saw 
a 20! tail (7 to 8™?). 
8 Sept., 
tail not visible. 
Measured by Moesta from 
2 Nov. to 23 Dec. 
19 Jan., spotted again by Schmidt (Athens); very faint apparition yet 
well visible with a tail and an 11” nucleus (9™). 
Radius of the head from 
the nucleus to the apex 33"; tail 7' long. 
21 Jan. and 22 Jan., head radius 
~ 60". By 27 Jan., the nucleus had dropped to 12™, the tail still 5'.5 long. 
Engelman saw the comet on 13 Feb. when too faint for measurements (10 to 
11™?). 
Last measurement on 24 Feb. by Peters (Clinton).

1865 I (1865a). 
Bright southern comet. 
Spotted with the naked eye by 
various persons in the evening twilight of 17 Jan. and 18 Jan. eye Abbot 
(Hobart Town), Moesta (Santiago), Ellery (Melbourne) and also observers 
at the Cape. 
Located in Indus, moving to Grus; 
in February, located in 
Phoenix; 
in the middle of March, 
in Eridanus. 
According to Moesta, the 
comet had an almost straight conical tail. 
20 Jan. , tail brighter than previ- 
ously, sharply bounded at the southern edge, diffuse at the northern edge

The main tail at least 25° long, 
about 10° wide near the head and 1°.5 wide at the end. 
Jets extended from 
the nucleus; head surrounded by an envelope, 
22 Jan. and 23 Jan, tail 
curving perceptibly; 
24 Jan., nucleus sharper than before. 
Moesta gave 
drawings of the head and the tail from 20 Jan. 
to 30 Jan. A.N., 
64: 109.

first quarter.

1866 I (1865f). 
Discovered by Tempel (Marseilles) in the evening of

1865 near B Ursae Minoris. 
5 Jan. 
1866, discovered independently 
by Tuttle (Harvard, U. S,A.). Observed in Europe. 
Passed rapidly near the 
pole, descending south and traversing more than 11° per day on 20 Dec. 
to 
24 Dec. 
Passed through Cepheus and Lacerta; 
at the end of December, 
located in Pegasus. 
In January, passed through Pisces; inFebruary, located 
in Aquarius.

condensation, 
diameter D=12',

D=3'.

1866, very bright, circular, 
small

and nucleus 12™.

1867 1 (1867a). 
Discovered by Coggia (Marseilles) on 22 Jan. and meas- 
ured first by Stephan on 25 Jan. 
Discovered independently by Tempel 
(Marseilles) on 28 Jan. 
LocatedinAries, moving to the north-east. 
At the 
end of February and in March, passed through Taurus and at the end of 
March was located in Auriga. 
Upon discovery, Valz described the comet 
as a glittering circular object with distinct nucleus and indefinite, 
fanlike 
tail (7™). 
28 Jan., however, Tempel reported the comet to be very faint; 
D=3'; 
near an 8™ star, the comet completely disappeared for several 
minutes (9™). 
4 Feb., Vogel (Leipzig) also described the comet as very 
faint. 
7 Feb., Oppolzer, observing the comet through a 7-inch refractor, 
described it as quite faint though easily visible (9 to 10™); 6 Feb., Vogel 
saw a faint comet with an extension at the position angle 75°; small, well- 
shaped nucleus. 
9 Feb., quite bright and easily visible in spite of moonlight 
(8 to 9™); D=1'.5. 
21 Feb., exceptionally faint. 
23 Feb. 
to 8 March,

The first recorded apparition 
of the short-period Comet

Moved 
very slowly to the south-east;

faint and diffuse in appearance,

1868 I (1868a). 
The third recorded apparition 
of the short-period Comet 
Brorsen (1846 II, 1857 II). Spotted according to Bruhns' ephemeris by 
Tempel (Marseilles) on 22 March; first measured by Schmidt (Athens) on 
11 April. 
Very bright object; Schmidt estimated the central condensation 
at 9 to 8™. 
By the end of April, moved from Cetus into Taurus; 
in May, 
passed through Auriga and Lynx; 
in June, located in Ursa Major. 
11 April, 
Schmidt reported a 5' tail and an ~2' coma.

condensation 8 to 7™; head 2' in diameter.

length of 40'.

At the end of May, 9™ 
central condensation; 
7 June, 
12™. 
At the end of May and the end of July 
no tail was visible. 
Schmidt established a 23-day period of variation in the 
size of the coma, 
similar to that observed in Comet 1851, linking this 
phenomenon with the rotation period of the Sun. 
2 May and 13 May, d'Arrest 
(Copenhagen) estimated the central condensation at 8 to 9™, reporting the 
comet to be fainter than in 1846 when the central condensation was 6 to 7™. 
17 May, comet appeared as a 4th class nebulous star when occulting a 7™ 
star. 
At the end of May, much fainter. 
Vogel (Leipzig) estimated the diame- 
ter of the head; 
14 May, he reported granular structure of the central con- 
densation, determining isolated bright regions in the southern (the front) 
part of the comet. 
29 May, faint due to low position, D=5'. 
Last observa- 
tion by Schmidt on 23 June (10 to 11™). 
10 July, no traces of comet detected 
(m>12™).

1869 I (1869a). 
The third recorded apparition of Comet Pons-Winnecke. 
Spotted in Leo Minor near the ephemeris position by Winnecke (Karlsruhe) 
on 9 April. 
Moved slowly east; inJune, located in Gemini. 
After conjunction 
with the Sun, observed from 4 Aug. to 12 Oct. in Eridanus in the morning 
sky. Upon discovery, Winnecke gave a diameter of 6 to 8', the comet being 
perceptibly brighter at the center; 
point nucleus visible through a 41/2-inch 
telescope (8 to 9™). 
13 April, noticeably brighter in appearance. 
D,=8'.

meter from 11 April to 15 April.

criptions of the comet in May and June 1869.

condensation at 12 to 11™ in the middle of May, at 11™ on 10 May, at 10™ at 
the beginning of May, at 9™ on 13 July.

at P= 338°; 
14 May, tail clearly visible. 
At the end of May and the beginning 
of June, the tail was reported also by Weiss. 
In May, Schonfeld (Mannheim) 
observed the multiple structure of the nucleus. 
The nonhomogeneity of the 
central condensation was observed also by Moller and Duner (Lund) at the 
end of April, when the nucleus consisted of 2 to 5 bright points. 
4 Aug., 
Winnecke reported that the comet was brighter and larger than it had been 
in May at the same distance from the perihelion; D>7'. 15 Aug., Rumker

bright halo and a magnificent, 
eccentric, 
starlike nucleus.

comet exceptionally faint, appearing as pale and diffuse nebula with scintil- 
lating starlike nuclei.

1871 1(1871a). 
Discovered by Winnecke (Karlsruhe) near x and h Persei. 
Discovered independently by Borrelly (Marseilles) on the night of 13 April 
and by L. Swift (Marathon, U.S.A.) on 15 April. 
Moved south through Perseus 
approaching Taurus in May. Upon discovery, small pale comet; 
10 April, 
Vogel (Bothkamp) describedit as a bright object with a nucleus and a 4! tail. 
11 April, tail reported by Winnecke (7 to 8™), who described the comet as 
bright. 
The same day, Vogel observed the comet's spectrum (7™). 
Position 
determined at various European observatories and in America; 
a bright 
nucleus.

several degrees in diameter; 
26 April and 27 April, a clear 8™ nucleus 
visible in moonlight (6 to 7™).

at 11™.

cleus 8™, tail invisible, D~2'; observed at an altitude of 4 to 5° (6™). 
In Hamburg and Lund, the position angle of the tail was measured begin- 
ning 8 April (8 to 11°); in May, 12 to 30°. 
In the middle of May, at an alti- 
tude of 3 to 4°, the comet was visible, though the comparison stars were 
very faint (5 to 6™). Tempel (Milan), observing the comet from 16 April to 
8 May, reported that after the Moon had set the comet appeared much 
brighter (6 to 7™ in spite of low altitude). 
After the perihelion passage, 
observed only at Cape of Good Hope on 5 Aug. in Vela.

of the short-period 
Comet Tuttle.

(Marseilles) on 12 Oct. in Ursa Major (a =9'9™; §8=+44°4).

dependently by Tuttle (U.S.A.) on 22 Oct.

comet as sufficiently bright; 2 to 3’ in diameter, with a point nucleus; 
scintillated slightly in occultation of a 9™.3 star (8 to 9™). 
Rumker and

visible.

at the beginning of November, located in Leo, at the

beginning of December;

well condensed (8 to 9™).

through a seeker (8 to 9™); substantial condensation; 
a 12™ nucleus.

1871 V (1871c). 
The twentieth recorded apparition 
of Comet Encke- 
Backlund. Spottedaccording to Glazenap's ephemeris by Stephan (Marseilles) 
on 18 Sept. and by Winnecke (Karlsruhe) on 19 Sept. as a very faint extended 
nebulous mass in Triangulum (11 to 10™). 
Schmidt (Athens) saw the comet 
but without certainty on 13 Sept. Moved west through Andromeda in the

===PAGE_0053 (book p.235)===
second half of October; traversed Pegasus, Vulpecula and Aquila at the end

December, moving through Ophiuchus.

object (9 to 10™) rapidly increasing in brightness. 
19 Nov. 
to 30 Nov., 
Schnelz (Uppsala) observed fan-shaped jets in the head.

November, described by M. Khandrikov (Kiev) as a bright fan-shaped nebu- 
lous mass.

(6™); traces of a tailvisible telescopically.

3 Dec.,

tail 
2° long, directed away from the Sun.

1872 I. Discovered on 2 Dec. by Pogson (Madras) after having received 
Klinkerfues' (Gottingen) telegram of 30 Nov.: 'Biela touched Earth on27Nov.: 
search near Theta Centauri.’ Klinkerfues maintained that the comet which 
gave rise to the stellar shower on 27 Nov. 
1872 could be observed at a point 
diametrically opposite the radiant of the stellar shower (a = 26°; 6=+37°). 
30 Nov. and 1 Dec., overcast and rainy in Madras. 
2 Dec., 05:15 hours 
local time, comet immediately spotted when the sky cleared. 
Pogson des- 
cribed the comet as bright and circular with a nucleus but no tail; D=45". 
On the next morning, 
3 Dec., the position was determined with full relia- 
bility. 
Circular comet, 
75" in diameter, with a bright nucleus and a faint 
8' tail at 
P=280°. 
Observed through an 8-inch telescope, yet also well 
visible through a seeker (7™). Pogson described the comet as 3 to 4 times 
brighter than the cluster M80 (Holetschek gives for M80m =7to 8™); comet's 
brightness was thus 6™.3. 
The next days were cloudy, and the comet was 
no longer observed, having passed into the region of the morning glow.

80, 81; M.N.,

Bruhns objected to this for sundry reasons; he calculated the orbit, main- 
taining that Comet Klinkerfues- Pogson had nothing to do either with Comet 
Biela or with the stellar shower of 27 Nov., His first orbit is quite similar 
to that of Kreutz orbit (A. Jb., 
3: 190).

The second recorded apparition of the short- period

(Marseilles) on 3 April as an exceptionally faint object (Lintoat 2? iia 
Ophiuchus. Moved south; located in Scorpio in July.

|

diffuse 12™ star (10 to 11™?).

as an exceptionally pale and small nebula; 
D=1 
to 11/2'; 
a small central 
condensation. 
23 June, comet could not be spotted among 13™ stars. Tempel 
(Milan) saw the comet only on 1 June; he mapped the comet's positions up 
to 24 June. 
Last observations were made in Marseilles on 1 July (12 to 13™) 
and in Paris on 1 Aug. (12 to 13™).

1873 Il (1873b). 
The first recorded apparition 
of the short-period Comet 
Tempel (2). Discovered by Tempel in Cetuson3July. 
Slowly moved south- 
east, remaining in Cetus till October. 
Upon discovery Tempel described 
the comet as faint (9 to 10™). 
5 July, in good sky, appeared unexpectedly 
bright; D~5' (8 to 9™). 20 July and21 July, numerous nuclei were observed 
in the head. 
Borgen (Leipzig) described the comet as elongated with 
a nucleus 
and an eccentric condensation; D>2'. 
6 July to 25 July, Schulhof (Vienna) 
also saw three 
starlike condensations in the head; he estimated them at 
11™ each. 
Hence the nucleus must have been brighter than 10™, and the 
comet as a whole could not have been fainter than 8 to 9™. 
In August and 
September the comet decreased in brightness. 
25 Sept., the granular struc- 
ture was still observed in Lund. 
22 Sept., Weiss (Vienna) described the 
comet as a very faint nebula several minutes in diameter (9 to 10™). Ob- 
served at Ann Arbor, Athens, 
Bilk, Clinton, Naples, Hamburg, 
Kremsmunster, Leipzig, Lund, Milan, Marseilles, Padua, Strasbourg, 
Twickenham, Washington and Vienna. 
This list indicates the substantial 
brightness of the comet which was, apparently, higher than Holetschek's 
estimates (m=9 to 10™in July, 10™ in August and 10 to 11™ at the end of 
September). 
Last measured on 10 Oct. by Hind and Plummer (Twickenham).

1873 V (1873d). 
Discovered by Andries (Paris) on 23 Aug. as a bright 
telescopic object (6 to 7™) between Lynx and Camelopardus. 
Moved rapidly 
south (to conjunction with the Sun); located in Leo Minor on 12 Sept. 
27 Aug., Pechule (Hamburg) saw the bright comet with a starlike nucleus; 
nebula clearly not homogeneous. 
28 Aug., Schulhof (Vienna) described the 
comet as very bright, large and slightly more condensed from above; Tempel 
(Milan) saw no tail. 
29 Aug., Tempel reported a narrow tail 1° long; 
237 

===PAGE_0056 (book p.238)===
circular sharply bound head with a strong condensation; D~8 to 10'. 
Tail 
extended from a narrow protuberance rather than directly from the head; 
initial width not more than 1'. 
30 Aug., 
tail reported by Schulhof. 
1 Sept., 
he estimated the tail at 10', whereas Tempel on 3 Sept. saw a tail over 3° 
long extending from a narrow 2 to 3' protuberance of the external cometary 
envelope. 
Ata distance of 15' the tail was only 2' wide; further on, 
16' to 
20! wide.

sort of luminous wave apparently 
travelling in the tail in the wide diffuse part of which parallel luminous lines 
were seen.

an eccentric nucleus and a fan-shaped tail at least 20' long.

Engelman (Leipzig) estimated the nucleus at 7 to 8™; very bright granular 
nebula, D~2!' (4 to 5™). 
In spite of low altitude, visible to the naked eye.

culmination, 
3°.3 above the horizon (4™?).

with the Sun, observed in Virgo by Palisa (Pola) on 17 Nov. and 28 Nov.

Discovered in Hercules 
by Coggia (Marseilles) on 10 Nov. and by Winnecke (Strasbourg) on the

1874 I (1874a). 
Discovered by Winnecke (Strasbourg) in Vulpecula on 
the morning of 20 Feb. before dawn. 
Moved south-east; observed only for 
6 days.

9 March, should have been tens of 
times brighter than upon discovery. Winnecke failed to spot the comet at

1874 II (1874b). 
Discovered by Winnecke (Strasbourg) on 11 April near 
B Aquarii. 
Since the comet was discovered independently by Borrelly 
(Marseilles) on 15 April and by Tempel (Milan) on 18 April, its brightness 
must have been considerable. 
Moved north-west; located in Vulpecula at 
the beginning of May; in Bootes at the end of May and in Canes Venatici at 
the end of June. 
Upon discovery, Winnecke described it as a magnificent, 
bright comet, circular and highly condensed towards the center; 
D~ 4! (6 
to 7™). 
17 April, Bruhns (Leipzig) described the comet as equal in bright- 
ness to the nebula in Aquarius (NGC 7089; according to Holetschek, m=6™) 
but larger in size (5 to 6™). 
12 April, Weiss (Vienna) described the comet 
as a bright nebula with central condensation; had it not been for twilight 
and low altitude, 
the head would have appeared equal to that of Comet Donati. 
The longest series of observations was undertaken by Schmidt (Athens). 
23 April, after the Moon had set, the comet appeared through a small seeker 
(x 8 magnification) as a circular nebula, larger in diameter than the nebula 
near € Pegasi (NGC 7078; according to Holetschek, m*=6™.2), but slightly 
239 

===PAGE_0058 (book p.240)===
fainter, 
Thus the comet's brightness was not less than 5 to 6™. 
24 April, 
visible with difficulty to the naked eye (5™,5); refractors showed it slightly 
condensed; 
an 11™ nucleus, coma 4'.4 along the sunward radius. 
6 May, an 
increase in brightness (5 to 7™), nucleus 12™, radius of the coma 4!.3. 
7, 
8 and 9 May, easily seen through a seeker (5 to 6™), 
12 May, large and 
dull; less dense and duller than the cluster near 7 Herculi (M13; according 
to Holetschek m=5™.8); 
difficult to see with naked eye (5.5). 
13 May, con- 
densation; nucleus 12 to 13™, radius of the coma 4'.7. 
15 May to 19 May, 
same appearance through seeker. 
18 May, nucleus 12™.5, radius of the 
coma 4.9, 
4 June, appeared considerably fainter through seeker (7 to 8™). 
9 June to 13 June, very pale, almost no condensation, 
no nucleus; 
radius 
of the coma 2'.4 (8 to 10™). 
3 June and 7 June, Schulhof described the comet as faint. 
12 June, 
Bruhns (Leipzig) reported that the comet was too faint for observations (9 
to 10™), 
27 April, 
Rimker and Pechule (Hamburg) estimated the coma 
diameter at 6 to 7! in moonlight; central condensation 50" in diameter. 
19 May, very diffuse, granular object (7™?). 
8 June to 10 June, at the 
visibility threshold in bright twilight (8 to 10™). Observed also at Kiev, 
Cracow, Kremsmunster, 
Milan, Naples and Pola. 
Last measured by 
Schulhof (Vienna) on 17 June (10 
to 11™). 
References: A.N., 83-86; V.J.S., 
10; E-II.

1874 Ill (1874c). 
Bright comet. 
Discovered by Coggia (Marseilles) on 
17 April in Camelopardus. 
Slowly moved south; inJune, located at only 
4 to 5° from the position upon discovery. 
In July, moved rapidly south 
passing through Lynx, Gemini, Canis Minor and Monoceros. 
In August, 
located in Puppis and Carina. 
In September, 
in Volans even nearer to the 
southern pole; in October, moved slowly toward the pole. 
19 April, Winnecke (Strasbourg) described the comet as very bright and 
elongated, 
3 to 4' in diameter, with a very faint nucleus (7 to 8™). 
20 April, 
Schulhof (Vienna) reported the granular structure of the condensation: 
at 
least three nucleoid points were detectible; 
22 April, two points of conden- 
sation observed in the nucleus; 
6 May, brighter comet, more eccentric 
nucleus; trace of a fan-shaped tail. 
27 April, 
Rumker (Hamburg) observed 
the comet as a glittering object in bright moonlight; D=1'; starlike nucleus. 
15 May to 23 May, d'Arrest estimated the nucleus at 8™.5, the tail at 2! 
(6 to 7™). 
6 May, Vogel (Bothkamp), observing the spectrum, found the 
comet to be bright and elongated at P= 35°; a condensation 2'.5 to 3' in 
diameter and a starlike nucleus (6 to 7™); 
15 May, circular object with a 
10' tail; 
18 May, Tempel observed the appearance of bright luminous arches 
in the head extending from the starlike nucleus; 
tail up to 15' long (P= 30 
to 35°); nucleus 8™, 
Beginning 11 May, observedby F.A. Bredikhin (Moscow) 
on the 91/2-inch refractor. 
240 

===PAGE_0059 (book p.241)===
In June, tail lengthened. 
8 June, Tempel (Milan) reported a tail more 
than 1° long. 
Beginning the middle of June, visible to the naked eye. 19June, 
tail over 3° long, 6 to 8' wide at the end. 
At that time the nucleus was 
slightly brighter 
than a reddish 
5™ star near the head; a 3 to 4™ head. 
29 June, Tempel estimated the nucleus at 4™. 
22 June, Vogel reported the 
appearance of a dark line extending from the nucleus and bisecting the tail; 
bright jets observed in the head. 
2 July, Bruhns (Leipzig) observed a jet 
sector with a 100° angle and 20 to 25" height. At thattime, 
Tempel estimated 
the nucleus at 2 to 3™(m=1 to 2™), tail 5° long, 
12 to 15' wide at the middle. 
6 July, Bruhns estimated the comet at 2™, 10° tail. 
7 July, to the naked eye, 
1 to 2 , tail exceeded by 11/3 the distance between a and B Ursae Majoris; 
very clear dark line in the tail. 
Same observations reported by Tempel. 
Systematic brightness estimates were undertaken by Schmidt (Athens) 
with the naked eye. 
He estimated the brightness at 2™.7, whereas Hind gave 
1™.9. 
Schmidt apparently underestimated the comet's brightness by neglect- 
ing the effect of the low altitude or by concentrating his attention on the 
central condensation only. 
9 July, Bruhns gave 
m=1™.4; Schmidt, 2™.0. 
Wide jet sector with a 140° apex angle, D=1'. 
At that time, Tempel esti- 
mated the nucleus telescopically at 9™, tail over 13° long. 
10 July, Bruhns 
gave 1 to 2™ for the nucleus; according to Schmidt, nucleus 1™.9. 
13 July, 
Bruhns estimated the comet at 1”.3 with the naked eye, brighter than Capella 
(0™.21) but 
in cirrus and at low altitude; according to Schmidt m=1™.5. 
To the naked eye, the tail was 20° long, 60' wide at a distance of 3°; a high- 
intensity jet sector 30'' high. Envelope at a distance of 90''. Bruhns reported 
an easily visible dark line in the tail. 
14 July, Tempel saw a 30° tail. 
D'Arrest (Copenhagen) estimated the nucleus at 1™.2. According to Bruhns, 
16 July, 
tail 33° long; 
17 July, 
36°; 
18 July, nucleus invisible, 
tail 48° long 
and 5° wide nearoa Ursae Majoris. 
20 July, tail last visible up to 63° long, 
estimated by Abbe (Washington) at 34°. 
Schmidt saw a narrow straight tail 
preceding the main one. 
Moved rapidly south; beginning 17 July, not visible from European ob- 
servatories. 
Beginning 27 July, observed from the southern hemisphere; 
till 6 Oct., measured by White (Melbourne) with the 4 1/2-inch equatorial 
(not fainter than 10 to 11™). 
Till 7 Oct., observed by Tebbutt (Windsor, 
Australia),also with a 41/2-inch telescope. 
He reported that in October 
the comet was exceptionally faint and diffuse (10 to 11™). 
Last observation 
obtained by Thome (Cordoba) on 18 Oct. (11™?). 
Drawings of the tail and 
head features were made by Tempel, Vogel, Brodie, Rayet, Bredikhin and 
others.

1877 iI (1877b). 
Discovered by Winnecke (Strasbourg) on 5 April in

located in Lacerta at the end of April, passing between Cepheus and 
Cassiopeia; 
in the middle of May, located at 10° from the pole; at the end 
of May and in June, 
in Ursa Major;

a 10 to 11” nucleus, very faint double tail up to 1° long 
extending away from the Sun; a short second branch formed a 95° angle with 
the main tail. 
7 April, Bruhns (Leipzig) estimated the brightness at 7™; 
D=4', Well visible in bright twilight. 
8 April, D=3'; strong condensation; 
nucleus 4" in diameter.

15 April, 
Winnecke reported a 2° main tail and a fan-shaped secondary tail. 
To the 
naked eye, the comet appeared as a 5 to 6™ object. 
19 April, Tupman 
(Greenwich) estimated the brightness of the comet at 6™.5; D=3'.5. 
In May, 
measured on meridian circles at Vienna and Pola.

by Copeland at the beginning of May.

estimated the comet's brightness to the naked eye (6 to 7™?); at the end of 
July, 6™.8 to 7%.0.

6™, Up to 10 July the tail consisted of two components.

was 9 to 10' in diameter, and the nucleus was 8 to 9™ through a refractor.

comet-seeker and binoculars.

tail.

1877 III (1877c). 
Spotted for the first time by Block (Odessa) on 10 April 
as a previously unknown nebula in Cassiopeia. 
Detected by Swift (Rochester, 
U.S.A.) on 11 April and independently by Borrelly (Marseilles) on 14 April. 
Cometary nature established on 16 April by Block. 
Moved rapidly north; 
located in Camelopardus at the end of April and in Lynx in the middle of 
May and at the beginning of June. 
Upon discovery, Borrelly described itas abright circular object witha 7 to 
8™ nucleus. 
10 April, Block reported the comet to be faint (8™). 
15 April 
Pechule (Copenhagen) described the comet as a small, very bright object 
highly condensed at the center; 
Bruhns (Leipzig) reported it to be faint 
with 
D=i1/2'. On the same day, observed by Winnecke (Strasbourg) near 
the lower culmination; oval shaped, no starlike nucleus (not fainter than 8™). 
16 April, Bruhns reported faint condensation and blurred edges. 
17 April, 
visible in moonlight and cirrus. 
26 April, nucleus visible in full moon (7 
to 8™), 
11 May, 10™ comet with an eccentric faint condensation. 
13 May, 
a diffuse spot with a hardly noticeable condensation. 
3 June, spotted by 
Bruhns 
with a 
12-inch equatorial as an exceptionally faint nebulous mass; 
inaccessible to measurements (11 to 12™). Apparently, 
a rapid drop in 
brightness in May; 
observed in bright twilight sky. 
Measured at Athens, Berlin, Bonn, Hamburg, 
Kiel, Kremsmunster, 
Leipzig, Milan, Mannheim, Odessa, Paris, Pulkovo, Washington, Vienna 
Clinton and Christiania. 
Last measured at Oxford on 4 June.

1877 IV (1877d). 
The fourth recorded apparition 
of the short-period 
Comet d'Arrest. 
Discovered almost simultaneously on 9 July according 
to Leveau's ephemeris by Coggia (Marseilles) and Tempel (Arcetri); ap- 
parently observed already on 13 June by White (Melbourne). 
Located in 
Cetus, moving slowly east through Taurus; entered Orion at the beginning 
of September. 
In July, according to Tempel, equal in brightness to a 1st or 
2nd class nebula; D=3' (10™?). 
10 July, 
17 July and 12 Aug., Schiaparelli 
(Milan) observed the comet with an 8-inch refractor as a luminous spot, 
very faint and ill shaped at the visibility threshold; 
D=11/2 to 2'. 
13 July, 
Schmidt (Athens) estimated the comet as a very faint 10 to 11™ object through 
a 4-inch telescope. 6 Aug. and 7 Aug., observed by Bruhns (Leipzig) as an 
exceptionally faint diffuse nucleus. 
D=1.5 to 2'.0; was detectible only in 
dark sky, with difficulty (11 to 12™?). 
10 Sept., last measured by Schmidt; 
fainter than any comet previously observed, with the exception of Comet 
Faye in 1861 (11 to 12™). 
Full moon on 25 July and 28 Aug.

1880 I (1880a). 
Large southern comet. 
The tail, extending from below 
the horizon, was seen in Argentina on the evening of 31 Jan. 
The tail was 
observed from 1 Feb. throughout South America, South Africa, and Australia. 
2 Feb., Gould (Cordoba) saw the tail extend to y Tucanae near§ and B 
Gruss; the comet's head remained below the horizon. 
Gwynne and Morris 
(Montevideo), Ellery (Melbourne) and Russell (Sydney) with the naked eye 
saw the tail extend over 30° above the horizon; the upper part curved slightly 
to the south. 
1 Feb., comet visible as an object of exceptional brightness, 
tail 3° wide; 2 Feb., noticeably fainter. 
3 Feb., Gould reported a 40° tail 
of almost uniform brightness, 
11/2° wide neart Gruss and 2 1/2° wide near 
n Phoenicis. 
Gould first saw the head in twilight and haze near the horizon 
as a dull mass 2 to 3' in diameter, 
no nucleus. 
4 Feb., Eddie (Graham's 
Town, South Africa) reported that the tail had lost its curvature. 
5 Feb., 
head spotted, equal in size to the planetary nebula in Lyra (1'xX1'.4); head 
resembled 47 Tucanae (m =3™); pale yellow nucleus, 
central condensation 
(3° south of Fomalhaut). 
Tail more than 50° long; 
1° wide at 20° from the 
head. 
7 Feb., nucleus near ySculptoris; 
tail extending beyond 
a Eridani. 
Gould reported that on the night between 7 Feb. and 8 Feb. the tail 
attained its maximum brightness —not brighter than the Milky Way in Taurus. 
Head elongated in the direction of the tail and not much brighter than it. 
The narrow tail was not wider than 1°30' and gradually grew smaller; 
14 Feb., still 35° long. 
At that time, Tebbutt (Windsor) saw neither nucleus 
nor condensations, and Eddie described the tail as quite rectilinear and 
colorless. 
Gill (Cape) mapped the tail from 8 Feb. to 15 Feb. 
17 Feb., 
in 
Melbourne, 
the nucleus could be observed with difficulty on dark field (7 to 
8™). 
19 Feb., head at the visibility threshold of the Cordoba equatorial. 
Beginning 14 Feb., not visible in Rio de Janeiro. 
Position observations 
carried out at Cordoba and the Cape.

Discovered by Schaeberle (Ann Arbor) on 6 April near 
the north pole in Camelopardus.

in

1880 III (1880c). 
Discovered on 29 Sept. by Hartwig (Strasbourg) as a 
bright object with a 50! tail (5 to 6™). Discovered independently on 30 Sept. 
by Harting (Ann Arbor). 
Located moving east in Bootes; 
in October, passed 
through Corona Borealis and Hercules; 
at the end of October and November, 
moved through Ophiuchus and Serpens; 
at the end of November, located in 
Aquila. 
30 Sept., Konkoly (Ogyalla) described it as very bright with condensation. 
According to Kobold (Ogyalla) the tail was 2° long, glittering and slightly 
curving. 
1 Oct., Schmidt (Athens) saw the comet with the naked eye (5.5 to 
6™); fainter than the Andromeda Nebula; nucleus 7™ through a telescope, 
6™.2 to the naked eye; radius of the coma 3'.7. 
Schmidt reported that this 
was the 81st comet observed by him; "telescopic, yet magnificent and 
brilliant object"; large coma and a narrow tail extending away from the Sun; 
resembled 1847 III and 1862 VIII (5 to 6™). 
2 Oct., visible in Washington

1880 IV (1880e) (fin Baldet's catalogue). The second recorded apparition 
of the short-period Comet Tempel-Swift. 
Discovered as a new comet by 
L. Swift (Rochester) on 10 Oct. Located moving north-east in Pegasus; at the 
beginning of November, 
located in Vulpecula and Andromeda; 
in the middle 
of November, 
in Cassiopeia; 
at the end of November, moved to Perseus; 
in the middle of December, passed near Capella; 
at the end of January, 
located in Gemini. 
Due to a telegraphic error, not observed in Europe in 
October and discovered independently by Lohse (Dun Echt) on 7 Nov., 
21 Oct. and 22 Oct., Barnard (Nashville) described the comet as large, 
diffuse, and completely transparent. 
24 Oct., visible through a 11/4-inch 
seeker (not fainter than 8™). 
Beginning the end of November, observed in 
Europe. 
20 Nov., Boergen (Wilhelmshaven) described the comet as faint 
and diffuse; observations impossible due to acrescent Moon (10™). 
24 Nov. 
to 28 Nov., Kobold (Ogyalla) described the comet as wide and diffuse, with 
no traces of condensation (9 to 10™). 
29 Nov., Auwers (Berlin) saw the 
comet as a very bright, circular, condensed object; D~3'; central conden- 
sation 11™ (8 to 9™). 
30 Nov. to 4 Dec., observed by Schmidt (Athens). 
He reported that ob- 
servations were hindered by the comet's location in the Milky Way (in 
Perseus); circular object, without nucleus; 
a condensation. 
A 12™.5 nucleus 
observed once.

follows: 
9!.4, 
14'.5, 
13'.3, 13.8 respectively (8 to 9™).

through a seeker, whereas invisible through a seeker in 1869. 
Kobold esti- 
mated the diameter at 5 to 6' at this time [sic]. 
1 Dec., D=1', condensation, 
nucleus 11™ (8 to 9™). 4 Dec., measured on the Konigsberg heliometer. 
Observed by Block (Odessa) till 4 Dec. 
asa noticeably bright object with 
a 5"x10" nucleus. 
23 Dec., 
still visible and measured by Auwers (Berlin) 
on a 6-inch refractor (11 to 12™). 
26 Dec., already invisible. 
24 Dec. to 
27 Dec., Schmidt (Athens) observed the comet with 
a 51/2-inch refractor. 
So faint that only the hypothetical position could be marked; nebula disap- 
peared near the rings. Measured by Tempel (Arcetri) from the end of 
December till 2 Jan. 1881; still observable as a small and faint object (11 
to 12™), From December to 7 Jan., observed on a 26-inch refractor in 
Washington.

up to 20 Jan.

1881 I (1880c). 
The sixth recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Faye. 
Spotted by Common (Ealing, near London) on 2 Aug. with 
18- and 36-inch reflectors. 
Discovered independently by Tempel (Arcetri) 
on 11 Aug. and measured first on 25 Aug. Located moving south in Pegasus; 
in October and November, located in Pisces. 
In December 1880 and January 
1881, moved north-east in Pisces; 
in February, located in Cetus; 
in the 
second half of March, 
in Taurus.

(10 to 11™); Pechule (Copenhagen) measured the comet on an 11-inch refrac-
