slight condensation; nucleus 12™.5 (10 to 11™). Beginning 24 Sept., meas- 
ured in Rome with a 9-inch refractor as a 13™ star surrounded by an ir- 
regular nebula. 
28 Sept., difficult to observe due to the proximity of a 
10™ star (not fainter than 10 to 11™). 
Beginning 25 Sept., easily observed in Washington through a 26 inch 
refractor; 
a small well-shaped nucleus.

the comet as very faint through a 10-inch telescope (10 to 11™); 25 Oct., 
observed at Rome as a faint object (11™).

ured the comet with a 5-inch refractor, registering a 1'.5 to 1'".7 diameter 
and central condensation; nucleus of 12™.8 to 13™.

1881 III (1881b) (c in Baldet's catalogue). 
Large comet. 
First spotted 
below Columba by Tebbutt (Windsor) on the evening of 22 May. 
Easily 
visible to the naked eye with a 5™ nucleus (3™). 
23 May, discovered inde- 
pendently by Ellery (Melbourne); 25 May, by Gould and Davis (Cordoba). 
Moved north along the 5-65 meridians. 
At the beginning of June, located in 
Lepus; from mid-June, traversedOrion; from 22 June, observed in Europe; 
atthe endofJune, passedthroughAuriga, traversing Camelopardus; 
in July, 
passed through the polar region (23 July, 
8° from the pole); in August and 
September, observed in Ursa Minor; in October and November, 
located in 
Draco, moving almost along the 70° parallel; at the end of November and in 
December, located inCepheus; in January and February, 
in Cassiopeia. 
25 May, tail estimated at 2° in Windsor. 
3 May, Ellery spotted a bright 
starlike nucleus of 5™. 
Elkin (Cape) observed the comet from 31 May to 
4 June, estimating the brightness at 2™ and the straight tail at 5 to 6°; D=10 
to 20', nucleus 10 to 20" in diameter. 
2 June, Tebbutt saw a 9° tail; 5 June, 
measured by Gould almost in daylight. 
11 June, Gould measured the position 
of the comet near the horizon with respect to a nebulous object which later 
on disappeared; 
an assumption has been advanced that this was a case either 
of a comet split into two components or of a cometary satellite. 
Another 
assumption has been advanced that Gould confused the comet with A Eridani 
and a nearby 5™ star; Gould, however, maintained that he actually measured 
the comet. 
22 July, Krueger (Kiel) estimated the head at 1™, reporting a noticeable 
tail, 
24 July, Abetti (Padua) with the naked eye saw a 10° tail. 
23 July to 
25 July, Fabritsius (Kiev) estimated the nucleus at 2™. 
Observed in lower 
culmination with meridian instruments at Moscow, Pulkovo, Warsaw, Berlin, 
Copenhagen, and at numerous other observatories. 
Plate XXIII, Comet 1881 III, Tails after Tempel, head after Tacchini

with a relatively bright eastern edge.

plume-like jets, 
15° tail.

Sun; 12° tail.

nucleus, and the position angle of the tail,

head were undertaken by Schur (Strasbourg).

the nuclear brightness under high magnification; 24 June, 5™;

1881 IV (1881c) (d in Baldet's catalogue). Discovered by Schaeberle (Ann 
Arbor) on the night of 13 July; located in Auriga, moving to Lynx. In August, 
located in Lynx; 
in the middle of August, 
in Ursa Major; at the end of 
August, 
in Coma Berenices; 
at the beginning of September, began to move 
rapidly south, passing through Virgo and Libra; 
in October, traversed 
Hydra. 
Upon discovery, brilliant object visible to the naked eye. 
19 July, excel- 
lently visible; according to Coggia (Marseilles), brighter at the center, 
which appeared to be composed of many bright points; 21 July, Tempel 
(Arcetri) described the comet as a 6™ star with a very diffuse tail 1° long. 
23 July, central condensation of 10 to 11"; 28 Aug., Coggia saw the comet 
with the naked eye.

visible to the naked eye (5 to 6™). After 31 July, Abetti described the comet 
as visible to the naked eye.

nucleus.

1881 V (1881f) (g in Baldet's catalogue). 
Discovered in Leo by Denning 
(Bristol) on the morning of 4 Oct. 
Moved east, 
in November, 
still in Leo. 
Upon discovery, 
a small bright nebula with a central condensation (7 to 8™). 
5 Oct., Coggia (Marseilles) described the comet as suitable for measure- 
ments only for a short time after the Moon had set. 
Rapidly grew faint; 
spotted in Bonn and Strasbourg only on 18 Oct. 
Through a 5-foot refractor 
the comet appeared as a gossamer, elliptical nebula; D=11/2'; aneccentric 
condensation (9 to 10™). Winnecke (Strasbourg) reported the comet to ap- 
pear as a class J nebula through an 18-inch refractor. 
19 Oct., D=1', very 
diffuse edges, a nebulous disk at the center. 
28 Oct., faint at low altitude 
and in poor sky; appeared elongated intermittently with a 2' semimajor 
axis; easily observed with a 6-inch seeker (8 to 9™). Zona (Palermo) saw 
luminous points near the center of the nebula. 
27 Oct. , visible with difficulty. 
27 Oct. ,and 3 Nov., Schmidt (Athens) described the cometas an exceptionally 
faint nebula (9 to 10™). 
Measured also at Cambridge, 
Dun Echt, Odessa, 
Marseilles and Paris. 
Last measured in Strasbourg on 24 Nov. (11 to 12™).

Virginis. 
Moved north through Bootes at the end of September and in October. 
Upon discovery and later, observed near the horizon as a circular, bright, 
highly condensed object; D=2'. 
18 Sept., Barnard reported a faint tail 
(6 to 7™),

Universities.

long.

a very faint starlike nucleus.

1881 VII (1881d) (e in Baldet). 
The twenty-third recorded apparition 
of the Comet Encke-Backlund. 
Spotted according to Backlund's ephemeris 
on 20 Aug. in Strasbourg and Leipzig (12™), on 21 Aug. in Arcetri and on 
24 Aug. in Pulkovo. 
Located in Perseus; 
rapidly moved east; 
in September- 
October passed Auriga, Lynx and Leo; 
in November, located in Virgo. 
20 Aug., too faint to bear illumination; 
24 Aug., hardly visible through 
a 15-inch refractor (13™). 
25 Aug., observers in Leipzig described it as 
a nebulous gossamer spot. D=4' (10 to 12™). In September, 
an increase in 
brightness, though the comet still remained pale and without a nucleus 
for some time. 
19, 20 and 22 Sept., Schmidt (Athens) easily saw the comet 
through a seeker (9 to 10™). 
At the end of September, 
a nucleus observed; 
through a seeker, brighter than the 9™ comparison stars (8 to 9™). 
19 Oct., 
Schmidt saw the comet through a seeker in moonlight as a large brilliant 
object with substantial condensation; visible to the naked eye (6 to 7™). 
Same shape as reported by Schmidt in October 1848. 
21 Oct., Barnard 
saw the comet as a very bright circular object with moderate condensation; 
well visible through a 30-mm seeker (7 to 8™). 
26 Oct., 
in Pulkovo, 
at 6:00 A.M. 
in the morning twilight, 
the comet 
was still bright, disappearing only at 6:15 A.M. 
27 Oct., with the naked 
eye Schmidt saw the comet without certainty; through a refractor, 
dense, 
white object without a nucleus. 
A narrow 5 to 7' tail. 
28 Oct., brightness 
estimated at Pulkovo at 6 to 7™ 
4 Nov., Schmidt saw the comet last before 
the perihelion passage through a refractor when low above the horizon; 
very small white, brilliant, dense nebula with a sharp-point tail 7' long. 
Through a seeker appeared as a diffuse star of 5". 
Schmidt reported varia- 
tion in comet's diameter at approach to the Sun (A.N., 101:297). 
6 Nov., 
Winnecke (Strasbourg) estimated the brightness at 6 to 7™ through a seeker 
{x30 magnification); 
through a telescope, 
a circular central disk 50'' in 
diameter. 
5 Nov., Tacchini (Rome) saw the comet with the naked eye; ob- 
servations difficult due to bright sky and low altitude. 
Observed in Moscow 
till 
7 Nov., 
in Rome till 11 Nov. 
Bright object at the visibility threshold 
of the naked eye.

nuclear brightness in May and June were compiled by Abetti (Padua);

In April Vogel (Potsdam) and other observers reported the strong con- 
tinuous spectrum of the nucleus and mere traces of ordinary bands. 
In May, 
considerable increase in comet's brightness. 
At the end of May, observed 
on the meridian circle at the Moscow Observatory. 
Photometric measure- 
ments on the Zoellner photometer were made by Muller (Potsdam), 
who 
reported that, with the approach to the Sun, brightness increased more 
rapidly than r7?, Following are the logarithms of the ratios of the brightness

From the end of May, Fearnly (Christiania) saw the comet with the naked 
eye on a bright twilight sky. 
31 May, Stone (Oxford) with the naked eye 
estimated the comet's brightness as equal to Persei (3™.10); through a 
telescope, 
a 4™.5 nucleus. 
2 June, Vienna observers estimated the nucleus 
at 3™; with the naked eye, 
a tail over 5° long. 
3 June, Stone estimated the 
nucleus at 2 to 3™ with a telescope. 
31 May, Vogel reported the appearance 
of a yellow line in the spectrum; 
its position later was identified with that 
of the sodium line. 
Spectrum showed traces of bright bands and some 
Fraunhofer lines. 
The sodium line was independently discovered by F.A, 
Bredikhin in Moscow and Gassel'berg in Pulkovo. A weak red band (6160A) 
was detected by observers at both Moscow and Pulkovo; 
the continuous 
spectrum of the nucleus extended from 6660 to 4550A. The sodium line 
was well visible in daytime after the sunrise; 
it was not limited to the 
nucleus, 
but was traced in the entire head. 
At various observatories comet was visible in daytime near the Sun. 
10 June, Schmidt (Athens) observed the comet near the Sun by means of a 
special provision on the telescopic objective, measuring its position with 
respect to the solar limb. Appeared as a white dense point with diffuse 
edges 5 to 6" in diameter; no trace of a tail or jets (-2™, -5™?). 23 June, 
observed from shipboard by Pakl, who measured its position with respect 
to Venus. 
Sharp nucleus like a 2™ star (1 to 2™); fan-shaped tail visible to 
the naked eye over 21/2°. 
17 June, end of tail visible 40 to 45° from the 
nucleus, 
Schmidt determined in the evening twilight the positions from 
16 June to 21 July, including 1 July (full moon) when comet was very low 
in the sky (-2™, -4™), 
2 Aug., located again before the Moon had risen. 
Faint object with small central condensation; 
D=31/2'. 
Measured with 
difficulty at an altitude of 6 to 9° (7 to 8™). 
6 Aug., comet was impossible 
to measure, disappearing near the ring (8 to 9™). Finlay (Cape) observed 
the comet till 16 Aug. (9 to 10™).

1882 II (1882d) (b in current publications). 
Brilliant comet known as the 
"September Comet". 
Spotted in the southern sky by sailors on board Italian 
ship on 1 Sept. 
3 Sept., seen in Aukland (New Zealand). 
6 Sept., Gould 
(Cordoba) was notified concerning the discovery and saw the comet first on 
14 Sept. 
Cruls (Rio de Janeiro) was notified of the comet on 10 Sept. and 
observed it on the morning of 12 Sept. Discovered by Finlay (Cape) on the 
morning of 8 Sept. 
In Australia, spotted by sailors on 7 Sept., and from 
8 Sept. observed throughout the world. 
5 Sept., located in Hydra, 
at 27° from the Sun; visible before sunrise. 
14 Sept., only 12° from the Sun. 
At that time bright enough to be observed 
in Melbourne with a meridian circle during daytime. 
Beginning 16 Sept., 
Tebbutt saw the comet with the naked eye in daytime as a brilliant white 
object with a 20! tail. 17 Sept., Finlay and Elkin (Cape) observed the comet 
disappear instantaneously upon occulting the solar disk, as a star disappears 
when occulted by the Moon. 
In Europe, the comet was observed on that day 
near the Sun: Common observed it before the perihelion passage, and Tollon 
after the perihelion passage; 
the comet could not have been fainter than 
gee 
ah prs 
During several months the comet was an exceptional celestial phenomenon; 
visible to the naked eye till the beginning of March 1883; located in the sky 
south of the equator. 
At the end of September, located in Sextans; in October, 
moved to Hydra; 
at the end of November, 
located in Pyxis; 
in December, 
moved west almost along the parallel, passing through Puppis and entering 
Canis Major. 
In January 1883, moved through Canis Major; in February, 
located in Lepus; 
in March, entered Orion and moved slowly along the 
border of Monoceros; 
in May and June, located in Monoceros, moving 
slowly east. 
As noted by Gould, the reports of observers show that on 5 Sept. the 
comet was as bright as Venus with a brilliant tail. 
7 Sept., Finlay (Cape) 
estimated the nucleus at 3™ in twilight. 
18 Sept. in the morning, astonish- 
ingly bright, pure yellow on the dark-blue sky during the sunrise. 
After 
sunrise, 
the comet did not grow fainter, becoming rather sharper; through 
a meridian, observed 10 minutes after passing the Sun(-5™). 
22 Sept., 
nucleus appeared as a 3™ star; visible in day time; the entire head probably 
visible (-2™). 
The immergence of the comet onto the solar disk was des- 
cribed by Elkin and Finlay as the occultation of a star by the Moon. 
Through 
a red glass, the immergence appeared as the occultation of a 4™ star on the 
bright edge; no traces of a dark nucleus on the solar disk. 
According to 
Elkin, on 7 Sept. the nucleus could not have been fainter than - 10™.

equal to Jupiter in brightness and size; 
12° tail.

refractor,

At the end of September and the beginning of October, Terby (Luvain), 
Riccd (Palermo) and Weinek (Leipzig) compared 
the nuclear brightness to that 
of a 1™ star. 
5 Oct., Engelhardt (Dresden) estimated the nucleus as con- 
siderably brighter than 1™ through a 5-inch seeker (0™), 
At that time, the 
tail was over 20° long with a rermarkable dark narrow band in the middle 
like a shadow of the nucleus. 
Beginning the end of September, the following changes in the nucleus 
were observed: 
till 28 Sept., a single nucleus (Cape); 30 Sept., two elon- 
gated nuclei; 
1 Oct., elongation. At the beginning of October, 
all European 
observers reported the multiple structure of the nucleus (four to five con- 
densations) elongated roughly inthe direction of the tail. 
17 Oct., the line 
of the nuclei was almost 40" long. 
Elongated nucleus with numerous centers 
was observed during the entire visibility period of the comet. 
9 Oct. to 11 Oct., Schmidt (Athens) observed to the south-west of the 
comet cloud-like formations which receded from the comet by approximately 
1° per day. 
9 Oct., Harting saw a cloud satellite south-west of the comet, 
determining its position relative to stars. 
Other observers reported a 
nebulous tube or envelope extending over several degrees towards the Sun. 
14 Oct., Barnard saw six to eight objects 6° south-west of the comet; some 
were elongated, resembling telescopic comets. 
These nebulous formations 
were also seen by other observers on that day. 
Beginning 16 Oct., an 
anomalous sunward tail was visible to the naked eye. 
23 Oct., Gothard 
(Herény, Hungary) estimated the tail at 30°. No polarization reported. 
For several months the comet was a remarkable object in the southern 
sky. 
In the middle of January 1883, according to Gould (Cordoba) the tail 
was up to 15° long, being easily visible to the naked eye (3 to 4™). Tebbutt 
(Windsor) observed an oval head with slight condensation and saw the comet 
with the naked eye till 13 Feb. 
Gould last saw the comet on 7 March (6 to 
7™), 
Engelhardt (Dresden) saw a very faint comet in March (low position); 
5 March, visible with difficulty through a 12-inch telescope; 
a bright point 
in the nebula. 
Till 8 Feb., Schmidt (Athens) described the comet as a 
phenomenal object; the multiple structure of the nucleus visible at any time 
through a small eye-piece. 
At the end of March, visible through a seeker; 
in April, fainter, 27 April only hypothetical position indicated. 
28 April, 
through a 123-mm objective, visible only by peripherial vision in the middle

1882 III (1882e) (c in current publications). 
Discovered by Barnard 
(Nashville, U.S.A.) on the morning of 14 Sept. as a result of regular 
searches; appeared as a circular nebula without a tail, 2' in diameter, 
m=10™ or fainter. 
Located in Gemini, moving south; passed near Procyon; 
at the beginning of October traversed Monoceros; 
in the middle of October, 
located in Pyxis, becoming invisible in the northern hemisphere. 
16 Sept., observed with difficulty by Krueger (Kiel) at poor transparency, 
appearing as a faint circular mass on illuminated reticle (9 to 10™). 26 Sept., 
Weinek (Leipzig) spotted the comet without difficulty with a 4-inch telescope 
in spite of the bright Moon (8 to 9); he measured the comet till the end of 
September. 
5 Oct., Engelhardt (Dresden) described the comet as very 
bright (8™?). 
Schmidt (Athens) with a seeker could not see the comet in 
moonlight. Wilson (Cincinnati) measured the comet with an 11-inch refrac- 
tor from 24 Sept. to 13 Oct. 
Initially very faint, no noticeable tail, a bright 
central point; 
12 Oct., highly condensed. 
Barnard measured the comet till 
16 Oct., when it was very bright and easily observed through a 11/4-inch 
seeker; traces of a tail directed away from the Sun (7 to Sy 
ele Oca 
Schmidt with a seeker easily saw the comet near the horizon, 
in the absence 
of moonlight; through a refractor, 
it appeared as a highly condensed circu- 
lar nebula, 
8™ at the center (7 to 8™). 
10 Oct., Schmidt saw traces of a 
faint tail 8 to 9' long. 
Measured also in Nikolaev, Glasgow, Cambridge, 
Konigsberg, Lyon, Palermo, Pola, Washington and Vienna. 
After the perihelion passage (13 Nov.) spotted by Tebbutt (Windsor) on 
30 Nov. and measured by him till 8 Dec. with a 41/2-inch telescope. 
Too 
faint for observations due to position in the bright band of the sky near the 
horizon (9 to 10™?).

1883 I (1883a). 
Discovered in Pegasus by Brooks (New York) on 23 Feb. 
and independently by Swift (Rochester) on the same day. Moved east; at 
the beginning of March, located in Andromeda, Pisces and Aries; 
at the 
end of March, 
in Taurus; 
at the end of April, moved to Orion. 
Initially, 
a bright comet with a faint tail and a highly condensed circular 
head, 
3 to 4' in diameter. 
26 Feb., Vogel (Potsdam) saw the comet as a 
very bright object with a 4' tail; a continuous spectrum and the three usual 
bands (6 to 7™?). 
26 Feb. 
to 3 March, Gonessiat (Lyon) described the 
comet as a bright nebula with a nucleus and a 13! tail (7™?). 
28 Feb.,

P=20° and 
second fainter one, perpendicular to the first, 
at 
P=300°; 
1 March, both 
tails visible.

starlike nucleus of 7", D=3', 
tail 40' long; the 
second tail also visible. 
24 March, comet almost circular, D=2'; nucleus 
well condensed, 
but not starlike. 
1 March, Engelman (Leipzig) saw a large head and a starlike, slightly 
granular nucleus of 10™, traces of a narrow tail (P =10°); through a seeker, 
a T head. 
1 March, Peter (Leipzig) estimated the tail at 20'. 
On that day, 
Engelhardt (Dresden) saw the comet as a very bright object, D=2'; through 
a 5-inch seeker, appeared as a 6™.5 star. 
3 March, Konkoly investigated 
the spectrum with a 10-inch telescope, measuring three bright bands and

1883 II (1884a). 
Discovered by amateur astronomer Ross (Elsternwick, 
near Melbourne) on 7 Jan. in Grus. 
8 Jan., passed through Grus and in 
February moved in Phoenix. 
Upon discovery, Galle saw the comet with 
the naked eye (6™?). 
12 Jan., Ellery (Melbourne) described the comet as 
a faint nebulous object; D=2'; blurred central condensation; brightness of 
the central part 10™(6 to 7™); a stubby 
tail. 
Rapidly 
grew 
faint; 
at 
the beginning of February measured in moonlight with great difficulty at 
Melbourne. 
Observed in Madras, Windsor and Launceston. Measured last 
in Melbourne on 19 Feb.

1884 I (1883b). 
The second recorded apparition 
of the periodic Comet 
Pons-Brooks. 
Discovered by Brooks (Phelps, New York) on 1 Sept. 1883 
in Draco as a new comet, independently of precalculations. 
Circular object, 
D<1', 
10™, well shaped nucleus, 
no tail. 
First determinations of the orbit 
showed the comet to be identical with 1812, 
269 

===PAGE_0088 (book p.270)===
Moved south; 
at the end of September, entered Hercules, passing through 
it in October and November; 
at the end of November, passed not far from 
Vega and in December, moved east almost along the parallel, moving through 
Cygnus. 
In January 1884, traversed Pegasus, Pisces and Cetus, descending 
south; 
in February, passed through Sculptor and Phoenix; 
in March, located 
in Eridanus; 
in April and May, observed near the Greater Magellanic Cloud. 
Increased in brightness more rapidly than ois 
5 Sept. to 24 Sept., Bigourdan reported an increase in brightness by a 
factor of 30 to 40; apart from the continuous increase, 
abrupt brightness 
flares were observed on several occasions. 
22 Sept., many observers still 
saw the comet as a faint diffuse nebula with a 12™ nucleus. On the evening 
of 23 Sept., however, 
in Vienna, Padua and Pulkovo, 
the comet was ob- 
served as a Stellar object of 7 to 8™, of noticeable extent and with a coma 
1 to 1.5 in diameter. 
Chandler (Harvard) established that the flare occurred 
on 22 Sept. between 7:45 A.M. and 11:45 A.M.; after that, the comet 
showed traces of a tail. 
Numerous brightness estimates were compiled by Schiaparelli (Milan), 
Muller (Potsdam), Wolf (Bonn), Wildon(Cincinnati), 
Sawyer (Cambridgeport), 
Weinek (Prague), H. Struve (Pulkovo), Engelhardt (Dresden), 
Abetti (Padua) 
and others. 
From 20 Nov., visible to the naked eye; 
a straight narrow tail, 
which was up to 1° long at the endof December. 
25 Nov., 
a new flare, 
or 
more precisely an increase in the brightness of the nucleus. 
23 Dec. and 
24 Dec., Schiaparelli gave m=4™.3 to 4™.5; 29 Dec., Engelhardt,, 
Schiaparelli and Weinek gave m=3"™.1. 
In the first half of January 1884, 
2 to 3™; at the end of January, 
37.2. to 37.9; 
1 Feb., m=4™.3 (Sawyer); 
8 Feb. and 10 Feb., Atkinson (New Zealand) gave m =4™.4 to 4™.6. 
15 Feb., 
still easily visible without a telescope; 
18 Feb., brightness decreased; 
22 Feb. 
still visible to the naked eye; by 2 March, 
the starlike nucleus 
grew faint and the comet appeared as an ill-defined condensation. 
Later, 
appeared as a circular nebula without substantial condensation. 
At the end 
of April, was at the visibility threshold for numerous observers. 
26 May, 
measured last in Cordoba (9 to 10™) and on 2 June in Nelson (New Zealand) 
(95):

flare.

1884 II (1884c). 
Discovered by Barnard (Nashville) on 16 July in Lupus 
as a diffuse nebula 2' in diameter. 
Slowly moved east almost along the 
parallel; 
in August, passed through Scorpio, 
in September, through 
Sagittarius; 
in October moved in Capricornus; 
at the end of October, assed 
to Aquarius, traversing it in November and then moving along the ecliptic. 
In the initial period, the comet was difficult to measure due to the absence 
of a nucleus or any other noticeable condensation, 
28 July, Finlay (Cape) 
reported a barely scintillating point and condensation in the nebula (9 to 10™). 
1, 5 and 7 Aug., measured in bright moonlight (9m), 
10 Aug., Tempel 
(Arcetri) reported the double structure of the condensation; very large 
nebulous mass easily visible through small telescopes and seekers (Sto. 9™). 
In August, observed in Tashkent; 
14 Aug. and 16 Aug. measured on the 
meridian circle of the Cape Observatory (8 to 9™?). 
14 Sept., Engelhardt 
(Dresden) through a 12-inch telescope saw the comet near the horizon as a 
very faint nebulous mass, D=1', no condensation, 
an intermittently scintil- 
lating tiny point. 
16 Sept., nebula elongated along the parallel, appeared 
to consist of bright and dark spots. 
15 Sept., Finlay reported sharp in- 
crease in brightness (8 to 9™). At the end of September, Perrotin (Nice) 
observed a sharp nucleus and a narrow luminous jet. The spectrum of the 
comet was also observed at Nice (not fainter than 8™). The central (green?) 
and the yellow bands were recorded; mere supposition of a third band. 
Till 24 Oct., measured by Schur (Strasbourg) and Tempel (Arcetri) (10™). 
20 Oct., Schiaparelli (Milan) saw the comet but could not measure it with 
an 8-inch telescope (10 to 11™). 
Thome (Cordoba) discontinued observations 
on 23 Oct. due to moonlight. 
5 Nov., Tempel spotted the comet easily (10 
to 11™); 6 Nov., even more clearly visible than on the previous day, but 
there were no comparison stars. 
8 Nov., an 11™ star when occulted by the 
comet, appeared as if surrounded by a haze (10™). 
15 Nov., Tempel spotted 
the comet again and tracked it till it crossed the ring (not fainter than 11™). 
16 Nov., comet not detected.

1884 III (1884e). 
The first recorded apparition 
of the short-period 
Comet Wolf (1). Discovered in Cygnus by Wolf (Heidelberg) on 17 Sept. Ap- 
_ peared as a bright nebulous mass 2'.5 in diameter with a 9 to 10™ central 
condensation. 
Slowly moved south, passing in October through Pegasus 
and in November-December through Aquarius and Cetus, 
At the beginning 
272 

===PAGE_0091 (book p.273)===
of February 1885, located near Mira Ceti; 
in March and April, passed 
through Eridanus. 
21 Sept., Engelhardt (Dresden), through a 5-inch seeker estimated, D=2'; 
a strong 8™ condensation 13" in diameter. 
At that time, the comet was not 
fainter than 6 to 7"; since on 22 Sept. 
it was independently discovered by 
Copeland (Dun Echt) in his spectroscopic observations of stars. 
21 Sept., 
Benko Boinik (Pola) saw traces of a tail; 24 Sept., Copeland estimated the 
diameter of the comet at 3'. 
24 Sept. to 29 Sept., many astronomers ob- 
served the comet in full moon, reporting an 8™.5 to 9™.5 nucleus (not fainter 
than 7 to 8™), 
14 Oct. to 17 Oct., observed on the meridian circle in 
Coimbra (not fainter than 7 to 8™). 
At that time, Kammermann (Geneva) 
saw the comet with a 10™ nucleus through a 2-inch seeker (7 to 8™). 
Ob- 
served in October-November at Kiel, Leipzig, Munich and other observa- 
tories as a bright object with a 9 to 10™ nucleus. 
11 Nov., Engelhardt 
(Dresden) observed an eccentric nucleus in the coma; 
intrinsic brightness 
of the comet 7™. According to Seeliger, 
a 9™ nucleus on 6 Nov. 
Till 20 Dec., observed by Franz on the Konigsberg heliometer (not fainter 
than 8 to 9™). 
In January 1885, Engelhardt and Winiler described the comet 
as fainter, though still sufficiently bright (10 to 11™). 
In March Lamp (Kiel) and Schiaparelli (Milan) with 8-inch refractors and 
Kammermann (Geneva) with a 10-inch refractor determined the position of 
the comet on a dark field (not fainter than 11 to 12™), though the comet was 
described by Lamp as a very faint, 
dull, and slightly brighter at the center. 
Observed at various European and American observatories, 
and also in 
Tashkent. 
Visible till the middle of April, but measured last on 6 April 
by Young (Princeton).

1885 I (1884f). 
The twenty-fourth recorded apparition 
of the Comet 
Encke-Backlund. 
Discovered according to Backlund's ephemeris by Tempel 
(Arcetri) on 13 Dec. 
1884; after that, observed only on 2 Jan. 
1885 by

Trepier (Algiers), Young (Princeton) and Barnard (Nashville). 
Discovered 
between Aquarius and Pegasus; 
in January amd February 1885, moved east 
in Pisces; 
at the beginning of March, located in Pisces; 
at the end of March, 
descended to Aquarius, moving west; 
in April moved slowly through 
Aquarius. 
Upon discovery, appeared as a faint nebulous glow (11 to 12™). 
3 Jan. 
1885, Tempel reported the comet to be brighter and larger than previously, 
appearing as a faint nebula through a small seeker. 
At the beginning of

nebula; D=1 to 1'.5, no nucleus. 
9 Jan.,

reported considerable decrease in brightness on passing near a 10™ star 
(9 to 10™); measured with a 6-inch equatorial. 
17 Jan., Comet Encke was 
larger than Comet Wolf but less condensed; Comet Wolf was more easily 
observed. 
At the end of January and in February, brightness increased considerably. 
Observers in Munich reported that on 2 Feb. the comet was incomparably 
brighter than on 16 Jan.; nucleus definitely observed. Onthat day, Holetschek 
estimated the comet at 8™.5.

1885 II (1885b) (1885a in M.N.). 
Discovered in Ophiuchus by Barnard 
(Nashville, U.S.A.) on 7 July; 
a circular nebula of 11™ or fainter, 
1' in 
diameter, 
small central condensation, 
no tail. 
This brightness estimate 
apparently referred to the center of the condensation. 
Moved south, passing

at the beginning of September, 
4° south of Antares.

a homogeneous nebula was observed.

brightness.

but without any starlike nucleus; D=3/4'.

1885 II (1885e). 
Discovered in Canes Venatici by Brooks (Phelps, New 
York) on 31 Aug. Discovered independently by Common (Ealing, England) 
on 4 Sept. Moved east; located in Bootes at the beginning of September; 
in the second half of September and in October, moved through Hercules. 
Upon discovery, circular comet 2' in diameter with a 9™ central con- 
sensation, no tail (8 to 9™). 
2 Sept., Pickering (Harvard) described the 
comet as bright without noticeable condensation. 
Millosewich (Rome) 
reported several bright points rather than a single nucleus. 
Observed at 
Tashkent, Hamburg, Kiev, Harrow and Berlin. 
All observers reported 
the absence of a nucleus, 
the diffuse appearance of the comet and the dif- 
ficulty of the measurements, 
6 Sept., observers in Washington measured 
the nebulous mass without nucleus or tail; external diameter estimated at 
3'.9. 
9 Sept. and 10 Sept., visible between clouds as a faint white spot 1'.2 
in diameter. 
12 Sept., Engelhardt (Dresden) described the comet as not a faint object, 
yet diffuse and without condensation. 
Numerous bright dots contributed 
to the granular appearance of the comet. Slightly elongated in the direction 
opposite to that of motion; D=2', 
Well visible through a 5-inch seeker (9 
to 10™). 
15 Sept., in moonlight, D=2'.5; elongated outline. 
13 Sept., 
Tupman (Harrow) gave D=3!'. 
Tempel (Arcetri) observed the comet pass 
near NGC 5772 (10™.7 according to Holetschek); 
he described the comet as 
apparently of class II (9 to 10™) but very diffuse, 
of irregular shape and 
without condensation. 
Measured also at Greenwich with a 6.7-inch refractor. 
16 Sept., Young (Princeton) saw the comet as a very diffuse object without 
traces of condensation; observations in moonlight very difficult. 
Measured 
last on 5 Oct. by Becker (Dun Echt) on a 15-inch refractor, when very faint 
in appearance, D=3! (11 to 12™?),

The fourth recorded apparition 
of the short-period 
Comet Tuttle. 
Discovered according to Rahts' ephemeris by Perrotin and 
Charlois (Nice) on 8 Aug. in spite of unfavorable position in the morning 
sky.

in Cancer; 
in the middle of 
September, 
in Hydra. 
Upon discovery, very faint object appearing as a white spot 2' in diameter; 
no central condensation.

morning twilight; observations possible only for 10 to 15 minutes. 
10 Aug., 
nebula elongated along the meridian (9 to 10™). 
Swift reported observing 
the comet on 13 Aug. on a black background as a very bright object with 
condensation.

(Charlottesville, U.S.A.) on 15 Sept. and 16 Sept. (10 to 11™),

1885 V (1885h). 
Discovered by Brooks (Phelps, New York) on the 
evening of 26 Dec. and independently by Barnard (Nashville) on the evening 
of 27 Dec. 
Located in Aquila, moving north-east. 
In the first half of 
January 1886, passed through Delphinus; 
at the end of January and in 
February and March moved through Pegasus. 
27 Dec., Barnard described the comet as almost bright, circular, con- 
densed, with no tail (8 to 7™). 
28 Dec., Harvard observers estimated 
D = 3'; intrinsic brightness 9™; nucleus 9™.5; 
highly eccentric condensation. 
29 Dec., Barnard described the comet as "almost large"; very faint short 
tail extending approximately away from the Sun. 
30 Dec., less bright; 
31 Dec., fainter yet still "fairly bright"; diffuse tail pointing away from 
the Sun (9 to 8™). 
5 Jan. 
1886, faint and difficult to observe, 
no condensa- 
tion (11™?). 
30 Dec., Engelhardt (Dresden) described the comet through 
a 12-inch telescope as bright even at low altitude; easily observed through 
a 5-inch seeker; D=2'.5 (8 to 9™), Wagner (Kremsmunster) reported a 
very bright nucleus on 1 Jan. 1886. 
1 Jan. to 4 Jan., Abetti (Padua) ob- 
served the comet at 13° altitude: nebulous circular mass, 
no nucleus, 
no 
tail, 
D=1'. 
7 Feb., measured by Rayet {Bordeaux); 
1 March, measured 
last by Pechtle (Copenhagen) on a 101/2inch refractor; exceptionally 
faint (but not fainter than 11 to 12™); 25 March, Pechule did not detect the 
comet (fainter than 12™).

1886 I (1885f) (d in current publications). 
Discovered by Fabry (Paris) 
on 1 Dec. 1885 witha Coudé equatorial; faint object with an 11™ nucleus. 
Located in Pisces, moving west; from the middle of December 1885 to 
January-February 1886, moved slowly in Pegasus; 
in March, ascended 
north into Andromeda; 
in the middle of April, located near M31; at the 
end of April, passed through Triangulum and Aries, moving south; in 
May, passed through Taurus, Eridanus, Lepus and Canis Major and 
descended into Puppis; 
in June and July, kept to its southward course, 
moving through Vela. 
At the beginning of December 1885, Tempel (Arcetri) described the 
comet as easily observed with a starlike nucleus; 
small, 
faint, nebulous 
envelope corresponding to nebulae of class II and III ( 8 to 9™). 
5 Dec., 
Engelhardt (Dresden) saw the comet as very bright with a distinct starlike 
nucleus of 11™; the bright part was 15" in diameter; through a 5-inch 
seeker, 
the comet was apparent at first glance; D=2'. 
12 Dec., 
at Harvard, 
the diameter was estimated at 3'. 
In Jan. 
1886, Engelhardt described the 
comet as faint and visible with difficulty, however Bauschinger (Munich) 
at that time saw the comet as a very bright object with a large envelope 
and a nucleus of 8 to 9™(7 to 8™?). 
22 Feb., Pomerantsev (Tashkent) 
estimated the nucleus at 17.5 (6 to 7™). 
25 Feb., Bauschinger reported a 
bright circular comet with an 8™ nucleus,

3 April, Rebeur-Paschwitz saw the comet easily with the naked eye.

a symmetrical bright envelope 
and a straight tail up to 1° long.

Andromedae (47.28; B 8)

and morning twilight (3 to 4™); no tail; easily visible to the naked eye near

7 April and 12 April, 
Barnard reported the yellow color of the nucleus; 
a 5° telescopic tail; naked

tail as split into two; traces of jets from the nucleus. 
4 April, 
a 3™ nucleus 
surrounded by a dense envelope, no traces of jets. 
13 April, both the para- 
bolic envelope with the nucleus at the focus and the long tail were sharp and 
homogeneous. 
25 April, Pechule (Copenhagen) saw the comet with the naked 
eye as a starlike point with a narrow 10° tail. 
1 May, Eddie (Cape) saw the comet first as a very conspicuous object 
with a 15' head and a tail up to 9° long and 11/2° wide. 
Straw-colored comet; 
straight, sharply outlined tail. 
Telescopes showed a well-condensed nucleus 
surrounded by a wide coma with sunward jets curving back. 
Eccentric 
nucleus in the coma; 
the hair-like (jet-like) structure of the coma was clear. 
4 May, slightly curving 6° tail appeared; reddish-brown nucleus surrounded 
by a pale-yellow hyperbolic coma. 
5 May, tail 5° long, very faint; bright 
and sharp nucleus; faint trace of a coma preceding the nucleus and forming 
two or three parabolic envelopes. 
7 May, a 2° tail visible inhazy atmosphere. 
* In the sense ''comet minus nebula." 
** 
These data, and the comparison thereof with Miiller's results, point to 
a phenomenon familiar to all 
observers of the brightness of nebulous formations, 
Estimates made with the naked eye agree very 
closely with exact photometric data obtained by comparison with 
a homogeneous object. 
High mag- 
nifications, however, introduce a systematic error which lowers the brightness estimates by 1™.5 to 2™,5, 
since only the brightness of the central, i.e. brighter, region of the comet is measured; the same instru- 
ment with different magnification will yield systematically lower results.

===PAGE_0097 (book p.279)===
11 May, still visible to the naked eye in bright moonlight; through a tele-

pale-yellow coma. 
12 May, appeared 
to the naked eye as a dull spot; 
14 May, invisible to the naked eye in bright 
moonlight (5 to 6™), 
At the beginning of May, measured on sextants from ships.

comet appeared very faint near a 10.5 star (10™?).

m=11™" on 30 July, we obtain Hjg=5™.1 an independent indication of the 
validity of Muller's measurements and of the adopted law for brightness 
variation (y=10).

1886 II (1885g) (1885e in current publications). 
Discovered by Barnard 
(Nashville) at about 9 A.M. on 3 Dec. 1885 as a faint object with insignifi- 
cant intensification near the center (9 to 10™). 
Located in Taurus, moving 
north-west; 
in January 1886, passed through the northern part of Cetus; 
in February, located in Aries; 
in March andApril, passed through Triangulum, 
moving to Andromeda; 
in May came back through Triangulum and Aries, 
traversing Eridanus and moving rapidly south; in June, passed through 
Columba and Puppis; 
in July, moved in Vela. 
5 Dec., Engelhardt (Dresden) described the comet as almost bright but 
fainter than Comet 1886 I; D=0'.5; 
a condensation, 
but no nucleus (9 to 10™). 
6 Dec., Lohse (Scarborough, England) saw it as a small and faint object 
through a 15.5-inch telescope; coma elongated away from the direction of 
279 

===PAGE_0098 (book p.280)===
motion; D=1 to 11.2; granular central part. 
7 Dec., Barnard saw a more 
definite short tail than on 3 Dec. and 5 Dec. 
8 Dec., Lamp (Bothkamp) 
described the comet as very bright and more clearly visible than Comet 
Fabry. 
10 Dec., Tempel (Arcetri) described Comet Barnard as brighter 
than 1886 I and equal to a class IJ nebula with a starlet at the center. 
9 Dec., 
Weinek (Prague) reported elongation, estimating the cometary nucleus at 11™. 
12 Dec., brighter than on 5 Dec. and 7 Dec. 
Engelhardt described the 
nuclear condensation as nearer the front edge of the cometary envelope 
(8 to 9™), 
11 Dec. to 15 Dec., both comets resembled 11™ stars surrounded 
by rarefied nebulae with suggestions of tails. 
22 Dec., well visible; 
Bauschinger (Munich) saw a sharp 9™ nucleus. 
22 Dec., Engelhardt des- 
cribed the comet as very bright with traces of a fan-shaped tail (7.5 to 8™,5). 
26 Dec.

smaller in size.

the beginning of March 1886.

the comet through a 5-inch refractor as a faint dull nebula, with an inter- 
mittently scintillating 9 to 10™nucleus.

no nucleus.

a faint jae

1886 III (1886b). 
Discovered by Brooks (Phelps, New York) on 30 April 
(1 May in the morning according to civil calendar) between 
and f Pegasi 
a bright object with a tail over 
1° long and a very faint nucleus (4 to bey, 
Moved north, passing through Andromeda and Cassiopeia in May; at the 
beginning of July, located near y Camelopardi. 
2 May, Lamp (Kiel) described the comet as very bright and well visible 
on bright background; magnificent tail, clear yellowish nucleus (4™), 
281 

===PAGE_0100 (book p.282)===
3 May, fainter appearance, 
dull white color; invisible on bright field. 4 May, 
clear yellowish nucleus visible on bright background, 
even though the trans- 
parency was lower than on 3 May. 
2 May, Stephan (Marseilles) reported a 
brilliant 7 to 8™ nucleus and a 10 to 12! tail (4 to 5™); 5 May and 6 May, 
comet fainter. 
Engelhardt (Dresden) described the comet as very bright 
and visible on bright field (4 to 5™); a bright linear protuberance extended 
from the disk-shaped nucleus over 2!' in the direction of the tail; an 8! tail, 
very bright, narrow near the head, growing wider and curving convexly to 
the north, 
Ata distance of 6' from the nucleus, 
the tail split into a main 
tail and a fainter secondary tail extending to the south. 
6 May, tail brighter 
than on 3 May; through a seeker, 
tail 36' long and much wider; 
at a distance 
of 8', a faint jet to the south. 
On a bright field the disk-shaped nucleus 
appeared to be surrounded by a gossamer, 
small, nebulous envelope. 
3 May, Pechule (Copenhagen), using a 10 1/2-inch refractor, 
saw a wide 
nebulous band extending over 12'' from one very faint nucleus to a second 
somewhat diffuse nucleus from which, 
in turn, 
a fan-shaped 10' tail ex- 
tendedcurving south. 
3 May and 4 May, Kammermann (Geneva) compared 
the very bright tail to 
a Turkish saber. 
7 May, observers in Vienna reported 
a bright telescopic 
tail 
extending from the nucleus at a 10° angle. After 
full moon (20 May) the comet changed radically in appearance. 
20 May, 
Tempel (Arcetri) saw the comet as a fan-shaped nebula 12' long and 11/2! 
wide, with a head like a nebulous spot (7 to 8™). 
21 May, Knorre (Berlin) 
saw ''a faint wisp'' 6 to 10' long, the moonlight preventing observation of 
any better image. 
Sharp decrease in brightness reported by Celoria (Milan); 
21 May to 24 May, fan-shaped mass with no traces of a nucleus, 
difficult to 
measure; same description furnished by Tempel. 
22 May, Weinek (Prague) 
saw the comet through a 31/2-inch telescope; "perturbed" by the light of an 
8™ star (7 to 8™). 
26 May, 
still fainter; 
3 June, Celoria described the comet 
as a hardly noticeable shapeless object not suitable for measurements.

1886 IV (1886c). 
Discovered by Brooks (Phelps, U.S.A.) on the evening 
of 22 May near o Virginis; 
the third comet discovered by Brooks within

passing through Virgo; at the beginning of July, located below Spica.

class I-II (8 to 9™); D=2', considerable condensation. 
26 May, observed 
with difficulty near a Virginis (9™).

scintillating luminous points (8 to 9™).

as a faint object 2' in diameter; 28 May, very faint and hardly visible.

telescope (10 to 11™).,

1886 V (1886a). 
Discovered by Brooks (Phelps) on the evening of 27 April 
above B Cassiopeiae as a quite bright circular luminous mass 1' in diameter 
with a scintillating nucleus. 
Moved through Cassiopeia; passed through 
Perseus in May, approaching conjunction with the Sun. 
After conjunction, 
at the beginning of July, located between Hydra and Monoceros, moving 
east along Hydra. 
At the end of April, observed in Rome, Kiel and Hamburg; described as 
sufficiently bright (8™). 
30 April, Wendell (Harvard) estimated D=2!'; 
m=10™; 
faint central condensation. 
1 May, faint due to low altitude and 
clouds. 
3 May, Engelhardt (Dresden) described the comet as a very bright 
and circular object; D=2'; eccentric condensation situated nearer the 
southern (foremost) part (8™). 
6 May, Tupman (Harrow) saw a circular 
comet, D=2', m=8™, central condensation, 
no nucleus. 
2 May to 5 May, 
observed at Ann Arbor on a meridian circle (not fainter than 7 to 8™). 
9 May, Engelhardt saw the comet as almost bright; 
a granular nucleus with 
numerous light points. 
11 May to 24 May, at lower culmination, observed on a meridian circle 
by Romberg (Pulkovo) (6 to 7™). 
18 May to 19 May, Bauschinger (Munich) 
estimated the comet as a very bright object with an 8™ clear nucleus (7 to 
6™), 
21 May to 25 May, more diffuse nucleus of 6 to 7™, appearing almost 
as a disk (5™); circular coma 2'.3 in diameter. 
Luther estimated the nuclear brightness: 
3 May, 11™; 6 May, 11™; 
20 May, 7™ 
28 May, Palisa and Oppenheim (Vienna) described the comet 
as well visible in spite of strong twilight. 
30 May, Tupman estimated the 
comet at 5 or 6™ near the horizon. 
That the comet was seen at various 
observatories is an indication of its substantial brightness. 
After conjunc- 
tion, spotted and observed from 3 July to 21 July by Tebbutt (Windsor) on 
a 41/2-inch refractor; 
3 July, 
D=11/2', comet appeared as faint condensa- 
tion (8™). 
5 July to 30 July, measured by Finlay (Cape) ona 7-inch refractor; 
5 July, bright circular mass 3/4' in diameter, insignificant central conden- 
sation (8 to 9™); 14 July, very faint in bright moonlight; 20 July, diffuse 
mass, no condensation; 24 July, very diffuse luminous spot (9 to 10™); 
30 July, very faint (10 to 11™).

1886 VIII (1887c). 
Discovered by Barnard on the morning of 23 Jan. 1887 
between Lyra, Hercules and Vulpecula. 
Upon discovery, circular object 
with 
central 
condensation, 
no 
tail, 
m=10™, 
1' in diameter. Moved north- 
east; 
in February passed through Cygnus; 
at the end of March and the begin-

at the end of April and in May, moved 
along the parallel in Cassiopeia. 
24 Jan., Wendell (Harvard) estimated the comet at 9™5; D=1!', faint 
central condensation. 
Rapidly grew faint. 
17 Feb. and 18 Feb., Luther 
(Hamburg) reported observations to be difficult with a 9-inch telescope; 
comet hardly visible (10 to 11™).

the comet with a 10-inch refractor; faint in appearance because of haze.

117.5). Measured last by Plummer on 20 May.

circular, 
small and faint; visible through a 5-inch seeker (10 to 11™). 
Seen from numerous observatories because of favorable celestial position. 
Last measurement made by Palisa (Vienna) on 22 May.

1886 IX (1886f). 
Discovered in Sextans by Barnard (Nashville) on the 
morning of 4 Oct. and independently on 5 Oct. by Hartwig (Bamberg) and 
Pechiile (Copenhagen); 
a bright (8™) 
circular object with sharp condensa- 
tion. 
Moved east; in October, passed through the southern part of Leo; 
in November, traversed Virgo, Bootes and Serpens; 
in December, moved 
through Hercules, Sagittarius and Aquila, approaching conjunction with the 
Sun. 
At the end of April, after the conjunction, observed near Fomalhaut; 
in May, located in Sculptor and Grus. 
Barnard spotted traces of a tail on the first days of observations. 
7 Oct., 
Oppenheim (Vienna) deseribed the comet as very bright. Observers in 
Prague saw the bright comet on that night as an almost circular object with 
strong condensation, brighter than 9™ (through haze and clouds); D=3!.1 
(7 to 8™). 
7 Oct., Riccd (Palermo) reported a trace of a tail extending 
westward. 
Disappeared in twilight 41 minutes before sunrise, when 8™ 
stars, but no 9™ stars, were visible (7 to 8™). 
8 Oct. and 9 Oct., spectrum 
showed the three ordinary bands, of which the central one was the longest 
and strongest; the nucleus gave a continuous spectrum with intensifications 
at the positions corresponding to the bands. 
In the middle of October, 
moonlight and twilight interfered with observations. 
2yOcts; 
circular 
nebula 5' in diameter; very faint tail extending 8' to the west; sharp nucleus. 
1 Nov., yellow spectral band weaker than the blue one (cf. the spectrum of 
8 Oct. and 9 Oct.). 
3 Nov., 
20! tail; 
9 Nov., 
28' tail, head 10' in diameter, 
eccentric nucleus, 
the yellow spectral band weaker than the blue one.

observations of 25 Dec.

Two main tails 
with a 50° angle between them. 
The southern tail was better outlined; the

===PAGE_0105 (book p.287)===
northern tail was stronger, consisting of three long bright rays enveloped 
in faint nebulosity.

ones, 
9 to 6'. On the next day, 28 Nov., photographs registered consider- 
able changes. 
The southern tail was much shorter, appearing as an irregu- 
lar nebula 15' long. 
The northern tail was excellently developed, 
consisting 
of two main rays diverging at a 10° angle; sharp outlines. 
The photographs 
also revealed two faint rays, eastern and western, 
and many other features 
invisible to the naked eye.

diffuse, 
slightly elongated nucleus with a faint envelope.

1887 1(1887a). 
Large southern comet. 
Spotted first on the evening of 
18 Jan. by a farmer (Blauwberg, near Cape Town) and Thome (Cordoba). 
287 

===PAGE_0106 (book p.288)===
A bright straight, narrow, 
silver-white tail over 40° long and 15' wide; 
no trace of a nucleus or a condensation (1 to 2™). 
The beginning of the tail 
was near the horizon at Microscopium; 
comet moved south; 24 Jan., located 
neara Gruis; 29 May, neart Pheonicis. 
21 Jan., Thome described the apparition as similar to 1880 I; a 25° tail 
inclined at 10° to the vertical; 
no nucleus (2 to 3™), head 5° above the 
horizon. 
22 Jan., 40° tail, brighter (2™); 24 Jan., 41° tail. 
25 Jan., 
comet rapidly grew faint; the beginning of the tail difficult to register. 
18 Jan., first observed in South Africa; 
on the evening of 22 Jan., first 
observed by Finlay (Cape); tail appeared as a faint narrow luminous ribbon, 
quite rectilinear and of almost uniform brightness throughout. 
No head, no 
condensation; 
the tail simply disappeared in telescopic field of vision; had 
already become invisible to the naked eye; position determined on equatorial 
only by taking circle readings. 
27, 28 and 29 Jan., small curvature of tail; 
tail not brighter than the Small Magellanic Cloud. 
In Australia, Todd (Adelaide) mapped the positions of the comet. 
20 Jan., 
observations with the naked eye and through binoculars showed the comet 
to be initially as bright as the Magellanic Clouds; rapidly grew faint. 
27 Jan., Todd saw the head —a very diffuse nebulous mass —as if cut by 
a wide crevass or discontinuity. Away and above the tail [sic] there was 
another narrow discontinuity in the tail. 
28 Jan., Tebbutt detected neither 
nucleus nor condensation; 
tail extended beyondaEridani. 
30 Jan., 
tail 
visible, but exceptionally faint; the lower end, neark Phoenicis, extended 
to qi-q2 Eridani (4 to 5™). 
1 Feb., no traces of the comet were detected, 
apparently due to the moonlight.

1887 IV (1887e). 
The third comet discovered by Barnard within a few 
months of 1887. 
Spotted on the evening of 12 May between Centaurus, Hydra 
and Scorpio; 
moved north-east through Libra (May) and Ophiuchus (June, 
July and August). 
Faint upon discovery. 
14 May, Lamp (Kiel) described the comet as faint 
and circular with a nucleus (9 to 10™).

starlike nucleus (9™).

central condensation.

motion; faint tail with elongated condensation.

eter, elongated east-west.

1888 I (1888a). 
Bright comet. 
Discovered by Sawerthal (Cape) on18 Feb. 
(morning of 19 Feb. according to civil reckoning) with the naked eye (3™) 
and independently by Nolau (Brankholm, Victoria) on 19 Feb. 
Located in 
Telescopium; moved to the northern hemisphere through Microscopium 
(beginning of March), Capricornus (March) and Aquarius (end of March); 
in April, located in Pegasus; in May and June, in Andromeda; 
in July, 
August and September, 
in Cassiopeia. 
Morning of 25 Feb., Eddie (Cape) reported a highly condensed nucleus 
of 4" with a curving tail 8° long; particularly bright up to the first 3° of its 
length, where it was 
30' wide. 
Through a 91/2-inch refractor the head 
had a deep golden color; 
a brilliant point nucleus; 
a small coma; 
two 
jets extending from the head. 
28 Feb., due to moonlight, hardly visible to 
the naked eye; the structure of the cometary envelopes could be observed. 
4 March, in spite of moonlight, 
a magnificent object of 3™.5, with a tail 
visible to the naked eye for 3° of its length. 
Through a reflector, the tail 
appeared reddish in color, and the nucleus greenish-yellow. 
A double jet 
curved from the apex to the tail. 
The bright tail behind the nucleus increased 
in brightness along the axis.

through a 9-inch telescope;

of the tail was visible.

In May, comet underwent sharp brightness fluctuations and changes in 
external appearance reported first by Blumbach (Dorpat).

and 22 May, the comet increased in brightness by 2 to 3™.

and Crater;

1888 IV (1888d). 
The seventh recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Faye. Discovered in Taurus on 9 Aug. by Perrotin (Nice) using 
Kreutz's ephemeris based on Moller's elements. 
Moved east, descending 
south; 
in October passed through the lower part of Gemini; 
at the begin- 
ning of November, not far from Procyon; 
in December, passed through 
Hydra; returning, passed near Procyon in January, February and March.

condensation.

1889 I (1888e). 
Discovered on 2 Sept. 1888 between Gemini and Monoceros 
by Barnard (Lick Obs.) with a 4-inch comet seeker; rather large circular 
object, D=1', an 11™ nucleus, 
no tail. 
Discovered independently by Brooks 
on 3 Sept.; not very faint with central condensation. 
Moved slowly south- 
west; passed to Orion at the end of October; then acquired rapid westward 
motion through Orion and passed between Taurus and Eridanus; 
at the end 
of November and in December 1888 located in Cetus. 
In January 1889, in 
Pisces and Aquarius; 
in March and May, 
in Pisces; 
in June, moved west 
between Pegasus and Aquarius; 
in July and August, passed through Aquila; 
in September and at the beginning of October, located in Scutum; 
in October 
and December moved slowly south roughly alongside the Milky Way. 
At 
the beginning of 1890, located in Scutum, moving north (February, March, 
May); then passed through Serpens; 
in July and August, 
in Ophiuchus. At 
the beginning and in the middle of 1891, located in Ophiuchus. Observed 
in 
four oppositions, 
a total of 971 days. 
In the initial period, 
the comet increased in brightness. 
13 Sept., 
Engelhardt (Dresden), using a 12-inch refractor, 
reported a bright elon- 
gated comet with granular condensation and double nucleus; 
easily observed 
through a 5-inch seeker (8 to 9™).

granular condensation; under:magnification, 
a 10™.5 nucleus, D=3! (8™).

nucleus at 10™.

at 9™, D=6'; white, bright comet (7 to 8™). 10Nov., Mattiessen (Karlsruhe), 
using a 6-inch refractor reported a very bright comet, with a 9 to 9™,5 
nucleus (7 to 8™).

tail;

at P=58°. According to Abetti (Padua), the nucleus increased in brightness 
from 9 to 7" between 30 Oct. and 14 Dec.; tail 20' long; in full Moon, 
comet appeared as an 8™ star out of focus.

1889 IV (1889e). 
Discovered on 19 July in the southern sky by Davidson 
(Australia) with the naked eye. A bright 5™ nucleus and an extended (D =5') 
nebula, but no tail (3 to 4™). 
Located in Centaurus, moving north-east. 
At 
the end of July, located in Virgo; 
in August, passed through Virgo, moving 
between Bootes and Serpens head, entering Hercules; 
in September and 
October, traversed Hercules; 
in November, located near Vega. 
Beginning 22 July, observed by Ellery and Baracchi (Melbourne); on that 
day, a sharp starlike nucleus of 5 to 6™ and a tail extending 30' south-east; 
D=4 to 5'; easily visible to the naked eye; 25 July, same appearance. 
30 July, diffuse nucleus, not brighter than 6™, coma fainter, 
30! tail; visible 
to the naked eye (4 to 5™). 23 July and 24 July, Tebbutt (Windsor) described 
the comet as a conspicuous object of 4™; small nucleus, not brilliant in 
appearance, surrounded by a bright coma. 
25 July, Finlay (Cape) saw an 
8™ nucleus, 
faint nebulous tail; 
14 Aug., nucleus 9™,5, 
In Europe (Italy) visible beginning 25 July. 
Riccd (Palermo), using a 
10-inch refractor reported 25 July, point nucleus of 6™.9, elongated, bright 
and diffuse coma; fainter on 26 July; nucleus had a line spectrum with traces 
of the hydrocarbon bands, 
29 July, intrinsic brightness exceeded that of the 
comparison star (6™.7); nucleus fainter than the star and less sharp; traces 
of ordinary hydrocarbon bands. 
30 Aug., faint, diffuse tail pointing east- 
south-east; comet could be seen with difficulty by the naked eye; well visible 
through binoculars (6™). 
31 July, a 24' tail, continuous spectrum of the 
nucleus, traces of the ordinary bands; visible to the naked eye. 
3 Aug., 
clearly visible near the Moon; double nucleus observed; comet appeared 
as a large faint mass. 
4 Aug., nucleus split in the direction 
P =96°; nucleus 
fainter than the 6™.5 comparison star. 
7 Aug., nucleus fainter than 8™; 
9 Aug., an exactly double nucleus; 
9 Aug., faint double nucleus, eastern 
component relatively large. 
10 Aug., elongated nucleus; spectrum of the 
nucleus appeared as a line, one long band and traces of others were visible. 
11 Aug., elongated nucleus; components 9™ and 10™.

1889 V (1889d). 
The first recorded apparition 
of the short-period 
Comet Brooks (2). Discovered by Brooks (New York) on 6 July in Aquarius 
as a faint object (11™), 
D=1', with starlike nucleus and 10' tail. 
Moved 
slowly north, remaining in Pisces in August and October; 
in November and 
300 

===PAGE_0119 (book p.301)===
December 1889 and January 1890, moved along the ecliptic in Pisces; 
in 
February and March, located in Aries; 
in November and December 1890 
and January 1891, located in Cancer. 
In July 1889, numerous observers (Engelhardt, Holetschek, 
Spitaler, 
Barnard, Backer, Bauschinger) estimated the comet at 11 to 10™; at that 
time, the tail was estimated at 8 to 10', the head 1.5 to 3' in diameter. 
In 
August, 
10 to 9™, tail up to 15' long, D=3 to 5'. 
6 Aug., granular structure 
of the nucleus observed in Strasbourg; 28 Aug., jets in the direction P = 240° 
1 Aug., Barnard spotted two cometary satellites, B and C, located at 
65" and 265" from the main comet, A; four other nebulous objects were 
discovered later, but the most stable were the satellites 
B and C. 
The first 
appeared as a bright object with nucleus and tail, while satellite C was 
larger in size and less well outlined. 
In the middle of August, B began to 
increase in size and grew diffuse; by the end of August, B rapidly grew faint, 
increasing in size and becoming progressively more diffuse in the direction 
towards A, as if the main comet were absorbing the satellite. 
5 Nov., B 
appeared as an exceptionally faint diffuse nebula; 
fully dissipated soon after 
(possibly absorbed by the main comet). 
Satellite C was at first fainter than 
B and 5 times fainter than A; gradually increased in brightness; 
definite 
condensation observable (nucleus). 
31 Aug., 
its nucleus and tail made the 
satellite brighter than the main comet A, which decreased in brightness for 
a few days. 
In September, A and C were equal in brightness, and A was 
3 times larger than C. Beginning the middle of September, 
satellite C 
gradually decreased in brightness, becoming considerably fainter than A 
by the end of September; 
its brightness decreased at an ever increasing 
rate, until C disappeared completely as B had; 25 Nov., C invisible through 
a 36-inch refractor. 
Satellites were observed at various observatories and 
measured with particular care by Rentz (Pulkovo) on a 15-inch refractor, 
Spitaler (Vienna) on a 27-inch refractor, and also at Strasbourg and 
Cambridge. 
24 Oct., the second satellite was bright with a 12™ nucleus and 
an elongated 21/2' coma. 
Satellites were also observed through medium- 
power instruments, 
e.g., by Kortazzi (Nikolaev), Abetti (Padua), 
Bauschinger (Munich) and others. 
Comet estimated at 8 to 10™in September; 
10 to 12™ in October, when 
considerable changes in the head were observed; 
16 Oct., granular nucleus 
observed at Harvard, D=1'.5. 
19 Oct., 
at Strasbourg, comet appeared 
bright with a 10 to 11™ nucleus and a fan-shaped jet to the north-east; 
at 
that time Plummer reported brightness variation in satellite and its approach 
to the main comet. 
In November, 
fainter than 10 to 11”; in December, 
11 
to 12™; 
13 Dec., Wendell gave D=1', faint tail 2' long; 
16 Dec., nebula 
2'.5 in diameter. 
20 Dec., 
at Strasbourg, 
nucleus estimated at 12™; jets 
1/2 to 1' long to the north-east. 
In January 1890, 
11™; in February, 
12 to 
13™; 
in March, not below 13 to 14™; in April, not fainter than 13™. 
7 April 
1890, Rentz (Pulkovo) observed the comet as a faint nebulous mass with 
a starlet (13™). 
After conjunction, discovered at the second opposition by Barnard (Lick 
Obs.) with a 36-inch refractor; 
the comet was seen with much difficulty as 
a small diffuse nebula. 
Position estimated by Barnard last on 13 Jan. 
1891; 
he described the comet as the faintest and the most difficult object he had 
ever encountered. 
12 Dec. 1890, observed by Kobold (Strasbourg) through 
an 18-inch refractor; impossible to measure.

1890 II (1890a). 
Discovered by Brooks (New York) on 19 March before 
dawn in Equuleus as a bright telescopic object with starlike nucleus and 
short, wide tail (7 to 8™). Moved north, passing through Cygnus in May; 
in June, traversed Draco, attaining +66° declination; 
in July, located in 
the dipper of Ursa Major; in August, slowly descended south in Canes 
Venatici; 
in November and December 1890, located in Coma Berenices. 
In January and February 1891, moved west to Ursa Major; March to June, 
passed slowly through Leo; October to December 1891, observed in Leo, 
passing to Cancer at the beginning of 1892. 
11 April, Luther (Hamburg) reported a 9™.5 nucleus; circular comet. 
16 May, Engelhardt (Dresden) observed the bright comet with a disk-shaped, 
granular nucleus and a short, fan-shaped tail; 
17 May, nucleus slightly 
fainter than 9™.2; 20 May, Millosewich (Rome) estimated the nucleus at 
10™, tail 3' long (8 to 9™). 
26 June, nucleus 8™; long, faint tail 5 to 6', 
extending at 
P=99°. 
Engelhardt estimated the brightness of the nucleus 
in June and July; 
15 July, tail 15' long and 12' wide with bright rays (6 to 
8™); observed in Kazan', even in moonlight. 
June to August, Kortazzi 
(Nikolaev) determined the position of the comet and the direction of the tail; 
22 June, P=230°; 
13 July, P=270°. 
Bauschinger (Munich) saw traces of 
the tail on 14 Oct., 
in spite of the comet's low altitude. 
Holetschek,

comet by comparison with stars.

1890 V (1890d). 
The fifth recorded apparition of the short-period Comet 
d'Arrest. 
Discovered in Leo by Barnard (Lick Obs.) on 6 Oct., apparently, 
independently of Leveau's ephemeris. 
Very faint; moved east; at the end 
of October and in November, located in Capricornus; 
at the end of November 
and in December, 
in Aquarius.

14 Nov., very faint, D=1!' (13™). 
9 Oct., Kobold (Strasbourg) reported elongated pale nebula with slight 
condensation through 18-inch refractor. 
10 Oct. and 11 Oct., because of 
haze, extremely difficult to observe. 
10 Oct. to 14 Oct., Millosewich 
(Rome) described the comet as a faint indefinite object, without nucleus; 
difficult to measure (11™). 
Bauschinger (Munich), using a 101/2-inch 
refractor, saw an exceptionally faint comet; Thome (Cordoba) could not 
spot the comet with an 11-inch refractor. 
Tebbutt (Windsor), using an 
8-inch refractor, reported an exceptionally faint and diffuse comet; 
12 Oct. 
and 6 Nov., he measured the comet for some time (10 to 11™). 
Comparing 
305 

===PAGE_0124 (book p.306)===
this with the 1870 apparition, 
which differed in the perihelion passage by 
only 5 days, Kreutz reported that the earlier apparition had been discovered 
considerably earlier (in July) and measured for a longer period with low- 
power telescopes. 
On the other hand, however, Barnard reported that in 
October he could see the comet easily with a 31/4-inch seeker (9 to 10™):; 
Measured last on 13 Dec. 
in Charlottesville. 
25 Feb. 1891, Barnard failed 
to detect the comet.

1890 VI (1890c). 
Discovered in Ursa Minor by Denning (Bristol) on23July 
with a 10-inch reflector. 
Appeared as a faint, circular, nebulous mass 1! 
in diameter; 
central condensation. 
Moved rapidly south; 
in August passed 
through Draco and Hercules; 
at the end of August and the beginning of 
September, located in Corona Borealis; 
in the middle of September, 
in 
Serpens; 
at the end of September in Scorpio; 
in October, observed in 
Scorpio. 
24 July, Denning observed the occultation of a 9" star; the comet re- 
mained visible, and the star appeared as if surrounded by a noticeable at- 
mosphere (8 to 9™). 
7 Aug. to 9 Aug., Schorr (Hamburg) measured the 
comet with great difficulty on a 10-inch refractor with illuminated reticle 
(10 to 11™). Aguello (Palermo) also measured the comet at the visibility 
threshold of a 10-inch refractor. 
19 Aug., Engelhardt (Dresden) with a 
12-inch refractor, observed a faint, irregularly circular comet D=1', 
granular structure (10 to 11™). 
5 Sept., fairlyfaint, small, circular object, 
noticeably granular, with a condensation; 
17 Sept., very faint (10 to 11™). 
18 Sept., faint object with jagged edges and starlike nucleus of 14™; during 
occultation of a 13.5 star, the nucleus appeared to be made up of two com- 
ponents. 
19 Sept., 
faint, 
but measured with filar micrometer (11™); 
19 Sept., Pechule (Copenhagen) with a 101/2-inch refractor reported that 
the comet appeared faint and small because of low altitude (11™). 
17 Sept., 
Luther (Hamburg) measured the comet on a 9-inch refractor, 
reporting it 
to be extremely faint. 
Last observation in the northern hemisphere on 6 Oct. 
(Lick Obs.); at southern observatories, measured for an additional month. 
1 Oct. to 7 Nov., Thome (Cordoba) described the comet as poorly visible 
on the dark field of.an 11-inch refractor; measurements effected at mini- 
mum illumination (not fainter than 11™). Tebbutt (Windsor) measured the comet 
on an 8-inch refractor with filar micrometer; 
faint and diffuse; fainter 
than Comet.d'Arrest in October.

1890 VII (1890f). 
Discovered as a faint cometary object near 1890 IV by 
Spitaler (Vienna) on 16 Nov. with a 27-inch refractor. 
Because of poor 
weather and an error in the telegram sent to America, the comet was not 
observed again for 3 weeks; 4 Dec., spotted by Spitaler near Ursa Major. 
In December 1890, January 1891 and February, located in Auriga, near 
Capella. 
In December, visible as a circular nebula 1/2' in diameter, with a sharp 
13™ nucleus (11 to 12™). 
6 Dec., measured by Pechule (Copenhagen) as a 
very faint and small object (not fainter than 11 to 12™), 
7 Dec., Engelhardt 
reported the comet to be hardly visible between faint stars, occasionally 
disappearing completely (12™). 
13 Dec., Kobold (Strasbourg) with an 18-inch 
refractor observed the comet near a 13™ star; 
its central parts were slightly 
fainter than the star (11 to 12™). 
30 Dec., pale nebulous spot difficult to 
observe (12 to 13™); 3 Jan. 1891, even fainter. 
29 Dec., Spitaler easily 
spotted the circular comet; small brilliant nucleus, D=1/2' (12™). 
Observed 
also at Algiers, Nice and Takubaya. 
12 Jan. 1891, measured by Barnard 
(Lick Obs.); measured last on 4 Feb. 1891 by Spitaler (Vienna) (12 to 13™ 
on both occasions).

1891 1(1891b). 
Discovered by Barnard (Lick Obs.) on 29 March in 
Andromeda and independently by Denning (Bristol) on 30 March. 
Bright 
comet nearly 1' in diameter with a 30' tail and a 10™ nucleus (8 to 9™). 
Moved south-east, approaching conjunction with the Sun; observed last in 
the northern sky in Triangulum on 10 April in evening twilight. 
Beginning 
19 May, observed in the southern sky in Eridanus; 
at the beginning of June, 
passed to Columba; 
in the middle of June, traversed Puppis; at the end of 
June and in July, moved in Vela. 
31 March, Engelhardt (Dresden) described the comet as a bright circular 
object without nucleus D=1'; visible at first glance through a comet-seeker 
(8 to 9™). 
1 April, well visible in early twilight; D=4'; Spitaler (Vienna) 
reported pale tail extending over 5' to the north-north-east; 
diffuse large 
nucleus of 10™; intrinsic brightness 8™. On that day, Holetschek estimated 
the nuclear brightness through a seeker as >8™; the brighter part of the 
coma also visible. 
3 April, Luther (Hamburg) described the comet as 
bright with a 9™.5 nucleus and a circular coma; 10 April, measured in twi- 
light without field illumination (7 to 6™). 
4 April, Pechule (Copenhagen) 
gave D=1'; 9 to 10™ condensation; 
faint, straight tail 6 to 8' long at 
P=12°. 
6 April, Bauschinger (Munich) observed the comet as a very bright, large 
object with diffuse nucleus (7 to 8™). 
13 April, observed by Rentz (Pulkovo) 
in twilight (6 to 7™). 
After conjunction, observed beginning 19 May by Tebbutt (Windsor); 
4 June to 13 June measured on a 41/2-inch refractor (8 to 9™); 14 June, 
exceptionally faint when observed through an 8-inch refractor (9 to 10™). 
At the end of June and on 2 July and 3 July, observed as a faint luminous 
spot (10 to 11™); 6 July, mere suggestion of a comet occasionally visible 
(11™), 
9 June, Finlay (Cape) observed the comet as a faint diffuse mass

moonlight.

moonlight and difficult to measure on a filar micrometer. 
Gradually grew 
faint, disappearing under the slightest illumination of reticle (11 to 12™).

1891 II (1891c). 
The second recorded apparition 
of the short- period 
Comet Wolf (1). Spotted on 1 May according to Thraen's ephemeris by Spitaler 
(Vienna) with a 27-inch refractor as a very small (D = 15") and pale object 
with a nucleus.

refractor (12 to 14™).

passed through Pisces;

at the 
beginning of September, 
in Taurus; 
in September, descended south along 
Orion; 
in November and December, located in Eridanus.

celestial regions it had covered in November; 
in March, 
slowly moved in 
Orion.

1892 III (1892h). 
The first recorded apparition 
of the short-period 
Comet Holmes. 
Discovered by Holmes (London) on 6 Nov. near the 
Andromeda Nebula as a bright circular mass 5! in diameter, with a central 
condensation but no nucleus. 
8 Nov., discovered independently by Anderson 
(Edinburgh); 
9 Nov., by Davidson (Mackay, Queensland). 
Moved slowly 
south-east; 
at the end of December, near fp Andromedae; 
in January and 
February, located in Triangulum; 
in March and April, located in Perseus. 
Head grew larger: 30' indiameter atthe endof November. A faint, short tail 
was observed in the first days; Barnard's photographs showed a 30! tail. 
An isolated diffuse nebulous mass was observed 1° from the head. 
21 Nov., 
Deslandres noticed a split nucleus on a photograph; 
this phenomenon was 
not noted by him on any further occasion. 
At the end of November, visible to the naked eye as a faint, small nebula 
(5 to 6™). At the beginning of December, 
easily observed with medium- 
power telescopes. 
Rapidly grew faint; at the beginning of January, 
difficult 
to measure even on large refractors. 
Apparently the surface brightness of 
the head decreased considerably, though the intrinsic brightness as deter- 
mined by high light-power instruments did not change strongly. 
A remark- 
able flare, i.e., an increase in brightness of at least 4 to 5™, occurred 
between 14 Jan. and 16 Jan. 1893. 
16 Jan., nucleus 7 to 8™; comet visible 
to the naked eye as a faint spot (5 to 6™). Gradually grew faint; in February, 
comet appeared as in December 1892; from the beginning of March, acces- 
sible to large instruments only. Kobold (Strasbourg) saw the comet on 
6 April and on the following nights, but it was difficult to measure. 
Upon discovery, various observers (in particular Weiss, Christie and 
Denza) described the comet as very bright. 
Copeland (Dun Echt) described 
the comet as considerably brighter than M31, but smaller (4 to 5™). 
9 Nov., 
Hayn (Leipzig) saw a fan-shaped jet at P= 120° in the circular (D=5') nebula. 
10 Nov. 
to 12 Nov., Millosewich (Rome) gave D=6'.4. 
13 Nov., Schorr 
(Hamburg) and Moller (Bothkamp) estimated D=10'; tail pointing opposite 
to the diurnal motion. 
15 Nov., Kammermann (Geneva) saw the comet with 
the naked eye (not fainter than M31); 24 Nov., Gruss (Prague) gave 
D=20'. 
On that day, F. Rentz (Pulkovo) reported D=30'; visible to the naked eye 
(3 to 4™), 
25 Nov., tail extended east-south-east; 
seen by Updengraf 
(Columbia) with the naked eye. 
26 Nov., Engelhardt (Dresden) gave D =15', 
reporting the Andromeda Nebula to be much brighter, smaller and much 
more condensed than the comet. 
8 Nov. and 9 Nov., Campbell observed a remarkable cometary spectrum: 
continuous in nucleus, coma and the portions of the tail both within and outside

Discovered by Barnard (Lick Obs.) on 12 Oct. near 
Altair. 
The first comet to be discovered on a photographic plate (excluding 
the "eclipse comet"! observed on 15 May 1882), 
Photograph showed traces 
of a tail. Moved south-east; 
at the end of October, located below Delphinus; 
in November and December, traversed in Aquarius.

1892 VI (1892e) (1892d in B.A.). 
Discovered by Brooks (Geneva, U.S.A.) 
on 28 Aug. in Auriga; very bright circular nebula with clear nucleus and 
short, faint tail. 
Moved south-east; 
in September, crossed Gemini; 
in 
October, passed through Cancer; 
in November, traversed Sextans; 
in 
December, crossed Corvus, Hydra and Centaurus. 
After conjunction with 
the Sun (February 1893), moved in the southern sky in Scorpio; 
in March, 
April and May, observed in Saggittarius; 
in June and July, located in 
Ophiuchus and then observed again at northern observatories.

1893 II (1893a, c*¥*). 
The first report on 
the discovery of the comet was received at the 
central clearing office from Quenisset (Paris), 
who discovered the comet on 9 July; Rordame 
(Boston) discovered the comet one day earlier. 
Later, 
it was learned that the comet had been 
spotted by Johnson and Miller (Akta, Iowa) on 
8 July; 4 July, discovered by Roso de Luna 
(Logrosan, Estramadura); Sperra (Randolf, 
U.S.A.) observed the comet from 19 June, 
taking it for the periodic Comet Finlay. Upon 
discovery, 
visible to the naked eye as a con- 
Spicuous object; 
in June, located in Aries and 
Taurus; at the beginning of July, located in 
Camelopardus, moving through Lynx, Leo 
Minor and Leo Major; in August, located in 
Virgo, prior to conjunction; 
in the middle of 
November and in December, observations 
resumed with the comet again located in Virgo. 
At the beginning of July the comet attained 
considerable brightness near the perihelion. 
11 July, Borrelly (Marseilles) saw the comet 
with the naked eye as a 3 to 4™ star; a 3" eccen- 
tric nucleus. 
14 July, appeared tothe naked eye 
as a 4™ star; a straight tail, 8 to 10° long 
through binoculars, 
25' long through telescope. 
15 Aug., tail visible to the naked eye; 25 Aug., 
comet grew fainter, but still visible to the naked 
eye. 
29 July, invisible to the naked eye; tele- 
scopes showed a 15! tail. 9 July and 10 July, 
Ross and Wendell gave m = 3™.5; tail 10° long.

1893 III (1893b) (1893a in M.N.). 
The second recorded apparition 
of the 
short-period Comet Finlay. 
Discovered by Finlay (Cape) on 17 May. Very 
faint in appearance, D=1', m=11™, no tail. 
Observed between Aquarius 
and Pisces, moving south-east along the ecliptic; 
in May and June, observed 
between Cetus and Pisces; 
at the end of June, located in Aries; 
in July and 
August, in Taurus.

when viewed through a 7-inch refractor (8 to 10™); circular nebula with an 
11” condensation. 
24 June, much brighter; withstood reticle illumination 
equal to that applied to 10™.5 stars (8 to 9™), D= 1/2'; no noticeable con- 
densation. 17 July, Fabry (Marseilles), using a 26-cm refractor, described 
the comet as a faint diffuse nebula without a nucleus; D=1'.5 (10 to 11™),

1893 IV (1893d) (1893c in M.N.). Discovered in Virgo by Brooks (Geneva, 
U.S.A.) after midnight on 16 Oct. 
Bright (7™) object with diffuse 9™ nucleus, 
11/2' coma and a tail 3° long. Moved north; in October and the beginning 
of November, crossed Coma Berenices, moving to Canes Venatici; 
at the 
beginning of December, moved to Draco; in the middle of December, 
in 
Ursa Minor; 29 Dec., located 14° from the north pole, neart Draconis; 
crossed Cepheus at the beginning of January. 
17 Oct., Schorr (Hamburg) estimated the brightness at 9™ (central con- 
densation); circular comet with 
D=2'; the brighter part of the tail was 1/3° 
long. Receded from the Sun and simultaneously approached the Earth, 
so 
that initially the apparent brightness increased. 
21 Oct., maximum bright- 
ness, according to Brooks; a 31/2° tail. 
Barnard (Lick Obs.) saw a 2° tail; 
photographs showed a 10° tail. Observers reported nonhomogeneity of the 
tail, also revealed by photographs; considerable changes in features and 
direction of tail.

diffuse 
nucleus of 97.4, D=54", tail 15' long.

tail 
only 8' long.

1894 I (1894a). Discovered by Denning (Bristol) on 26 March in Leo 
Minor as a bright nebula of 10™, 
1' in diameter, with a starlike nucleus of 
11 to 12™. 
Short faint tail 2' long and 1' wide extending from a fan-shaped 
coma.

moved to Virgo; at the beginning of June, located near y Virginis. 
Dis- 
covered 50 days after the perihelion passage; rapidly grew faint, receding 
from both the Earth and the Sun,

as a faint object with a tail extending south; granular head (9 to 10™).

structure still obvious.

observation period indicate that the magnification, backgroundbrightness, alti-

1894 II (1894b). 
Discovered on 1 April near yn Horologiiby amateur astro- 
nomer Gale (Paddington, 
Sydney) with a 3-inch telescope. 
Circular 
bright nebula with condensation and faint tail that was 1° long on 2 April 
(6 to 7), Passed through Dorado and Pictor; at the end of April, moved 
rapidly north through Columba and Puppis; 
at the beginning of May, moved 
to Hydra; 
10 May, located between Cancer and Leo; at the beginning of June, 
moved to Ursa Major; 
in July and August, moved in Canes Venatici. 
7 April, Gale saw the comet with the naked eye (5 to 6™); 25 April, 4™. 
12 April, narrow tail 2° long and 1' wide; the head diameter was up to 2. 
15 April, no tail; comet appeared as a circular nebula till 20 April when 
an extension evolved, developing into a 40! tail. 
Beginning 5 April, Russell 
(Sydney) photographed the comet with 1 to 2-hour exposures. 
5 April, 
D=3', 
faint double tail with two rays up to 10' long; 20 April, 
tail with rays up to 
20' long, D=6'; 24 April, tail 2° long and 2' wide, appearing to bifurcate 
at 45' from the head; 25 April, D=9', tail 3/4°; 26 April, D=10', wide 
diffuse fan-shaped tail that could be observed only photographically; 27 April, 
D=25!', single tail up to 11/4° long —through felescopes, up to 2° long; 
29 April, D=15', no tail —plates showed a faint, diffuse protuberance. 
Thus, the repeated evolution and deterioration of the tail was definitely 
observed. 
In April, observed at Windsor, 
the Cape, Melbourne, Cordoba and other 
observatories; 
in the northern hemisphere, observed from the end of April 
and the beginning of May. 
26 April, detected in Flagstaff (Arizona); from 
28 April, in Nice and Cincinnati.

4 to 5™ nebula, D=10 to 15'. Numerous photographs taken at Heidelberg, 
Paris and the Lick Obs. showed a 4 to 6° tail with a multitude of details 
and condensations.

near @ Camelopardi.

1895 IV (1895c). 
Discovered by Perrine (Lick Obs.) on 16 Nov. in Virgo. 
Bright comet with a tail (6 to 7™). Moved south; at the beginning of December, 
passed Libra, moving toward the Sun and gaining in brightness; before the 
perihelion observed last in the morning twilight of 11 Dec. (near Scorpioni). 
20 Dec. to 25 Dec., observed in Sagittarius in evening twilight, mainly at 
the Cape; from 30 Jan. 1896 through February, visible again in the morning 
sky in Aquila; observed for almost 6 months; passed through Aquila (March, 
April) and Hercules (May, June, July); at the beginning of August, located 
between Hercules and Serpens. 
18 Nov., Lamp (Kiel) described the comet as fairly bright but invisible to 
the naked eye (6 to 7™), 
18 Nov., Halm (Edinburgh) saw the comet as a 
bright object with a 6™ nucleus and a tail pointing north; on that day, 
Millosewich (Rome) estimated the nucleus at 7™, tail 12 to 15' long at P=330°; 
observers described bright comet poorly visible in twilight. 
19 Nov., Gruss 
and Laska (Prague) observed a 5™ nucleus; comet sufficiently condensed 
with a wide tail, 
18 Nov., Benko Boinik (Pola) estimated the brightness at 
7™ (apparently central condensation); Schorr (Hamburg) on that morning 
saw an elongated

1896 II (1895b). 
The eighth recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Faye. 
Discovered according to Engstrom's ephemeris by Javelle 
(Nice) on 26 Sept. 1895. 
During the entire observation period, exceptionally 
faint (12 to 13™). 
Located in Aquarius; at the beginning of January 1896, 
moved to Pisces. 
Upon discovery, circular faint nebula with D=0!'.3 to 0'..4. Measured in 
Nice till 20 Oct. From 8 Oct. to 21 Oct. and from 12 Nov. to 18 Nov., 
measured with a 26-inch refractor in Charlottesville; D=0'.5, central 
condensation (11 to 12™).
