threshold of a 12-inch refractor (12 to 13™). Observed last by Brown

1896 V (1896e). Discovered in Serpens by Giacobini (Nice) on 4 Sept. 
as a circular, small nebula 1' in diameter (12 to 13™) with central conden- 
sation. 
Moved east; at the end of September, crossed Serpens; 
in October, 
traversed Scutum; 
at the end of October and the beginning of November, 
moved between Aquila and Sagittarius; 
at the end of November and in 
December, crossed Capricornus and Aquarius, slowly ascending to the 
north; at the beginning of January 1897, located above 
 Aquarii. 
5 Sept., observed by Villiger (Munich) with a 101/2-inch refractor; 
11™.3, no sharp nucleus, D=1'; measured at the same time in Algiers 
(32-cm refractor) as a faint, diffuse object; central condensation occasionally 
observed.

condensation of 12™.

the comet as elongated in the direction 
P=160°.

servers in Nice saw an exceptionally faint satellite in direct proximity to 
the comet; the satellite was not reported by other astronomers.

Cerulli (Teramo) measured the position (12 to 13™); 7 Nov., Kobold meas- 
ured the comet with difficulty on dark background as a very faint, diffuse

1897 I (1896g). Discovered in Vulpecula by Perrine (Lick Obs.) on 2 Nov. 
1896 as a small faint nebula without a tail, 2' in diameter, 
m=11™. 
Moved 
toward the Sun, descending south; at the end of November, moved in Aquila. 
Before the perihelion passage (8 Feb.), visible only to the end of December, 
when located below yn Aquilae.

sky (in Sagittarius);

the end of March, moved to Pavo; 
in the middle of April, passed 12° from 
the south pole, crossing Apus, Chamaeleon and entering Carina; at the end 
of April, in Vela; in May, moved north between Antlia and Pyxis.

slightly granular condensation, D=1'.5.

1897 III (1897b). 
Discovered in Camelopardus by Perrine (Lick Obs.) on 
16 Oct. Intrinsic brightness 8™; sharp starlike nucleus of 12™ in the northern 
part of the elongated nebulous mass; D=2'; tail 10' long; from the nucleus 
extended a 4 to 5' ray of uniform brightness throughout its length. 
Moved 
through Cassiopeia and Cepheus; Oct. 29, passed 2° from the pole; at the 
beginning of November, moved to Draco and descended south passing near 
the pole of the ecliptic.

in Pola, the central condensation 
was estimated at 10™, tail at 
P=200°;

as large; through a large telescope, 
a 10 to 11™ nucleus and a 2' tail.

30" tail

The twenty-eighth recorded apparition 
of Comet Encke- 
Backlund. 
Spotted on the evening of 7 June by Grigg (Thames, New Zealand) 
according to his own ephemeris position only 3° above the horizon; 
11 June, 
Tebbutt (Windsor) discovered the comet according to A. Ivanov's ephemeris. 
Located in Gemini, moving rapidly south; crossed Canis Minor and 
Monoceros, passing south of Hydra and moving in Centaurus in July. 
Upon discovery, well visible in twilight through a 4 1/2-inch refractor 
(6™); 25 June and 26 June, observed only through an 8-inch telescope, 
though still visible with great difficulty through a 41/2-inch refractor; 
appeared as a faint spot with 
D=2 to 3'. 
27 June, invisible even through 
an 8-inch telescope. 
10 July, having left the twilight region, the comet was 
visible with periphereal vision in an absolutely clear, moonless sky and as 
a faint white spot 5 to 6' in diameter.

1898 IV (1898f). 
The third recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Wolf (1). Discovered in Aries according to Thraen's ephemeris by 
Hussey (Lick Obs.) on 16 June. 
Moved east-south-east; 
in July and August, 
passed through Taurus; in September, through Orion; in October, moved 
in Monoceros; 
in November and December, located in Canis Major; 
in 
January 1898 slowly moved north; in February and March, again in 
Monoceros. 
Upon discovery, was apparently no fainter than 12™. 
16 July, Kobold (Strasbourg) described the comet as a bright, circular 
nebula with an 11™ central condensation. 
14 July to 25 July, Abetti measured 
the comet with a 101/2-inch refractor; 
15 July, he reported a 12™ nucleus 
out of focus; 
16 July and 17 July, faint; 20 July and 21 July, difficult to ob- 
serve.

1898 V (1898g). 
Discovered in Capricornus by Giacobini (Nice) on18June 
as a faint 11 to 12™ nebula 1/2 to 1' in diameter with a 15'' condensation. 
Moved west; 
at the end of June, crossed Sagittarius; 
in July, moved in 
Ophiuchus, and then through Libra and Virgo; 
in August, located in Virgo. 
21 June, Hartwig (Bamberg) estimated 
m=10™; circular comet, D=2', 
eccentric condensation. 
20 June, Abetti (Arcetri) described the comet as 
extremely faint, with a 12™ nucleus and a faint nebula; 28 June, brightness 
estimated at 10 to 11™ in moonlight. 
27 July, Cerulli saw the comet through 
a seeker with a 21/2-inch aperture (9 to 10™). 
19 June, Stephan (Marseilles) 
described the comet as very faint and granular; 
20 June, even fainter and 
more condensed; 
23 June, even more highly condensed about a small 10™ 
nucleus (9 to 10™); 22 June to 29 June, measured by Kortazzi (Nikolaev) 
with a 9-inch refractor. 
9 July, observers in Besancon reported exception- 
ally faint comet (11 to 12™). Observed in Bordeaux, Munich, Padua, 
Palermo, Paris, Rio de Janeiro, Toulouse and Washington. 
16 July, Kobold 
saw the comet as exceptionally faint with a 15™ nucleus in the front part of 
the nebula (12 to 13™). 
Javelle (Nice) last measured the position with a 
76-cm telescope on 16 Aug.; observed at the visibility threshold (13 to 14™). 
References: A.N., 146-149; B.A., 15, 16;,A.J., 18; V.J.S., 34, 35;

1898 VII (1898c). 
Discovered on 11 June by Coddington (Lick Obs.) from 
a photograph of the Antares region taken on 9 June; observed visually as a 
remarkably bright object; discovered independently on 14 June by Pauly 
(Bucharest). 
Moved south-west, passing through Lupus in June; in July,, 
August and September, through Centaurus; 
in October, located near Apus; 
in the middle of November, passed through Octans at 5° from the south pole; 
in December located in Tucana. 
14 June, Millosewich (Rome) estimated the nucleus at 8™, tail at P =20°; 
on that night, Schorr (Hamburg) reported a diffuse 9™.5 nucleus, D=1/3'. 
Pauly spotted the comet while observing the star cluster NGC 4183 through 
a 75-mm telescope; appeared as diffuse, nebulous mass, smaller and 
fainter than the star cluster (6 to 7™). 
16 June, comet grew fainter; 14June 
and 15 June, photographed by King (Harvard); Wendell estimated the bright- 
ness at 7™.7 within a circle 1' in diameter. 
15 June, Cruls (Rio de Janeiro), 
with a 24-cm refractor, 
described comet as bright; D=4 to 5'; 8™ nucleus. 
24 June, comet fainter, eccentric nucleus; 
25 June, bright nucleus, nebula 
larger in diameter.

envelope with 
D=1 to 1.5.

D=3'; circular comet, 
no nucleus.

meridian circle at the Lick Obs.

illuminated field; comet very suitable for measurements (7 to 8™); a fan- 
shaped jet at 
P=30°.

Abetti (Arcetri).

a 10™nucleus.

1898 X (1898i). 
Discovered on 20 Oct. by Brooks (Geneva, N.Y.) while 
observing the northern sky with a 10-inch refractor. 
Located near a 
Draconis; 
m=7™; D=4'; central condensation. 
Moved rapidly south-east; 
at the end of October, located in Hercules; 
in November, crossed Ophiuchus; 
at the end of November, passed through Scutum. 
22 Oct., Brooks described the comet in moonlight as brighter than upon 
discovery; easily observed through a 3-inch seeker. 
2 Nov., short, wide 
tail first reported; 
11 Nov., two tails clearly visible extending at right 
angles to one another; the large tail was directed away from the Sun; the 
short tail pointed north; 
15 Nov., only one tail observed. 
At maximum 
brightness (beginning of November), the comet was visible to the naked eye 
and with full certainty through binoculars (6™). 
25 Oct., Ristenpart (Kiel) gave m=7™, D=2'; Hartwig (Bamberg), with 
a heliometer, 
saw the comet in spite of moonlight; D=7 to 8'; slightly 
elongated; an 8™,5 starlike nucleus observed in the central condensation. 
* The divergence in estimates is mainly due to the differences in magnification of the telescopes used. 
At a given distribution of brightness in the cometary head, such differences alter the apparent brightness 
of the background on which the central condensation is observed.

1899 I (1899a). 
Discovered in Eridanus by L. Swift (Echo Mountain) on 
3 March when visible.to the naked eye, with starlike nucleus, two envelopes 
and a short tail. 
Moved north; 
at the end of March, having entered Cetus, 
disappeared in morning glow. 
After the perihelion passage, located in 
Pegasus at the end of April; 
in May, passed through Pegasus, Lacerta and 
Cygnus; 
in June, moved in Hercules and Bootes; 
at the beginning of July, 
passed near a Bootis; 
in August, located in Virgo.

(Utrecht) reported 7™ (6™ in later publications) at low position in bright 
twilight.

5 to 6™ star out of focus; no tail; D=1'.5.

on 11 May; the second nucleus (9™.5) was at a distance of 12'.5 from the

of the nucleus was not observed through telescopes.

of the tail.

F,.A. Bredikhin studied in detail the tail of the comet on Lick plates, 
establishing the existence of two tails, type I and type III; he maintained 
that the considerable changes in the comet were correlated with solar 
activity, Solar energy being responsible for the ejections from the comet, 
Bull. Acad. Petersburg, 14(5) p. 501. 1901; Obs. p. 320. 1901. 
The comet 
obviously had an active type I tail with numerous ray systems, some of 
which were erroneously referred by F.A. Bredikhin to type III tail, 
T.V. Vodop'yanova, A. Zh, , 10: 187. 1933.

no continuous spectrum, another factor testifying to the absence of a type

again; at the end of December, located 12° from the pole.

1901 1(1901la). 
Bright southern comet. 
Discovered by Viscara (Uruguay) 
on 12 April and independently by Hill (Queenstown, Africa) and Tattersall 
(Australia) on 23 April. Appeared as a bright 1 to 2™ object with a noticeable 
$51 

===PAGE_0170 (book p.352)===
tail in the bright morning sky. 
In April, moved eastward between Pisces 
and Cetus; after the perihelion passage (24 April), observed in the evening 
sky. 
In May, moved between Eridanus and Taurus; 
in the middle of May, 
crossed Orion: 
at the end of May and in June, passed through Monoceros 
and Canis Minor, receding from the Sun and the Earth. 
24 April, nucleus visible for some time after the sunrise. 
Innes (Cape) 
described the comet as bright (0 to 1™) with a 10° tail curving south; 
deep 
yellow comet; nucleus equal in brightness to Mercury. 
Nucleus exceeded 
Sirius in brightness (-1™.58). 
3 May, a secondary straight tail was reported; 
6 May, secondary tail 30° long extending at 40° to the main tail, which was 
1/4 of the secondary tail's length. 
One of the photographs taken on 6 May 
showed two additional rays between the main and the straight tails. 
5 May, 
Nijland(Sumatra) described the comet as brighter than Aldebaran.

observed.

OP onto Lomas 
Accordingto Nijland, 
who collected the estimates of sailors observing 
the comet at sea, the main tail was 15° long on 14 May to 18 May; the 
straight, faint tail was occasionally tracked over 3 times this distance. 
The tails were also observed by Lunt (South Africa), Otten (Peru), Prosé 
(Brazil), 
et al. 
The main tail was divided along the axis by a dark gap, the 
"shadow" of the nucleus. 
5 May and 8 May, the angle between the main and 
the secondary tail was 15°; 15 May, 
11°. According to Hill, the nucleus 
had a continuous spectrum with some bright lines. 
Comet photographed by 
Hill (Cape of Good Hope) and Barnard (Yerkes Obs.). 
Important drawings 
made by Nijland(Sumatra), Innes (Cape), Prose (Brazil), Viscara (Uruguay), 
and others. 
From 23 May, comet was invisible to the naked eye. 
Measured last on 
13 June by Tebbutt (Windsor, 8-inch refractor) and on 14 June by Innes 
(Cape, 7-inch refractor) (10 to 11™).

F.A, Bredikhin, 
Bull. Acad. Petersburg, 15(5): 451; A.N., 158: 219, 
proceeding from photographs and drawings, classified the main tail as 
type II and the isolated straight rays as type II synchron and type III tail; 
trace of a type I tail. 
N.D. Moiseev according to the suggestion of S. V. Orlov, 
who considered F.A. Bredikhin's conclusions erroneous, 
reconsidered the 
nature of the main tail, showing that it could be a type III synchron produced 
by the explosion on 26 May.-A. Zh., 2: 73. 1925. 
Unfortunately the entire 
available body of data was not analyzed, and therefore the opinion of 
S.V. Orlov and N.D. Moiseev requires additional confirmation (S. V. Orlov 
in his book ''Komety" gives only a type III tail for comet 1901 I). The comet 
352 

===PAGE_0171 (book p.353)===
undoubtedly had a type I tail, apparently type II synchrons and possibly a 
type III tail.

1901 II (1901b). 
The twenty ninth recorded apparition 
of Comet Encke. 
Spotted near Thonberg's ephemeris position by Wilson (Northsfield) on the 
morning of 5 Aug. Located in Auriga, moved through Gemini (August), 
Cancer (end of August) and Leo (beginning of September) and then passed 
rapidly to Ophiuchus (October). 
8 Aug., observers in Konigsberg and Heidelberg reported a very diffuse 
comet lacking any sharp nucleus, D=1'; 9 Aug., central condensation, 
. 
nonhomogeneous nebula; 
11 Aug., nucleus more obvious.

1902 I (1902a). 
Discovered in Pegasus by Brooks (Geneva, N.Y.) on the 
morning of 14 April as a bright telescopic object (7 to 8™) with a small star- 
like nucleus and a short narrow tail. 
Moved south-east to conjunction with 
the Sun. 
16 April, Hartwig (Bamberg) estimated m = 8™.5; circular comet witha 
9™ condensation, D=3', tail 25' in the direction 
P=300°. 
16 April, Aitken 
(Lick Obs.) gave m =8™,5, straight tail extending over 30! away from the 
Sun; a condensation without any starlike nucleus observed in the head. 
17 April and 18 April, observed in Pola; faint at dawn with a 10 to 10™.5 
nucleus. 
18 April, photographs (7-minute exposure) taken by Wolf 
(Heidelberg) showed a wide faint tail; the southern, concave edge of the 
tail was noticeably brighter than the northern edge. Observed also in 
Copenhagen and Arcetri. 
After 19 April, invisible in morning glow; in May, 
after the perihelion passage, observers at the Lick Observatory searched 
for the comet in vain with 12- and 36-inch refractors. 
References: A.N., 158, 159; M.N., 62, 63; V.J.S., 38.

1902 II (1902c). 
The first recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Grigg-Skjellerup. 
Discovered on 22 July by Grigg (Thames, New 
Zealand) with a 31/2-inch telescope. 
Located between Leo and Virgo, 
moving south-east; 
at the beginning of August, located in Virgo. 
Because 
of a delay in the discovery report, observed only by Grigg up to 3 Aug., 
when it was very faint in haze (9 to 10™). According to his descriptions, 
the comet appeared as a faint nebula 1'.5 in diameter. 
Could not have been 
fainter than 9 to 10™, since observed and measured witha 31/2-inchtelescope. 
References: A.N., 159; M.N., 63; V.J.S., 38. 
Orbit: Merton. —Mem. R.A.S., 64: 74.

1902 III (1902b). 
Discovered in Perseus by Perrine (Lick Obs.) with a 
12-inch refractor on the night between 31 Aug. and1Sept. asa 9™ object, D=4 
to5', slightly elongated with a well-developed 10.5 to 11™ nucleus and a short 
(8 to 10') wide tail (jet?) extending south-west. 
Discovered independently 
in Europe by Borrelly (Marseilles) on 2 Sept. Moved through Perseus 
(September) Cassiopeia (end of September), Lacerta, Cygnus (beginning of 
October), Lyra, Hercules and Ophiuchus (October-November); 
in December, 
descended south, passing through Scorpio, Libra and Centaurus. 
In 
January 1903 passed along Centaurus and Vela; in February, crossed 
Puppis and Canis Major; in March, 
moved through Monoceros, ascending north. 
Extensive observations of appearance and brightness were carried out, 
particularly by Holetschek (Vienna), Nijland (Utrecht), Wirtz (Strasbourg), 
Perrine (Lick Obs.) Barnard (Yerkes Obs.) and observers at Lyon and 
Copenhagen. 
According to Holetschek, 
3 Sept. to 5 Sept., m=8™.5, D=2', nucleus 
9™,5; 20 Sept. and 21 Sept., 
m=7™.5, D=3', tail 30'; 27 Sept. 
to 30 Sept., 
m =5.5 to 47,9, D=4!', tail 15'; 8 Oct. to 15 Oct., maximum apparent 
brightness (4.2 to 4™.1), D=4', tail 30 to 60' (visual observations), nucleus 
9™_5; at the beginning of November, m =4™.5 to 57.5, D=3 to 1.5, tail 20! 
long. Nijlandestimated the intrinsic brightness at 3™.8 and the tail length at60'. 
19 Sept. to29Oct., observed by Modestov (Moscow, witha 15-inchrefractor; 
at the beginning of September, observed by Sokoloff (Pulkovo). On 27 nights 
between 6 Sept. and 29 Oct., the comet was photographed with the 30-inch 
reflector of the Greenwich Observatory. 
26 Sept. to 29 Sept., long-exposure 
plates showed a bright circular head, no less than 7 rays and a long 1° tail 
directed approximately away from the Sun. 
Photographs taken on 25 Sept., 
26 Sept., 
7 Oct., 
8 Oct. and 9 Oct., by Sikora (Yur'ev) with 2 to 4 1/2 
hours exposure time showed two tails; the long straight tail extended over 
3° and the second short tail was pear-shaped; 
the long tail was classified 
by Sikora as type I, the short one as type III. 
25 Sept. and 1 Oct., photo- 
graphed at the Meudon and Harvard Observatories, and also by Roberts 
and M. Wolf, the latter trying to detect the stereoscopic effect on photo- 
graphs of the comet. 
Extensive photographic observations were carried out by Curtis (Lick 
Obs.) with 78 and 83-cm cameras and exposure times from 25 minutes to 
6 hours. In the second half of October photographs showed an 11° straight 
tail; the secondary tail was highly curved and considerably shorter than 
354 

===PAGE_0173 (book p.355)===
the main tail but nearly equally bright. 
Photographs show numerous rays 
and considerable activity in the tail; the nature of the ray systems changed 
radically within 24 hours. 
17 Nov., observed for the last time at northern observatories before 
disappearing in the solar rays. 
After the perihelion passage, observed in 
Santiago from 11 Dec. 1902 to 4 Jan. 1903. 
Tebbutt (Windsor) observed the 
comet up to 16 Feb.; Wiggin (Cordoba), up to 5 March 1903, 
The comet 
was observed from 29 Jan. 1903 at Lick and University Park Observatories; 
in February, 
11 to 12™. Wirtz (Strasbourg) described the comet asa circular 
diffuse nebula with slight condensation. 
23 Feb. to 28 Feb., Aitken (Lick 
Obs.) reported very faint irregular comet, no nucleus. 
Measured last by 
Howe (University Park) on 30 March; 
17 April not spotted with a 36-inch 
refractor. 
References: A.N., 159, 160; M.N., 63; B.A., 21; A.J., 41; L.O.B., 
28: 42; V.J.S., 38, 39. 
Orbit: Peck and Lindsey. —A.J., 27. Remarkably close encounter with 
Mercury (29 Nov.) at a distance of 0.023 a. u. 
In V.I, according to Holetschek's observations, y=11.5; Hj=6™.0. 
The 
same source gave, according to the estimates of Nijland, Graff, Wirtz et al., 
y= 9:2; Hp =5™.7; in .GsA.M. ,. Hip = 6™.0.

1903 Ill (1903b). 
Discovered in Eridanus by Grigg (New Zealand) on the 
evening of 17 April. Moved north-east (away from the Sun) through Eridanus 
(beginning of May), Lepus and Canis Major (end of May). 
Observed only in New Zealand, Australia and atthe Cape. Upon discovery, 
appeared faint through a 3-inch telescope; no nucleus (fainter than 8 to 9™). 
Receded from the Sun (perihelion passage on 26 March) growing rapidly 
faint. 
Measured last on 28 May in Windsor with an 8-inch refractor and 
at the Cape of Good Hope with a 7-inch refractor when hardly visible (not 
fainter than 12 to 13™). 
References: M.N., 64; V.J.S., 39.

21 June. 
Later,

and 30 May. Upon discovery, fairly bright (8™?) with a 10™ nucleus anda 
10' fan-shaped tail; brightness rapidly increased.

Major (end of July and August); after disappearance in the solar rays, ob-

in July and

STEM 

===PAGE_0176 (book p.358)===
Curtis and Albrecht (Lick Obs.) photographed the comet with 83-cm and 
178-cm cameras. 
All plates showed two tails: long straight tail, which 
was the fainter of the two, and a short curving, very bright secondary tail; 
20 June, tails up to 30! long; 29 July, two rays observed; 20 July, the first 
tail 5° long, the secondary (bright) tail 1°30' long. 
Changes were observed 
in the straight tail; 
14 July, 1.8-hour exposure showed an 8'30' narrow tail; 
on that day, the Earth was near the plane of the cometary orbit and the 
short tail was not observed. 
In July and August, the long tail was traced 
over 10°, changing in shape from day to day; it was directed precisely 
away from the Sun and split into two distinct branches at a 7° angle to one 
another. 
In August, numerous 
condensations 
were 
observed in the tail. 
20 June, the secondary (curved) tail formed a 22° angle with the main tail; 
in the middle of July, both tails coincided; 
18 Aug., the angle between the 
two tails was 33°; numerous rays observed, 
Barnard (Yerkes Obs.) photographed the comet with a small camera, 
reporting a 17° tail on several nights. 
24 July, sharp change in tail's 
shape; part of the tail seemed to separate from the main body; 23 July and 
25 July, no anomalies reported; "separation" of the tail registered on 
plates taken by Quénisset, Roberts and Smith (Yale Obs.). 
After the perihelion passage, measured at the Cape Obs. from 30 Sept. 
to 22 Oct., growing rapidly faint; on the last days, not fainter than 11 to Wau.

1904 I (1904a). 
Discovered in Hercules by Brooks (Geneva, N.Y.) on 
16 April as a 9™ object with a clear 10™ nucleus and a 1'.5 coma; fan-shaped 
tail 4' long. 
Later, the comet was spotted on plates of the discovery region 
taken by Rudeauxon16 April; astronomers at Harvard also spotted the 
comet according to Weiss' ephemeris onplates taken at the Arequipa outpost 
on 14 May, 
18 May, 22 June, 23 June and 24 June 1903 (i.e., almost one 
year before the discovery). 
Moved slowly north-west passing through 
Bootes, Draco (May), Ursa Major (June) and Canes Venatici 
(July- 
November); 
in December, moved north again through Ursa Major (January- 
February 1905); in April, May and June 1905, located in Lynx, 
Observed extensively because of the favorable celestial position (about 
1300 visual and photographic determinations). 
Thorough series of estimates 
of intrinsic brightness andnuclear brightness were obtained by Holetschek, 
Wirtz, Nijland, Abetti et al. According to Holetschek, 
in April 1904 the 
comet was stable at 97.0 to 9™.2 with D=1'.4 anda 
5!' tail.

10™;

1904 II (1904d). 
Discovered in Corona Borealis by Giacobini (Nice) in the 
morning sky of 17 Dec. as a small faint nebula of 11", granular in structure, 
with several condensation centers. 
Moved north-east through Hercules 
(December 1904 to January 1905), Draco (February), Cepheus (March) and 
Cassiopeia (March-April). 
19 Dec., Wolf (Konigstuhl) reported short tail and nonhomogeneous 
nucleus.

numerous condensations in the nebulous mass.

1904 III (1904c). 
The fifth recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Tempel (2). Spotted according to Coniel's ephemeris by Javelle (Nice) 
on 30 Nov. as a very faint, well-outlined, whitish spot with 
D=1.5 to 2'; 
no nucleus (12 to 13™). 
Located in Sagittarius, moving east; at the end of 
December 1904 and in January 1905, moved in Capricornus. 
Up to 8 Dec., observed in Nice; 
5 Dec. to 7 Dec., measured in Algiers 
as a very faint object. From 8 Dec., exceptionally faint and small; meas- 
ured near the horizon by Howe (Denver, Colorado) with a 20-inch refractor. 
9 Dec., not far from moon; 
27 Dec. and 29 Dec. exceptionally faint in haze; 
2 Jan. 1905 extremely faint.

1905 IV (1906b). 
Discovered by Kopff (Konigstuhl) on 3 March 1906 ona 
photograph of the Virgo-Leo region; 
a conspicuous nebula with 10™,5 nucleus 
and a 30' tail. 
Moved slowly north-west, remaining in Leo; in May and 
June, returned through Leo and Virgo.

at the Lick Obs. Wolf (Konigstuhl) spotted the comet according to Abell's

when the comet was near B Tauri and was 12™. 
It was also detected on two

located in Aries; 
15™.5.

1906 II (1906c). 
Discovered in Cetus by Ross (Melbourne) on 18 March. 
17 March (according to universal time) as an 8™ circular object, D=3', 
with small central condensation. 
Moved north; at the beginning of April, 
entered Aries. 
19 March and 20 March observed by Giacobini (Nice) and estimated at 
8™, 
21 March, Wirtz (Strasbourg) saw the comet as a diffuse nebula with 
D=2'5, m=10™1, 
29 March and 31 March, Abetti (Arcetri) observed the 
comet at an altitude of 5 to 8°. Observations discontinued on 3 April at 
Washington and Lick Observatories because of poor visibility and proximity 
to the Sun.

1906 III (1906f). 
The third recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Holmes. 
Spotted photographically according to Zwiers' ephemeris 
by Wolf (Konigstuhl) on 28 Aug. as a faint 15.5 to 16™ object. Remained in 
Perseus, moving slowly north-east and then west 
. 
28 Aug. plates showed a concentric halo, almost circular in appearance, 
with the western edge brighter than the eastern one. 
Photographed by Wolf 
on 25;Sept. and estimated at 15™; 10 Oct., Kopff gave m =15™.5 (photo- 
graphic). 
22 Oct., Wirtz, with an 18-inch refractor on dark background, 
saw no traces of the comet. 
In August and September, invisible through 
the 36-inch Lick refractor. 
Photographed on 7 Dec.; m=16™.

Comet Kopff.

in December, observed 
in Pisces.

1906 V (1906d). 
The third recorded apparition 
of the short-period 
Comet Finlay. 
Discovered photographically according to Fayet's ephemeris 
by Kopff (Heidelberg) on 16 July; later, detected on a plate taken on 14 July. 
Located between Aquarius and Cetus, moving north-east; passed through 
Cetus, Taurus (August), Gemini (September-Ocotber) and Cancer. 
Upon discovery, bright object; Wirtz (Strasbourg) gave 
m=9™, D=12'. 
21 July, described by Guillaume (Lyon) as an 11.5 nebula with diffuse 
edges (the brightness estimates obviously referred to the central conden- 
sation). 
29 July, Brunn (Heidelberg) reported two nuclei through a 12-inch 
refractor. 
The favorable position of the comet facilitated extensive observa- 
tion, e.g., by Abetti (Arcetri), Knopf (Jena), Palisa and Rheden (Vienna), 
Silbernagel (Munich), Wirtz and particularly Holetschek (Vienna). Hereare 
selected m-and D-values from Holetschek's data*,

Andromeda Nebula (5™).

longer observed, and Esclangon discounted his previous report, attributing

lenses.

Discoveredin Canis Major by Giacobini (Nice) on 10 March

and April; observed only till 11 May because of proximity to the Sun and

1907 II (1907b). 
Apparently, 
a return of comet 1742. 
Discovered in 
Caelum not far from a Columbae by Grigg (New Zealand) on 8 April. 
Faint 
object nearly 15' in diameter; because of poor sky, neither a clear nucleus 
nor a tail was observed (6 to 7™). Discovered independently by Mellish 
(Washburn Obs., U.S.A.) on 14 April in Monoceros as faint and diffuse 
object; spotted by Barnard (Lick Obs.) on a photograph of 13 April. 
At 
that time, the comet moved rapidly north; 
in the middle of April, located 
in Lynx; in the middle of May, in Ursa Major. 
15 April, Price (Washington) saw the comet with small instruments 
(8 to 9™), 
16 April, Bianchi (Rome) described the comet as faint and highly 
diffuse; D&1' (apparently central condensation). 
17 April, Abetti (Arcetri) 
observed the comet with great difficulty on a poor sky as a small white spot, 
hardly distinguishable in the field of vision (9 to 10™). 
30 April, Javelle 
(Nice) measured the comet with a 76-cm refractor as a diffuse nebula, 
D=3 to 4'. Observed by Aitken (Lick Obs.) with a 12-inch refractor. 
17 April, visible through a 3-inch seeker even after the near-by Moon had 
risen; absence of a condensation made measurements difficult (8 to 9™). 
16 April, D=2', almost circular comet with a wide V-shaped tail 8' long. 
Rapidly grew faint. 
7 May, difficult to observe through a 36-inch refractor; 
7 May, Wirtz (Strasbourg) measured the comet as a diffuse nebulous spot 
without a nucleus; 
10 May, inner condensation 1'.5 in diameter; 
7 May, 
m=10™.9, D=3',3; 
10May, 11™.5, 3'; 14 May, 
12™.0, 
2'.5. 
7 May, plates 
taken in Heidelberg showed the comet as a uniformly faint nebula with an 
exceptionally faint eccentric nucleus; fainter than 12™.5. 
References: A.N., 174-176; M.N., 68; L.O.B., 120; V.J.S., 44. 
Orbit: Weiss. 
— Denksch. Wiener Ak., 84:12. The probable identity with 
comet 1742 was first suggested by Berberich. 
Orbit calculated by assuming 
a period of 165 years; encounter with the orbit of Saturn and the orbit of 
Earth at the descending node. 
V.I gave, according to Wirtz, y=21, Hjj)=9™.7; 
in C.A.M., Hjg=10™.6; 
from first estimates, we obtain Hjyg=10™.4,

1907 III (1907c). 
Presumably the second recorded apparition 
of the 
short-period Comet Tuttle-Giacobini-Kresak. 
Discovered by Giacobini 
(Nice) on 1 June when exceptionally faint, nearly on the visibility threshold 
of a 46-cm telescope, D,=1.5 to 2' (13™). 
Located in Leo, moving south- 
east; 
at the end of July, should have been located in Coma Berenices.

5 June and 8 June, measured by Millosewich and Zappa (Rome) with a 
38-cm refractor; 
difficult to observe.

as too faint to observe with a 12-inch refractor; measured with a 36-inch 
telescope (13™), no nucleus, no condensation.

measured the comet on a 27-inch refractor ina somewhat cloudy sky at 
the visibility threshold.

1907 IV (1907d). 
Discovered by Daniel (Princeton, 
U. S.A.) on 9 June 
near the vernal equinox as a nebula of m=9™.5 and D=2! with a 10™ conden- 
sation. 
Moving north-west, passed through Pisces (June-July), Aries, 
Taurus (July), Gemini Cancer (August) and Leo (September). 
From 24 Sept., 
disappeared in the solar rays. 
At the beginning of November, located in 
Virgo; then moved in Libra (January to March 1908); from April to June, 
again located in Virgo. 
Along with 1908 III, comet 1907 IV yielded many new observational data 
which facilitated the explanation of the specific physical processes in 
comets. 
Numerous photometric, photographic and spectral observations 
were obtained, 
the most significant being the estimates of intrinsic and 
nuclear brightness due to Holetschek (Vienna), Nijland (Utrecht) Wirtz 
(Strasbourg), Schwab (Kremsmunster) et al. 
Already by 20 June, 
m=8™, D=6 
to 7'; a tail pointing west. 
1 July to 
5 July, 
m=7™6; 
10 July, 4™.2; 21 July to 31 July, 37.2, D=5 to 9', visual 
observations gave a tail 2 to 3° long; 2 Aug. to 9 Aug., 2.8 to 27.5; 10Aug. 
to 19 Aug., 2.1 to 2™,3, tail 6 to 9°; 20 Aug. and 30Aug., 2™, D=2', tail ~2°; 
inSeptember, nucleus 4 to 5™ in twilight. 
12 Aug. to9Sept., measured at the 
Catania Obs. witha wedge photometer; brightness deviatedfrom the Po As daw. 
Comet extensively photographed on 36 nights between 10 June and 12 Sept. 
by Duncan (Lick Obs.) (71 plates); 21 July to 27 Aug., Wolf (Heidelberg) 
obtained 41 negatives within 10 nights; 
12 Aug. to 11 Sept., G.A. Tikhov 
(Pulkovo) obtained 11 photographs on the Bredikhin astrograph (with filters); 
11 July to 8 Sept., photographed by Barnard (Yerkes Obs.) (25 negatives); 
12 July to 25 July, photographed by Quenisset (Juvisi), 
I. and J. Fri¢ and 
Nusl (Ondrejov Obs.), Riccd, Horn, Bemporad (Catania Obs.) and others. 
10 July to 12 July, main tail appeared as a straight, homogeneous band; 
a system of jet-rays undergoing rapid changes emerged from the head 
(Barnard observed changes take place within 2 to 6 hours). 
Photographs 
taken at the Lick Obs. showed tail condensations. 
In the second half of July 
and in August, the tail consisted of a multitude of rays emerging from the 
head. 
Duncan reported in August a dark stripe extending from the nucleus 
along the middle of the tail (''shadow" of the nucleus); tail up to 17° long. 
In September, 
a parabolic envelope with dark axial gap. 
Ray structures 
located outside the orbital plane, 
a conclusion drawn from the appear- 
ance of the comet when the Earth passed through its orbital plane on 
15 July. 
Considering 59 photographs between 10 July and 27 Aug., Horn 
* This example illustrates the preferability of medium power instruments for observing diffuse nebulosities. 
372 

===PAGE_0191 (book p.373)===
deduced that the axis of the tail cone oscillated with a period of 16 hours, 
deviating from the orbital plane. A detailed study of the photographs was 
undertaken by Kopff, who attempted to determine solar repulsive forces and 
to investigate the rays and the jets. Polaroscopic observations of Duncan 
(8 Aug. to 12 Aug.) indicated no reflected sunlight in the tail. 
Plate XXX. Comet 1907 IV (Daniel). Photographed by M. Wolf 
After emerging from the solar rays the comet was observed from 14 Nov. 
to 18 Nov. by Abetti (Arcetri) at dawn as a circular 1' spot without a tail; 
14 Nov., m=8 to 9™; 18 Nov., 
8 to 9™; 
well visible in moonlight. 
15 Dec. 
to 17 Dec., appeared as a 9™ star out of focus on a dark sky.

sation brighter than 10™.5, 
a dense coma 2! in diameter; 
12 Feb., nucleus 
10™, asymmetric coma elongated north-west; 26 March, nucleus 12™ with 
hardly noticeable nebula; 21 April, nucleus 12”, asymmetric coma 0'.6 in 
diameter. 
4 May, hardly visible; 
18 May, not detected. 
24 June, measured 
by Javelle (Nice); a 15™ nucleus that disappeared the next day. 
27 June 1908, 
measured last in Nice: 
a pale, ill-shaped nebula 0'.4 in diameter. 
19 April 
1909, M. Wolf (Heidelberg) at a precalculated spot on two plates spotted a 
nebulous object that could have been the comet (16™.5).

1908 III (1908c). 
Discovered at the Des Moines (Iowa) University 
Observatory on 1 Sept. by Morehouse on a photograph showing the comet 
as a 9™ object with a long tail. 
Discovered independently by Borrelly 
(Marseilles) on 3 Sept. 
One of the remarkable comets of the 20th century that played a particular 
role in the evolution of our knowledge of the processes in cometary heads 
and tails. 
Located in Camelopardus; moved through Cassiopeia, Cepheus 
(September), Cygnus, Lyra (October), Hercules, Aquila (November), 
Scutum and Sagittarius (December) approaching conjunction with the Sun. 
At the northern observatories, observed till 14 Dec. (Rome and Lick Obs.); 
in the southern hemisphere, observed from 22 Jan. 1909. 
Passed through 
Corona Australis, Telescopium (February 1909), Pavo, Apus, Chamaeleon 
(March), Carina, Vela (April) and Pyxis (May). 
Favorable celestial position and considerable brightness of the comet 
enabled numerous observers to obtain prolonged series of photometric, 
photographic and spectrographic observations. 
The most important were 
the estimates of intrinsic and nuclear brightness and size of head and tail 
undertaken by Holetschek (Vienna) 13 Sept. to 5 Dec., Nijland (Utrecht, 
375 

===PAGE_0194 (book p.376)===
14 Sept. to 19 Nov.), Wirtz (Strasbourg), 
5 Sept. to 8 Dec., van Biesbroeck 
(Uccle), 30 Sept. to 11 Dec., Abetti (Arcetri), 28 Sept. to 27 Oct., and 
Buttner (Breslau), 
13 Oct. to 13 Dec. 
Plate XXXI, Comet 1908 III (Morehouse). Photographed by S. K. Kostinskii on 
23 Oct., exposure 2 hours 
In September, 
the intrinsic brightness increased from 9 to 6™; D#&5'; 
tail traced visually over 1°5; brightness fluctuated by 1™ within few hours. 
In the middle and the end of October, m#5™; D=3'.5; 
tail 4.5 long. 
In 
November, m=5.2 to 5.4; D=4 to 3'; tail 3 to 5° long. 
15 Sept. to 28 Nov., 
Bemporad made observations with a wedge photometer. 
In December, 
m =5™,4 to 57.8; 30 Jan. 1909, Prager gave m=6™.3; silvery-white comet. 
Remarkable photographs were taken by Barnard (Yerkes Obs. ), and many others. 
Numerous photographs were taken in February and March 1909 by Curtis 
in Santiago (Lick Obs. expedition). The photographs revealed the complicated 
ray structure of the head and the tail, and rapid changes occurring in the 
376 

===PAGE_0195 (book p.377)===
envelopes and the tail. 
The most remarkable cases of motion of tail con- 
densations occurred on 16, 29 to 30 Sept.; 
1 Oct. 
to 2 Oct.; 
6 Oct. to 
7 Oct.; 
15 Oct. to 17 Oct. 
At the end of September, 
the tail was 6 to 8° 
long; 
in October, 
9 to 11°; in November, 
8 to 11°. 
Observers, 
particularly 
Barnard and Wolf, studied the numerous instances of high activity of the 
comet and interesting cases of ''waves'"' in the tail. Enormous accelerations 
were determined by Eddington who studied the motion of the envelopes and 
the rays. 
Measuredfirst on 10 May 1909 by Ristenpart (Santiago) witha 24-cm refractor.

Auriga; 
in the middle of January and in February 1910, located in Lynx; 
in March and April, continued moving in Lynx,

1910 1(1910a). 
Brilliant comet first spotted on 12 Jan. by diamond 
miners in Transvaal; 
15 Jan., detected by railway workers at Copier 
Junction in the Republic of Orange; Innes (Johannesburg), 
after having 
read the newspaper report, observed the comet on 17 Jan. (16.8 hours 
universal time). 
Exceptionally bright comet observed in daytime only 4° 
from the Sun. 
18 Jan., measured in Vienna, 
Algiers and Rome; 
19 Jan., 
in Cambridge and Santiago; beginning 20Jan., observed throughout the world; 
20 Jan. and 21 Jan., discovered independently by various amateurs; 
equal 
in brightness to Venus, 
a 10° tail. 
Upon discovery, located in Sagittarius, 
moving towards the Sun and then north-east through Aquarius and Pegasus 
(February), rapidly receding from the Earth and the Sun. 
In March, April, 
and May, slowly crossed Pegasus, moving north; 
in June, moved to Cygnus; 
in July, located above w Cygni. 
17 Jan., D=5', tail 1° long; after the perihelion passage (17 Jan.) tail 
developed rapidly, becoming more than 40° long at the end of January. 
18 Jan., Wright described the comet as several times brighter than Venus 
at its maximum brightness. 
23 Jan., Graff (Bergedorf) estimated m=1 
to 
2™; bright yellow envelope and a very curving tail. 
In Breslau, 
tail and 
nucleus yellow in color; in Santiago and Bothkamp, twotails were reported. 
23 Jan., Celoria and Gabba (Milan) estimated the nucleus at 1™; 25 Jan., 
2™; 27 Jan., 
3™. 
27 Jan., observers reported a red nucleus. 
28 Jan., 
Silbernagel (Munich) estimated the nucleus at 17.5. 
29 Jan., Baranof 
(Kazan') reported m =3™, yellow color; identical report by Graff and Thiele. 
31 Jan., Hartwig reported a 5™ nucleus. 
Numerous observations were made 
in France, Algeria and at various European and American observatories. 
380 

===PAGE_0199 (book p.381)===
Tail shaped like a curved horn growing wider at the end; nonhomogeneous 
structure with isolated branches extending at considerable angles to horn's 
axis. 
Photographs taken by Barnard at the beginning of February showed a 
sunward (anomalous) tail 15' long. At the end of January, observers at 
Nice reported jets in the head. 
Thorough descriptions of the cometary 
phenomena by Weinek (Prague), Fauth (Landstuhl), who reported a dark 
channel along the tail's middle, and particularly Wolf (photographic des- 
criptions). 
The latter reported interesting features of the anomalous tail. 
Photographed also by Bohlin (Stockholm), Lampland (Flagstaff), Quenisset 
(Paris) and others. 
Spectrum studied at various observatories; 
particularly 
detailed descriptions by Wright and Albrecht (Lick Obs.), Konkoly (Hungary) 
and also Deslandres and Quenisset (France). 
A characteristic feature of 
the spectrum was the bright sodium lines, which, however, 
grew faint as 
early as 30 Jan.; strong continuous spectrum; from 26 Jan., the common 
cometary carbon and CN bands.

1910 II (1909c). 
The thirtieth recorded and the third precalculated ap- 
parition of Halley's Comet. 
Discovered according to Holetschek, Cowell and 
Crommelin's ephemeris by Wolf (Heidelberg) on 11 Sept. 1909 near y 
Geminorum. 
Later, spotted on plates taken on 24 Aug. in Helwan and on 
9 Sept. in Greenwich; estimated at 16™ with small 8 to 10" nebula and 
central condensation. 
First observed visually on 15 Sept. by Barnham 
(Yerkes Obs.) with a 40-inch refractor and on 17 Sept. by Barnard; at that 
time, m=15™.5, D=12''. From September to December 1909, crossed 
Orion and Taurus, moving west and slowly descending south; from January 
to March 1910 moved in Aries and Pisces. 
25 March, superior conjunction 
with the Sun; till 19 May (inferior conjunction with the Sun) observed in the 
morning sky. From June to August, crossed Sextans and Leo; in November 
and December, located in Corvus and Crater, moving west; 
in March 1911, 
passed through Hydra; at the end of April, located in Sextans. 
Initially the brightness slowly increased; 
19 Oct., Barnard estimated 
m=13™ D=15"; 20 Oct., Javelle (Nice) estimated the nucleus at 14 to 15™. 
22 Oct. spectrograms taken with objective prism at the Lick Observatory 
showed weak CN III and C IV bands on strong continuous spectrum. 
In the 
middle of November, Wirtz (Strasbourg) gave 
m=12™; D#1'.3; elongated 
coma; 
a rudimentary tail first reported. 
In the beginning of December, 
Millosewich gave m=10™.5; 
13 Dec. and 14 Dec., Wirtz gave 10.2 to 10™.3, 
D=2'.5. 
Phillips reported remarkable brightness fluctuations and flares 
on 22 Nov. and 8 Dec.; at that time, photographs showed a 1'.5 tail. 
At the 
end of January 1910, m=9™.3; 
in February, 
7.8 to 8".2, D=2', tail 40' long 
with ray systems and jets. CN IV bands brighter than C IV bands; the 
diameter of the external CN envelope was 4!'.4.

===PAGE_0201 (book p.383)===
11 Feb., Wolf saw the comet with the naked eye. 
Some observers 
reported a multiple nucleus in February. 
At the beginning of March, m =8.6 
to 7.9, D#3'; photographs showed a tail with numerous ray systems; jets 
observed in nucleus and head. 
After emerging from the solar rays, comet's 
brightness was 2™.5 in the middle of April. 
15 April to 19 April, photo- 
graphs showed parabolic envelopes and isolated condensations in the head. 
For 20 April to 22 April, Bobrovnikoff mentioned condensations in the tail 
moving under the effect of a considerable repulsive force. 
At the end of 
April, intense activity inthe nucleus and the head; changes recorded on 
photographs and spectrograms, and visually. 
In May, considerable in- 
crease in tail length; 4 May, 6°. 
In May, particularly vigorous activity 
(condensations), rays in tail and head. 
18 May, comet passed across 
the solar disk. 
At the end of May, Millosewich (Rome) reported a 27° tail; 
nuclear brightness 2™.2; intrinsic brightness higher by 2™. At the beginning 
of June, m=3.2 to 4™.1; at the beginning of July, 5.0 to 5™.5.

1910 IV (1910c). 
The seventh recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet d'Arrest. 
Discovered according to Leveau's ephemeris by Gonnessiat 
(Algiers) on 26 Aug. with a 32-cm refractor; m=14™, D=3', conspicuous 
faint nucleus. 
Located in Ophiuchus, moving south-east; 
in September and 
October, crossed Ophiuchus and Sagittarius; 
in November and December, 
located in Aquarius; at the end of December and in January 1911, located 
in Cetus. 
The longest series of observations and descriptions were by Gonnessiat 
and Rembaut (Algiers).

Faye.

in

1911 I (1910d). 
The fourth recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Brooks (2). Spotted according to Bauschinger's ephemeris by Aitken 
(Lick Obs.) on 28 Sept. 1910 as a very faint object (15 to 16™ apparently). 
Located in Sagittarius. 
Discovery doubtful, yet comet observed by Barnard 
(Yerkes Obs.) on 29 Nov. with a 40-inch refractor in Capricornus; m=16™; 
condensation 3 to 4". 
References: 
A N., 186; 205; AJ., 41: 154; V.J.S., 46.

Discovered photographically by Kies (Lick Obs.) in the

tail.

Taurus;

and Sculptor; 
at the end of August and in September, located in Telescopium.

1911 IV (1911g). 
Discovered by S.I. Beljawsky (Simeis) 
in the morning 
of 28 Sept. before sunrise; discovered at various other observatories on 
29 Sept. 
Located in Leo, visible to the naked eye; m=3™; nucleus and tail. 
Moved east; observed near the Sun, at first in the morning sky and then, 
- 
beginning the middle 
of October, 
in the evening sky, when located in Virgo; 
in November and December, observed in the southern sky, passing through 
Libra and Scorpio; 
in January and February 1912, moved in Corona 
Australis. 
From 29 Sept., observed throughout Europe and America, 
29 Sept., 
Millosewich, Schaumasse, Knox-Shaw, Barnard, et al. estimated the nucleus 
at 2™ and the head at 1". Beljawsky's photographs showed a tail consisting 
388 

===PAGE_0207 (book p.389)===
of two 1° branches at 35° to one another. 
30 Sept., three branches; the 
longest was up to 51/2°; 2 Oct., plates taken in Simeis registered a 
three-branch tail over 15° long; the branches were classified by Beljawsky 
as types I, land III. 
At that time, brightness estimated at 2 to 3™; Nijland 
(Utrecht) visually estimated the tail at 15°; parabolic envelopes. 
Brightness 
observations made by Holetschek (Vienna), Rembaut and Sy (Algeria), 
Chernyi (Warsaw), Weinek (Prague), Graff (Bergedorf), Ebell (Kiel), 
Nijland (Utrecht), Battermann (Konigsberg), Borrelly (Marseilles), van 
Biesbroeck (Uccle) et al. 
Detailed descriptions of head structure and the 
shape of the tail in October were compiled by Barnard, Graff, Nijland, 
Rembaut, Sy and mainly Beljawsky (Simeis photographs). 
15 Oct. to 17 Oct., Chernyi, Batterman and Graff gave m=1 
to 1™.5; 
18 Oct. and 19 Oct., m=2 to 2™.5. 
Visual observations described a tail ~2 
to 3° consisting of two symmetric branches emerging from the nucleus at 
P=49°. 
Deep-yellow comet, clearly distinguishable from the bluish Comet 
Brooks; 
17 Oct., Comet Beljawsky observed simultaneously with Comet 
Brooks (1911 V) and Comet Quenisset (1911 VI). 
19 Oct., observers in 
Algiers gave m=2™,5, tail 6°; 26 Oct., m=5™ (low altitude). 
28 Oct., 
observed last in the northern hemisphere (in Vienna); from 3 Sept. , invisible 
through a 12-inch refractor. 
Beginning November, telescopic object observed by Perrine (Cordoba) 
and also in La Plata. 
28 Jan. 1912, Perrine gave 
m=11™"; D=3'; slight 
condensation and jets directed away from the Sun; 14 Feb., D=3', small 
diffuse cendensation; 
15 Feb., m=12™, 
difficult to observe; 
16 Feb., D=3 
to 4', hardly noticeable condensation.

1911 V (1911c). 
Discovered in Pegasus by Brooks (Geneva, New York) 
in the morning of 20 July as a 10™ object moving slowly north-west. 
In 
August, passed through Cygnus; 
in September, located in Draco and Bootes; 
in October, rapidly descended south through Canes Venatici, Coma Berenices 
and Virgo; 
in November and December, crossed Hydra and Centaurus; 
in 
January and February 1912, passed through Lupus and Circinus.

gave m=10 to 10.5, D=4 to 5', nucleus 117; 25 July, elongated coma.

estimates (11™ and 8™.5 for instance) was the result of differences in ob- 
servational means.

1 Oct. 1911, Doberck reported a split in the nucleus. 
According to ob- 
servations at the Engelhardt Observatory, 
26 Sept., tail 26° long; 21 Oct., 
Valier reported a 30° tail. 
Photographed at various observatories: Vienna, Geneva, Bergedorf, 
Jena, Teramo, Helwan, Pulkovo, Madrid, Juvisi and particularly at the 
Yerkes and Lick Observatories. 
Photographs indicated two activity periods. 
At first, there was a wide diffuse head with a straight tail connected to the 
head by narrow jets. Another period began in the middle of October: 
less 
diffuse head, tail not fan-shaped but made up of narrow rays and filaments. 
20 Oct. and 22 Oct., motion of condensations. 
Near 
the 
perihelion 
(28 Oct.), lateral rays appeared; small and sharply outlined head. 
Valier 
reported variations in color; 
till 16 Sept., bluish; 
at the end of September 
and in October, greenish; 
30 Oct., yellowish. 
Photographs taken at Helwan 
in October showed the head envelope extending into a tail, whereas in 
November the tail extended independently from the portion of the nucleus 
facing away from the Sun. 
Last observations in Santiago on 31 Jan. 1912. Observed till 27 Feb. at 
the Cape.

1911 VII (1911h). 
The first recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Schaumasse. 
Discovered in Virgo by Schaumasse (Nice) on 30 Nov. 
as a faint 12™ object with D=3 to 3'.5. 
Moved south-east; 
in January, passed 
between Serpens and Libra; 
in February, located in Ophiuchus. 
Measured at Arcetri, 
Padua, Nice and Uccle, remaining a faint tele- 
scopic object. 
At the beginning of December, measured at Arcetri and 
Padua in moonlight; therefore not fainter than 10 to 11™. A very faint spot 
with a hardly noticeable nebula; according to Antoniazzi on 18 Dec., m=11", 
D=0'5. 
16 Dec. to 29 Dec., observers in Algiers described the comet as 
a diffuse nebula without nucleus, m=12™.5, 
D=2!', faint condensation. 
16 Feb. 1912, measured at Nice and Algiers as a whitish 14™ spot, D=2!'. 
Visible on 17 Feb,. and 18 Feb., but neighboring stars interfered with 
measurements.

1911 VIII (191le). 
The second recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Borrelly. 
Discovered visually according to Fayet's ephemeris by 
Knox-Shaw (Helwan) on 19 Sept.; discovered independently by Schaumasse 
(Nice) on the same night; estimated at 13™, 
Located between Fornax and 
Eridanus; 
in December, moved north-west through Eridanus; 
in December, 
crossed Cetus; 
in January 1912, moved in Aries; then passed through 
Perseus (February), Auriga (March) and Lynx (April). 
Approaching both the Sun and the Earth the comet grew brighter.

nucleus estimated at 12™ in Algiers; 2 Oct., nebula elongatedby 3/4, nucleus

1912 1(1911la). 
The fourth recorded apparition of the periodic Comet 
Wolf(1). Discovered photographically according to Kamienski's ephemeris 
by Wolf (Heidelberg) on 19 June 1911; starlike nucleus slightly brighter 
than 15™.0 with an exceptionally faint envelope, D=0'.3.

in August and September,

still accessible to

1912 II (1912a). 
Discovered between i and > Centauri by Gale (Australia) 
on 8 Sept. through field binoculars; visible to the naked eye; 5™; two tails 
394 

===PAGE_0213 (book p.395)===
ata50to60° angle toone another; head 3 to 4' in diameter. 
Moved north 
passing through Libra (September and October), Serpens (October), Corona 
Borealis (November), Hercules and Draco (December. 1911 and January 
1912); at the end of January, located 5° from the North Pole (in Cepheus), 
descending south through Cassiopeia (February), Camelopardus (March) 
and Auriga (April and May). 
Beginning 13 Sept., photographed at Johannesburg. 
Main narrow tail 
4° long (P = 137°); short fan-shaped tail 1/2° long (P = 183°) with an apex 
angle of 60°; 
14 Sept., main tail over 5° long and divided into two branches; 
m=5™; D=4!'. 
15 Sept. to 19 Sept., photographed by Cooke (Sydney). 
A 
perceptible angle between the two main tails which appeared visually to be 
of equal length (2/3 to 1°) yet actually differed sharply in shape and length. 
Measured at the Cape, La Plata and Santiago. 
Beginning 25 Sept., meas- 
ured at the northern observatories (Rome, Arcetri, Algiers); from the 
beginning of October, observedthroughout Europe. Plates taken at Bergedorf 
and Juvisy on 9 Oct. to 15 Oct., showed a straight main tail 5°.5 long (P = 79°). 
The tail consisted of isolated filaments diverging over 11' at the end. 
Lateral tail forming an 86° angle with the main one; 
10 to 15' long, curving; 
invisible to the naked eye; main tail traced visually over only 1°; m =4™.4; 
D=3'; bluish comet. 
Ordinary spectrum with bright CN and C lines anda 
continuous spectrum particularly intense in the blue-violet region; similar 
to the spectrum of comet 1911 II at the beginning of July. 
Extensive photometric estimates by Holetschek (Vienna), Nijland 
(Utrecht), Kritzinger (Bothkamp), Graff (Bergedorf), van Biesbroeck (Uccle) 
and Barnard (Yerkes Obs.). 
20 Oct. to 28 Dec., Silva (Padua) observed 
the comet on a wedge photometer. 
Brightness estimates were also made 
by Guillaume, 
Luther, Eschinitis, Anestin, Knopf, 
Steib, Valier et al. 
At the end of October, m=5.5 to 5™.7, D#4', 
tail over 1° long visually;

1912 IV (1912b). 
The sixth recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Tuttle. 
Discovered by Schaumasse (Nice) on 18 Oct. as a new comet, 
since the tentative ephemeris compiled from Raths' elements gave the date 
of perihelion passage as 11/2 months later than actually occurred. Dis- 
covered independently on 21 Oct., by Brooks (Geneva, N.Y.). 
Circular 
nebula with D = 3' and central condensation of 10.5 to 11™, 
Located in 
Sextans, moving south-east; 
in November, passed through Hydra; 
in 
December, located in Centaurus. 
Observed at Algiers, Nice, Lick Observatory and Washington: 
in the 
southern hemsiphere, 
at Johannesburg, La Plata, Santiago, Melbourne, 
Cordoba and the Cape.

1913 III (1913c). 
The first recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Neujmin(1). Spotted on a photographic plate by G.N. Neujmin (Simeis) 
on 3 Sept. as a 10™,0 asteroidal object. A photograph of 6 Sept. showed a 
1' tail at 
P=130 to 140°; 
5 Sept., nebula detected; 
6 Sept., observed by 
Graff (Bergedorf) with a short tail at 
P=110° and an 11™.0 nucleus. 
Located 
near the vernal equinox, moving north-west; 
in October, moved in Pegasus; 
in November, located above y Pegasi; 
in December 1913 and January 1914, 
moved east in Pisces toward a Arietis. 
7 Sept. and 8 Sept., Palisa (Vienna) and Bianchi gave m= 10.5 to 10™.6. 
9 Sept. to 11 Sept., Barnard (Yerkes Obs.) and van Biesbroeck (Uccle) 
described the appearance of the comet as unusual. 
Ata 
first glance, the 
comet was quite starlike; under high magnification, appeared as:white star. 
Thorough examination revealed a fan-shaped, wide, faint tail (at P = 110°) 
slightly over 1' long; 
nucleus 11™.5 appeared to be independent of the faint 
nebula extending mainly south-east. 
Pokrovskii's photographs (Yur'ev) 
showed the comet as a faint stroke with a nebulous envelope. 
Barnard 
reported that initially the comet was well visible through a 5-inch telescope; 
tail clearly recorded on photographs taken with the Brusov telescope in 
September. 
22 Sept.,-Aitken (Lick Obs.), with a 36-cm refractor, sawa 
small nebulous halo surrounding the nucleus and extending over 3 to 4! in 
the direction 
P=145°. 
1 Sept., hardly detectible through a 12-inch refractor. 
In September, envelope blurred even through a 15-inch refractor (van 
Biesbroeck). 
Later, the comet appeared fully starlike. 
Wolf's photographs 
(Konigstuhl) of 24 Sept. showed a very wide fan-shaped and short tail with 
its axis at P =170° and an apex angle of 135°, 
Numerous estimates of the nuclear brightness were obtained by Graff 
(Bergedorf) from 6 Sept. to 21 Oct.; brightness fluctuated with the bright- 
ness increasing on 10 Sept. and 26 Sept.

1913 VI (1913d). 
The second recorded apparition of the periodic Comet 
Westphal. 
Discovered near a Aquarii by Delavan (La Plata) on 26 Sept. ; 
visible through a small telescope (7.5 to 8™), Moved north through Equuleus, 
400 

===PAGE_0219 (book p.401)===
Delphinus (October), Vulpecula and Cygnus (November and December); 
at 
the end of December, 
located near u Cephei; 
22 Oct., located a few minutes 
of arc from comet 1913 IV, both appearing in the same telescopic field of 
vision. 
28 Sept., Graff (Bergedorf) described it as a circular 8™.0 nebula with 
a bright nucleus, 
Neujmin (Simeis), van Biesbroeck (Uccle) and Nijland 
(Utrecht) gave m=7™.5 to 77.8; at that time Palisa (Vienna), Zappa 
(Capadimonte), Batterman (Konigsberg) and Millosewich (Rome) estimated 
the nucleus at 9 to 10", Kritzinger (Bothkamp) recorded a 3.5° tail at 
P=72 to 108° on a 40-minute exposure; 
a comet seeker revealed a 1°2 tail, 
circular coma D=20!', elongated nucleus. 
29 Sept., van Biesbroeck, through 
a seeker, 
Saw a circular envelope 10! in diameter; 
1 Oct., Bulov and 
Kritzinger, using peripheral vision, saw the comet with the naked eye, 
however Holetschek gave m =8™.5 on 2 Oct., and Dziewulski gave m =9™.3 
on 1 Oct. 
This discrepancy can be explained only by differences in atmos- 
pheric conditions or considerable fluctuations in brightness and structure 
of the head,

(28 Sept. to 29 Oct.), Barnard (27 Sept. to 25 Oct.), van Biesbroeck (up to 
30 Oct.), Graff et al.

parison through a 4-inch telescope. 
The 3™.5 disagreement with Barnard 
was due to differences in magnification and effective light- gathering power 
of the instruments (in the absence of a nucleus and noticeable central con- 
densation). 
Graff and Barnard, and Nijland on 28 Oct. worked, apparently, 
under similar conditions and used similar methods of estimating brightness, 
while another group of observers (Holetschek, van Biesbroeck, Dziewulski, 
Nijlandet al.), using low magnification and extrafocal comparison, agreed 
with one another * 
* This makes obvious the absolute reliability of photometric observations of comets when due allowance is 
made for the systematic effects characteristic of the methods used. A purely formal approach to photo- 
metric cometary data, such as the use of fictitious dependencies on the objective aperture of the instrument 
without taking into account the structure of the comet (nucleus, head), as practiced by Bobrovnikoff, leads, 
on the contrary, to totally erroneous results.

1914 II (1914a). 
Discovered near wy Scorpionis by Kritzinger (Bothkamp) on

through Ophiuchus (April), Hercules, Lyra and Cygnus (May and June);

in November and December, 
located in Pisces. 
Visible throughout the world due to the exceptionally favorable celestial 
position and the sufficient brightness.

1914 V (1913f). 
Discovered 10 months before the perihelion passage by 
Delavan (La Plata) on 17 Dec. as a_telescopic object of 10 to 11™; no tail. 
Located in Eridanus, moving slowly north-west through Cetus (January to 
March 1914), approaching conjunction with the Sun; beginning the end of 
June, visible again in Taurus; 
in July and August, moved through Auriga 
and Lynx; 
in September; passed through Ursa Major; 
in October, through 
Canes Venatici; 
in October and November, through Bootes; 
in December, 
through Serpens; then moved through Ophiuchus (January, February), 
Sagittarius (February, March), Scorpio (April, May), Norma (May), 
Lupus (June, July) and Centaurus (August). 
The exceptionally favorable celestial position and considerable brightness 
made the comet visible throughout the world. 
Extensive series of photo- 
metric estimates with small telescopes, binoculars, and the naked eye 
were made by Holetschek (Vienna), van Biesbroeck (Uccle), Ebell (Kiel), 
Breson (Helsingfors), Valier (Bosen), Abetti (Arcetri), Dziewulski (Wilna 
[Vilnius]), Graff (Bergedorf), Hoffmeister (Sonneberg), Wirtz (Strasbourg), 
Kupffer andSchoenberg(Yur'ev) and also Castro and Maggini (the latter 
with color filters) et al.

Photographed at various observatories,

1914 VI (1914d). 
The thirty-third recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Encke. 
Detected by Barnard (Yerkes Obs.) on 17 Sept. and independ- 
ently by Neujmin (Simeis) on 20 Sept. and Thiele (Bergedorf) on 29 Sept., 
in all cases according to Matkiewicz's ephemeris. 
Upon discovery in Simeis 
and Bergedorf, estimated at 14™.0; D=30"'. 
Located in Perseus, moving 
north-east; 
in October, passed through Auriga, Lynx and Ursa Major; in 
November, traversed Coma Berenices and Virgo, moving to conjunction 
with the Sun. 
14 Oct., observed by Silbernagel (Munich) as an elongated nebula without 
traces of condensation; negligible surface brightness; visible through a 
seeker; estimated at 9™.5. 
11 Oct. 
to 21 Oct., photographed by Tikhov 
(Pulkovo) on the Bredikhin astrograph and a small camera. 
According to 
Murashkinskii, 13 Oct., m=9™.9; 
21 Oct., 
9™.0. 
Plates showed the motion 
of a protuberance in the comet's nucleus. 
25 Oct., spotted by Holetschek 
(Vienna) as a comparatively bright nebula with a very eccentric nuclear 
condensation of 10™; nebula 8 to 10' long and 5 to 6' wide; through a small 
seeker, 
the intrinsic brightness was estimated at 77.5. 
27 Oct., still visible; 
m=7™; size 10'x7'. 
In October and November, photographed by Kostinskii 
(Pulkovo) on a normal astrograph. 
6 Nov., Steavenson (London) observed the 
comet easily through binoculars; 
a starlike nucleus of 8™.5 (6 to 7™).

1915 II (1915a). 
Discovered in Ophiuchus by Mellish (near Madison, 
U.S.A.) on the morning of 9 Feb.; the cometary nature of the object was 
established by the discoverer on 10 Feb. 
Bright comet, D=3', 
a very short 
fan-shaped tail (8 to 9™). Moved south-west through Ophiuchus (February, 
March), Serpens, Scutum (April, May), Sagittarius (May) and Indus (May, 
June); then through Tucana, Hydra, Dorado, Pictor (July), Columba (July), 
Canis Major (August, September), Orion, Eridanus (September) and Taurus 
(December 1915 to May 1916). After conjunction with the Sun, observed in 
Auriga in September 1916, 
16 Feb. 1915, photograph by Thiele (Bergedorf) showed m =9™.17; in 
February, Abetti estimated the nucleus at 107.0. 
18 Feb., Auzan (Tashkent) 
reported a distinct nucleus; no tail. 
Beginning in March, the comet was

(Vienna) saw the nucleus elongate. 
Starting 10 May, a double nucleus was 
observed throughout Europe; the distance between the two components 
gradually increased. 
21 May to 23 May, measurements made from 
Lampland's photographs (Lowell Obs.) showed the distance between the two 
components increase from 54 to 64".

ponents of the comet lying in one line.

nuclei to differ by 1™.5 inmagnitude. 16 May, Abetti gives 38"' for the distance 
between the nuclei.

and the Cape; measured in Cordoba and in Australia.

1917 II (1917b). 
Discovered in Pegasus below a Pegasi by Schaumasse 
(Nice) on 15 April as a 9™.5 object; visible through small telescopes. Rapidly 
moved north; passed through Pegasus, Andromeda, Perseus and 
Camelopardus; located in Lynx at the end of May; in June, moved through 
Leo, descending south and disappearing in the solar rays. 
3 May, Hoffmeister and Hartwig (Bamberg) gave m = 9™.0; circular comet; 
D=2', 
4 May, Bernewitz (Babelsberg) estimated m =8™,5 through a seeker; 
nuclear envelope 0'!.7 in diameter. 
Silbernagel (Bamberg) gave m =9™; 
D=2'; faint because of moonlight, clouds and dawn. 
12 May, Stromgren 
and Braae (Copenhagen) gave

1917 IM (1916b). Discovered by Wolf (Konigstuhl) on 3 April 1916 but 
taken for a minor planet; photographic appearance: nebulous stroke of 
12™.8; located near 6 Virginis, moving north-west; 
the nature of the object 
was subject to discussion for over a month, until the orbit was finally estab- 
lished as parabolic. 
27 April, Palisa (Vienna) reported having observed 
a halo around the object when viewed at the edge of the field of vision while 
no Similar appendage was observed near stars of the same magnitude. 
30 April, Wolf reported a nebulous envelope 15" in diameter; object had 
sharp edges on the west-south-west but was blurred on the side diametrically 
opposite the Sun, as should be the case for a developing cometary tail, halo 
visible through a reflector and a 12-inch refractor. 
Observed for almost 
2 years, passing through Virgo and Serpens (1916), 
Ophiuchus (January, 
February 1917), Aquila (March, April), Delphinus, Pegasus (May, August), 
Pisces and Cetus (October 1917 to February 1918). 
4 May 1916, comet grew fainter; no further changes till6 June. Observed 
by Barnard and van Biesbroeck (Yerkes Obs.) and Sanford (Lick Obs.). 
After conjunction with the Sun, observed on 23 Dec. and 25 Dec. by Gallo 
(Tacubaya) and on 31 Dec. by Barnard. 
January to April 1917, observed at Washington. 26 April, observed by 
Hoffmeister (Bamberg); m=11™; condensation and tail. 
In Konigsberg, 
observed with a faint tail and a coma elongated at 
P=270°; m=11™,

1918 V (1918b). 
The fifth recorded apparition of the short-period Comet 
Wolf (1). Discovered according to Kamienski's ephemeris by Jonckheere 
(Greenwich) on 9 July and independently by Barnard (Yerkes Obs.) 2 days 
later; Barnard estimated 
m=14™; D=15" to 30". 
In July and August, 
located in Vulpecula; then passed between Aquila and Delphinus (September, 
October), passed through Equuleus (October), Aquarius (November, 
December), Cetus (January, February 1919). 
In March and April, located 
in Taurus, 
Was visible at numerous observatories due to favorable celestial position. 
The most comprehensive and protracted descriptions of comet's appearance 
and brightness were collected by Barnard and van Biesbroeck (Yerkes Obs.) 
with 40- and 12-inch refractors, by Chofardet (Besancon) with a 33-cm 
refractor, by Burton (Washington) with a 26-inch refractor and by Abetti 
(Arcetri). Observed also at Algiers, Barcelona, Bergedorf, Heidelberg, 
Johannesburg, Rome, Trieste and Uccle.

1919 III (1919b). 
The second recorded apparition of the periodic Comet 
Brorsen-Metcalf (1847 V). Discovered by Metcalf (South Hero, U.S.A.). 
on 20 Aug. as a bright 8™ nebula in Pegasus. 
21 Aug., discovered independ- 
ently by Giacobini (Nice), 27 Aug., by Leiner (Constance), 28 Aug. , by 
Ostrovlev (Feodosiya), 
1 Sept., Selivanov (Petrograd). 
First orbit calcula- 
tions indicated identity with Comet Brorsen. 
Moving very rapidly north, passed through Lacerta; 
31 Aug., located 
in Cepheus; at the beginning of September, traversed Cepheus, Draco and 
Ursa Minor, passing 13° from the pole; passed through the dipper of Ursa 
Major; 
in the second half of September, descended south; moved between 
Ursa Major and Canes Venatici; passed through Coma Berenices (beginning 
of October), Virgo, Hydra (October, November); located in Centaurus in 
December. 
Observations of physical features and brightness estimates were due to 
Holetschek (Vienna), Nijland (Utrecht), Hopmann (Boni), Jost(Johannesburg), 
Wirtz (Kiel), Hellerich (Kiel), Ebell (Kiel), Luther (Dusseldorf), Abetti 
(Arcetri), Selivanov (Petrograd), Nabokov (Pugachev), van Biesbroeck and 
Barnard (Yerkes Obs.), Vanderlinden (Uccle) and others. 
The comet evolved as follows: 
24 Aug. and 25 Aug., irregular diffuse 
nebulous spot of 7 to 8™, no nucleus (Abetti). 
27 Aug. and 29 Aug., m=7.5 
to 8™; D=8 to 10' (Holetschek, Abetti). 
1 Sept., visible to the naked eye, 
m =5™,8 (Chofardet), 
D=12', m=5™,68(Nijland);

5™.00

P=18° (Nijland).

a narrow ray 1°.5 long was observed on 15 Sept. 
Till the middle of October, 
visible to the naked eye.

1921 II (1921a). 
Discovered by Reid (Capetown) on 13 March as a 9™ 
nebula near B Capricorni; 4 April, discovered independently by Dubiago 
(Kazan') with a 16-cm comet seeker as an 8™nebula. 
Moving directly north, 
passed through Aquila, Delphinus, Vulpecula, and Cygnus inApril; traversed 
Cepheus in May, passing near the pole and continuing through Camelopardus 
and Ursa Major (May); in June and July, located in Lynx, slowly descending 
south; after conjunction with the Sun, moved in Orion and Lepus. 
Because of exceptionally favorable position in the sky and the consider- 
able brightness, the comet was observed throughout the world in April and May. 
Beginning 14 March, observed in Johannesburg. 
23 March and 24 March, 
estimated at 9™, an annular envelope 1'.1 in diameter and a 30"' coma. 
3 April, Innes reported a tail. 
Important photometric observations and brightness estimates as well as 
descriptions of comet's appearance, were made by Holetschek (Vienna), 
van Biesbroeck (Yerkes Obs.), Dziewulski (Wilna), S.V. Orlov (Perm'), 
Chofardet (Besancon), Hellerich (Bergedorf), Luther (Dusseldorf), Wirtz 
(Kiel), Pokrovskii (Pulkovo), Malsch (Konigstuhl), Hoffmeister (Sonneberg), 
Nijland (Utrecht) and others.

1923 II (1923b). 
The eighth recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet d'Arrest. 
Spotted according to Cripps and Dubyago's ephemeris by 
Reid on 1 Dec. 
Later, spotted by Wood on plates taken in Johannesburg on 
5 Sept. and 7 Sept., when the comet was in Ophiuchus (14™). 
Detected for 
the first time by Reid on 10 Nov. as a new comet: large 11™ object. 
It was 
later learned that Mellish (Wilmot, U.S.A.) saw the comet in Sagittarius 
early in the morning of 5 Oct. 
Moved parallel to the ecliptic in Aquarius 
(December) and Cetus (January, February 1924). 
6 Dec., Chofardet (Besancon) estimated the brightness at 12.5 to 13™ 
(central condensation apparently). 
On that night, van Biesbroeck (Yerkes 
Obs.) observed the comet as a faint circular nebula, D = 45", m=13™ and 
a central condensation of 14™.5. 
10 Dec., Graff (Bergedorf) described the 
comet as a very faint circular nebula D=1', no condensation, m= 12™. 
28 Jan. 1924, Baade (Bergedorf) reported an extremely faint envelope; m=16™.

1923 III (1923a). 
Discovered by the physician Bernard (near Madrid) on 
11 Oct. and independently by A. D. Dubyago (Kazan') on 14 Oct. asa large 
circular diffuse 8™ nebula over 8' in diameter; 
located near the horizon 
between Monoceros and Corona. 
Moved very rapidly south-west. 
Data 
concerning the comet were given wide publicity only at the end of October. 
4 Nov., detected again in Musca; 
5 Nov., observed moving north in 
Triangulum Austrinus. 
6 Nov., rediscovered independently by Morshead 
(New Plymouth, New Zealand). 
Passed through Triangulum Austrinus and 
Ara (November) ascending north and approaching conjunction with the Sun. 
After conjunction should have been located in Scorpio and Ophiuchus in

Discovered by Wolf (Heidelberg) on plates taken on 
22 Dec.; 
a very faint 16™ cometary object.

moved south in Taurus throughout the entire observation period.

than 17™.

1925 I (1925c). 
Discovered in Pegasus by Orkisz (Mount Obs., near 
Cracow) on 3 April as a 9™.0 object. 
Moved north-east; passed through 
Pegasus (April), Andromeda and Cassiopeia (May); at the beginning of June, 
located 8° from the pole; descended south through Camelopardus (June) and 
Ursa Major (July); 
in February and March 1926, moved between Ursa Major 
and Leo Minor; 
in April and May, moved through Lynx. 
Beginning 6 April to 8 April, observed throughout the world as a circular 
condensed nebula 4 to 5' in diameter; 
intrinsic brightness estimated at 
77.5 to 8™.0; nucleus 12™; very faint tail. 
Beginning the end of April, after 
a period of low altitude and lunar interferences, 
conditions were exception- 
ally favorable for observations.

1925 II (1927j). 
The short-period Comet Schwassmann-Wachmann (1). 
Discovered photographically by Schwassmann and Wachmann (Bergedorf) 
on 15 Nov. 1927 as a circular 13 to 14™ nebula with D=2' and a distinct 
nucleus. 
At opposition located in Pisces, moving extremely slowly south- 
west. 
It was later established that the orbit was almost circular, 
slightly 
inclined to the ecliptic and situated between the orbits of Jupiter and Saturn. 
Therefore, 
the comet, like a planet, can be observed at all points of its 
orbit, with oppositions every 13 months;.-however, 
the comet is usually 
accessible to large instruments only. 
23 Nov., Castro (Santiago) gave m=12™ through a 28-cm refractor, D=2'. 
The most conclusive and continuous brightness estimates and general ob- 
servations were due to van Biesbroeck: 
26 Nov., m=14™5, nucleus Lem, 
D=6'.7; 
28 Nov., 
15”; 
1 Dec., 
16™; 
14 Dee., 
14™. 
This variability in the 
comet's brightness, 
as reported by van Biesbroeck, was confirmed by 
Baade's observations; 
incidentally, 
this seems to be a specific feature of 
all comets which move at considerable distances from theSun. InJanuary and 
February 1928 brightness dropped to 17 and even 18™; from September to 
December 1928, m=i6".5. 
6 Nov. 1929, Baade described the comet as 
fainter than 17™, but already on 2 Dec. he gave m=13™.5. 
Brightness flares 
up to 15™ [sic] reported 
by van Biesbroeck, Schorrand Baabeon23 Nov. 1930;

The appearance of the comet also varied with the brightness flares; 
it 
was 
observed 
as

either

D=0':3; 
5 Dec., D=30", an eccentric 14".5 nucleus; 28 Dec., van Biesbroeck 
gave D=1', a sharp eccentric nucleus; 
30 Dec., coma D=24" with a 16™.5 
nucleus. 
Changes in appearance were also observed in December 1932 and 
January 1933. 
In 1940 the comet's spectrum was photographed by Mayall (Lick Obs.). 
15 Sept. 1941, 
a sharp flare of 20-hours duration when the comet was trans- 
formed from a diffuse nebulous spot into a starlike object with a very faint 
coma. 
In January 1946 another brightness flare was reported. 
In March 
1947 Jeffers gave 
m=19™. 
Recently, van Biesbroeck has estimated the 
comet at 16 to 18™.

Orbit: Behrens. —A.N., 245; 309. 
Proceeding from brightness estimates for 1927 and 1928 (normal bright- 
ness 16-17™), C.A.M. gave Hjg=5™.0. 
In brightness flares, the Hig-values 
would increase to 2 to 0™ 
D,=10' (external envelope) and 4 to 5! (normal 
state). 
Brightness fluctuations of Comet Schwassmann-Wachmann 1 were studied 
by Richter, A.N., 271: 207. 
He tried to correlate them with solar activity 
and fluctuations in the ultraviolet radiation; 
later, he associated them with the 
solar corpuscular streams which give rise to magnetic disturbances, ASSNES 
277: 12 and 280. 
These streams (corona rays) should be held responsible 
for the brightness flares of comets at substantial distances from the Sun. 
Spectrum observed by Mayall, P.A.S.P., 53: 340, and Herbig, 
P. eas) Bares 
58:60, during periods of increasing brightness. 
In both cases the spectrum 
was continuous without the usual cometary bands. 
However 
any 
conclu- 
sions concerning the dust nature of the comet are quite premature.

1925 IV (1925d). 
The eighth recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Tempel(2). Spotted photographically according to Crommelin's 
ephemeris by Stobbe (Bergedorf) on 11 July [sic —must be June], and by 
Delporte (Uccle) on 12 June as a 12™.0 object with a nucleus and a faint fan- 
shaped coma pointing north-west. 
Located near n Serpentis, moved south 
through Scutum (July), Sagittarius (July, August) and Microscopium 
(September); ascended north through Aquarius and Cetus (December), be- 
coming accessible to northern observatories at the end of the year. 
17 June and 18 June, H. Struve and Pavel (Babelsberg) and Hoffmeister 
(Sonneberg) described the comet as a large diffuse nebula of 11™.0 witha 
conspicuous condensation of 12™.

1925 VI (1925a). 
Discovered by G.A. Shain (Simeis) on 22 March from 
double-astrograph plates as an 11™ object near f Virginis; 
D=1'; slow 
~ 
retrograde motion. 
23 March, discovered independently by Comas Sola 
(Barcelona). 
Moved west, passing through Leo in April and Sextans, where 
it was observed till 18 June; after conjunction with the Sun, observed from 
the middle of October in Sextans (October, December), Hydra (January 1926), 
Puppis (February) and Monoceros (March). 
After the second conjunction, 
the comet was observed in October 1926 between Monoceros and Canis 
Major; descending south, passed above Sirius, traversed Lepus (December 
1926 to February 1927) and passed to Eridanus (March).

1925 XI (1925d). 
First discovered by Peltier (Delphos, U.S.A.) on 
13 Nov. as a bright 8™ nebula located above v Bootis; however, according 
to the same report, 
it was not detected on 16 Nov. either at the Harvard or 
the Yerkes Observatories. 
19 Nov., discovered by Wilk (Krakéw[Cracow]) in 
Hercules (8™.5). Afterwards identified on Harvard plates of 16 Nov. and 
17 Nov. 
and on an 18 Nov. plate taken in Babelsberg. 
Moved very rapidly 
south-east; 
18 Nov., encounter with the Earth, 4=0.58a.u.; passed through 
Hercules, Aquila, Capricornus and disappeared in the solar rays at the end 
of December; 
after conjunction, 
at the end of March should have been located 
in Microscopium. 
In November and December, observed visually and photographically at 
numerous observatories. 
22 Nov., van Biesbroeck (Yerkes Obs.) described 
the comet as a diffuse 7”.8 nebula with D=2' and a large nucleus; plates 
showed a faint narrow tail 15' longs 28 Nov., m=%7™.3 in moonlight;

in diameter, without noticeable condensation.

1926 III (1925e). 
Discovered by Ensor (Pretoria, South Africa) on 13 Dec. 
in Reticulum; m= 8™, tail 15' long. Moved south-west, reaching Tucan; 
then moved north-west through Grus (January 1926), approaching conjunction 
with the Sun; after conjunction, located in Aquarius at the end of February; 
moved rapidly north through Delphinus, Vulpecula, Cygnus, Lacerta, 
Cepheus and Cassiopeia; located in Camelopardus in the middle of April. 
Gradually grew brighter; 
in January Ensor reported 
m=6™.5, 
a 1° tail. 
Observed in the southern sky at Johannesburg, Melbourne, 
Rio de Janeiro 
and the Cape. 
At conjunction, the comet apparently underwent considerable 
changes in general appearance. 
After the conjunction, observed first in

===PAGE_0258 (book p.440)===
Leningrad on the night between 22 Feb. and 23 Feb. 1926 from an earth-tied

a faint 20' tail was 
reported.

1926 VI (1926e). 
The third recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Giacobini-Zinner (the 1920 apparition was overlooked). 
Spotted 
according to Cripps' ephemeris by Schwassmann (Bergedorf) on 16 Oct. 
as a 14" nebula in Ophiuchus. 
Later, detected on photographs of 6 Oct. and 
8 Oct. (14™). 
Slowly moved south-east through Ophiuchus (October), Serpens, 
Scutum, Aquila (November), Capricornus (December), Aquarius (December 
1926, January 1927) and Cetus (January, February); 
in March 1927, located 
in Eridanus. 
In spite of its substantial southern declination, 
the comet's 
favorable position with respect to the Sun made observations convenient. 
A photograph taken on 29 Oct. by van Biesbroeck (Yerkes Obs.) showed 
the comet as a nebula with m=13™.8, D=18'' anda 
diffuse 14™.5 nucleus; 
a main tail 1'.5 long at 
P=57°and another, fainter tail 1' long at 
P=40°. 
6 Nov., m=13™; 40-inch refractor revealed a clear nucleus ina nebula 
with 
D=18"; asymmetric tail in the first quadrant (P =60°) and a faint ray 
1' long (P=40°). 
25 Nov., Chofardet (Besangon) reported m=12 to 12™,5 
through a 30-cm refractor. 
Maximum brightness in December when m=11™, 
Measured at Tokyo, 
Bergedorf, 
Besancon and Nice.

1926 VII (1927b). 
Discovered by Reid (Capetown) on 25 Jan. 1927 asa 
conspicuous 8™ object in Tucana. 
Moved north-east through Tucana, Hydrus, 
and Eridanus. 
Observed only at the southern observatories Johannesburg, Calle and 
Santiago. 
In January and February, 
8™; Reid described the comet as 
similar to 1927 II; a large, bright, circular nebula. 
At the beginning of 
March, brightness dropped to 9™.5, the comet growing very faint and much 
larger in size; 
at that time, visibility conditions in the northern hemisphere 
were unfavorable. 
In March, comet was well visible in the morning sky; then 
it was repeatedly looked for at Yerkes Observatory but without success. 
Van Biesbroeck described it as fainter than 14™; Reid however reported 
that the comet was observed till April.

1927 IV (1927d). 
Discovered by Stearns (Middletown, U.S.A.) on10March 
as a 10™ nebula 2° north-east of B Librae. 
Slowly moved north-west through 
Virgo (April); then located in Bootes (May to August), moving north-east; 
in December 1927, observed in Corona Borealis; 
in 1928, moved east 
through Hercules (January to March); then locatedin Ursa Minor and Draco 
under favorable conditions for observation; 
in 1929, having passed through 
Draco (January, February), located in Cepheus (March to December); in 
July and August, passed 14° from the pole; in 1930, observed in Cepheus; 
in 1931, passed to Cassiopeia where it was observed in March. 
Numerous measurements and descriptions of appearance were made 
during the long observation period. 
The longest series of visual and photo- 
graphic observations were by van Biesbroeck (Yerkes Obs.) using a 40-inch 
refractor and a 24-inch reflector.

a 16™ nebula.

on
