According to Astapovich, 
on 26 June and 27 June the comet was 2™ 
brighter than the Andromeda Nebula. 20Julyand21 July, Danjon (Strasbourg)

nucleus through the Meuson refractor, estimating its size at 400m. 
Photo- 
graphed also by Barabashev and Evdokimov (Khar'kov Observatory).

variations in the relative intensities of several bands.

ning of June, the most intense was the 3838A band, followed by 4050A; 
the 4690A band was considerably weaker.

intense; total intensity of the spectrum was higher.

1927 IX (1927k). 
A bright comet discovered at the end of November in 
Australia and South America. 
Beginning 28 Nov., observed in New Zealand 
by O'Connell; from 3 Dec., observed by Skjellerup (Melbourne); from 
5 Dec., by Maristany (La Plata); a 1™ head anda 3° tail. On the morning 
of 15 Dec., discovered independently by Chidamera, 
an assistant at the 
Kodai Kanal Observatory; located in Norma three solar diameters from 
the center of the Sun; moved north-east through Ara, Scorpio, 
Ophiuchus, 
Serpens (December) and Sagittarius (January, February 1928).

1928 II (1927h). 
The thirty-seventh recorded apparition of the short- 
period Comet Encke-Backlund. 
Discovered according to Matkiewicz's 
ephemeris by van Biesbroeck (Yerkes Obs.) on 13 Nov. 1927 as a circular 
nebula, D=24", m=16™, distinct nucleus. 
After discovery, 
spotted on plates of 19 Oct. and 20 Oct. as a 17™ object. 
Located in the south-east part of Pegasus, slowly retrogressed; 
in January, 
passed through Pisces; 
in February, rapidly descended south, moving 
through Aquarius and Capricornus. 
Before the perihelion passage, 
spotted and observed on 17 Jan. 1928 by 
Tikhov (Pulkovo) on the Bredikhin astrograph and by Pokrovskii on the 15- 
inch refractor. 
20 Jan. and 23 Jan., photographed by Deich on the normal 
astrograph; estimated at 97.5 with an 11™.0 nucleus. 
23 Jan., Tikhov ob- 
tained a high-quality spectrogram with an objective prism mounted on the 
Bredikhin astrograph; 
the spectrogram showed all the characteristic cometary 
bands. 
4 Feb. and 5 Feb., Martynov and Dubyago (Kazan') reported a 4 to 
8' tail and a clear nucleus. 
At that time, van Biesbroeck's photographs 
showed a clear nucleus and a wide 5! tail pointing west; m=8™, 
In January 
and February, Steavenson reported m=6™; nearly circular D=2', 
an almost 
starlike nucleus situated eccentrically on*the eastern side of the coma. 
Last 
observation before conjunction with the Sun was by van Biesbroeck on 12 Feb. 
when the comet was a nebulous 8™ object low in the sky soon after sunset. 
After the conjunction, observed photographically in Johannesburg from 
20 March to 3 April as an 11 to 12™ object.

1928 Ill (1928b). 
Discovered visually by Giacobini (Paris) on 17 March 
through a window in the clouds as an 11™ nebula to the west of v Orionis; 
after a few minutes! observation, appeared to move rapidly south. 
To con- 
firm this point Giacobini delayed the report, but because of illness had to 
discontinue observations; 
the report on the comet was dispatched on 24 March. 
Hoffmeister (Sonneberg) 
spotted a faint cometary trace near the position 
of the new comet on a plate 23 March; Carrasco (Madrid) located a definite 
nebulous object on the edge of a plate taken on 26 March; finally, Mineur 
(Paris) detected a faint diffuse trail (10™.5) on a plate taken on 28 March. 
If all these objects were really Comet Giacobini, whose actual existence 
was not doubted by the discoverer, 
then it moved east rather than south. 
Comet was unsuccessfully searched for by Wilk (Cracow), Comas Sola 
(Barcelona) and van Biesbroeck (Yerkes Obs.). 
Doubts were expressed as 
to the identification of the four observations with a single object; neverthe- 
less, Carrasco and Crommelin calculated cometary elements which are in 
fairly close agreement.

Photographed at Pulkovo, Yerkes Observatory, Moscow, Simeis and

Comet considerably active with condensations in the tail. 
In April, 
the straight tail appeared to bifurcate; 
24 April, the main tail observed to

In May, 
the tail grew considerably shorter.

sation;

Vorontsov-Vel'yaminov classified the tail as type I. The spectrum of the 
comet was studied by Tikhov, Circ. Pulkovo Obs., 2: 14; Barabashev and 
Semeikin, A.N., 241: 307 and Beyer, A.N., 238: 332. 
(r, A): 21 March —(0.53, 1.07); 9 April —(0.56, 0.96); 29 April —(0.87,

1930 IV (1930b). 4 March, Beyer (Hamburg) detected a nebulous trail 
extending north-north-west on a plate taken on 26 Feb. with a 6-inch astro- 
graph; the discovery was confirmed not before 11 March or 12 March; 
m=10™.5, 
After the first orbit calculation, 
the comet was detected on photographs 
taken at Babelsberg by Prager on 23 Jan., 2 March and 3 March, and also 
on Harvard plates taken between 18 Feb. and 19 March; an 11™.0 object. 
After more precise elements had been determined and the ephemeris had 
been calculated for the period before the discovery, the comet was detected 
on 10 plates taken on 9 Oct. and 30 Oct., and also on 6, 7 and 27 Nov. 1929 
by van Gent (Johannesburg). 
Located in Gemini, moved through Auriga (April), Lynx (May, June), 
Ursa Major (June to August), Canes Venatici (September), Bootes, Corona 
Borealis (November) Hercules (December 1930, January 1931), Lyra 
(February to May), and again Hercules (June, July). 
In the first half of 1930 the comet was accessible to medium- power instru- 
ments; observed at Babelsberg, Simeis, Hamburg, Milan, Vienna, 
Wiesbaden, Heidelberg, Athens, Pulkovo, Kazan', Engelhardt Obs., Trieste, 
Padua, and other places. Dubyago (Kazan') reported on 20 March, m= 11™.0, 
nucleus 127.5; 23 March, m=10™5; 
24 March, 
10™ 8.

with a 40-inch refractor and a 24-inch reflector.

1930 VI (1930d). 
Discovered by Schwassmann and Wachmann (Bergedorf) 
on 2 May as a rather bright 9™.5 nebula in Corona Borealis. 
After discovery, 
detected on plates taken by Schneller (Babelsberg) on 27 April and 29 April.

gated 6" nebula.

(June); in August, located in Fornax.

19 May, nebula described at Athens as spindle-shaped,

Observed by Baldet (Meudon) with a 233-inch refractor on 29 May, 
30 May and 2 June, when nearest the Earth. 
Baldet found a very small 14™ 
nucleus (assuming the nucleus to be a rigid body, we obtain a 0.4km diam- 
eter); 
15™ stars disappeared near the nucleus, but an 11™ star was visible 
through the nucleus. 
An elongated envelope (5'x1'); two diffuse fan-shaped 
jets issuing from the nucleus; a gaseous nucleus 3".5x2'.5 surrounded the 
starlike nucleus, which was situated to the west of center.

1931 IV (1931c). 
Discovered by amateur-astronomer Ryves (Saragossa) 
on the morning of 10 Aug. as a bright 5™ object east of x Geminorum. 
Moved east to the Sun, gradually increasing in brightness; disappeared in 
the solar rays and rediscovered in October in Leo (above @ Leonis); slowly 
moved east through Sextans (November, December).

Comet Grigg-Skjellerup.

the west of Procyon.

Leo Minor, Ursa Major, Canes Venatici, Bootes (June), Corona Borealis 
and Hercules (July).

1932 V (1932k). 
Discovered by Peltier (Delphos, U.S.A.) on 8 Aug. as 
a bright 8™ object between Perseus and Aries. 
Independently discovered by 
Whipple on an 6 Aug. Harvard Plate; spotted by Sace (Southern California) 
on 9 Aug. Moved rapidly north through Perseus, 
Camelopardus (August), 
Draco, Ursa Major (September, October) and Bootes (October, November). 
10 Aug., visible to the naked eye; m=5 to 6™; D=5'; 
a 30! tail at P= 249°; 
H. Struve (Babelsberg) gave m = 8™.3 (obviously the central condensation). 
13 Aug., Lacchini (Pino-Torinese) reported an 8™.5 nucleus; 
D=4!'; tail 
over 12' long at P=259°. 
Observed throughout the world due to substantial brightness and very 
favorable celestial position. 
Several photometric comparisons and bright 
ness estimates obtained by Florya (Tashkent) from 21 Aug. to 5 Oct., 
Dziewulski (Wilna) from 26 Aug. to 1 Oct., Krumpholz (Vienna) from 
20 Aug. to 19 Sept., Zweck (Graz) from 16 Aug. to 9 Nov.; Chernyi (Kiev) 
from 20 Aug. to 4 Nov., and particularly Bernheimer (Vienna) from 13 Aug. 
to 11 Sept.* and Beyer (Hamburg) from 18 Aug. to 7 Oct., who studied 
thoroughly the brightness and the shape of the tail, Fragmentary brightness 
estimates were obtained also by van Biesbroeck (Yerkes Obs.), Parenago 
(Moscow), Pokrovskii (Pulkovo), and other observers.

1933 IV (1933d). 
Discovered on a special plate for minor planets by 
Carrasco (Madrid) on 15 July as a 10™ object in Virgo, south-east of Spica. 
The plate was exposed twice for 15 minutes each time, and the motion of 
the comet-like object was clearly noticeable. 
On 17 July two plates were 
exposed —one for 15 minutes, 
the other for 30 minutes; 
a diffuse image of 
the comet was obtained on the second plate only. No other observations; 
apparently, comet grew faint very rapidly.

An exceptionally intense continuous 
spectrum with ordinary Fraunhofer lines and several unknown lines; the 
CN band varied greatly in intensity in different parts of the head; the C

of the Sun.

1937 VI (1937h). The fortieth recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Encke-Backlund. 
Spotted according to Crommelin and Matciewicz's

Triangulum.

in December, located in Ophiuchus,

north-west.

Discovered independently on 15 April by two amateur

now Kestym, Udmurtskaya ASSR) as a bright 3™ object well visible to the 
naked eye; a tail over 4° long.

1939 IV (1939b). 
The first recorded apparition of the short-period Comet 
Vaisala.

(1939 CB) of 15™in Leo.

at Turku revealed the cometary nature of the object.

1939 VI (1939h). 
The second recorded apparition of the periodic Comet 
Caroline Herschel-Rigollet. 
Discovered in Taurus by Rigollet (Lagni) on 
28 July as a diffuse 8.0 comet with no tail. 
Moved rapidly north-east; 
passed through Auriga, Lynx (August), Ursa Major (September) and Canes 
Venatici (October, November); 
in December 1939 and January 1940, moved 
slowly through Canes Venatici. 
* The considerable differences between the various estimates are due to the light-gathering power of the 
instruments used and depend in large measure on the comparison methods employed; 
these factors play 
a major role in reports on comets of large angular dimensions.

1939 VII (1939g). 
The seventh recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Brooks (2). Detected by Jeffers and Adams (Lick Obs.) on 17 June 
as a diffuse 17™ object without condensation or nucleus; located in Pisces 
near Cripps' ephemeris.

Pisces (June to August) and Aries (September, October); veered south 
and

1939 VIII (1940a). 
Discovered, almost 3 months after the perihelion 
passage, by Kulin (Budapest) on 6 Jan. 1940 as a minor planet (1940 AB) 
of 15™.8 in Cancer. 
19 Feb., reported that the diffuse appearance and the 
considerable eccentricity of the orbit indicated that the object was a comet. 
Moved north-west. 
Observed at Budapest on 11, 12 and 30 Jan. as an object slightly more 
diffuse than the surrounding stars. 
Apparently, comet grew rapidly faint, 
since not detected by Jeffers (Lick Obs.) on 6 April plates of the Crossley 
reflector or by van Biesbroeck on 1 April and 3 April plates of the McDonald 
82-inch reflector revealing 19™ stars. 
References: BPZ) 22" Pop. Astr: » 46° PlAlS. © 42; Jez

1939 X (1939k). 
The eighth recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Tuttle. 
Detected according to Crommelin's ephemeris by Jeffers 
(Lick Obs.) on 12 Aug. as a faint 18™ nebula with a nucleus but no tail. 
Moved between Auriga and Camelopardus; then moved south through Lynx 
(September), Cancer, Leo (October), Sextans (November), Crater, Hydra 
(November) and Centaurus (December). 
Upon discovery, Jeffers reported a faint coma D=18". 
16 Sept. and 
25 Sept., observed by van Biesbroeck (Yerkes Obs.) as a very diffuse 
nebula.

a well-developed central con- 
densation; 
m=9™.5.

eincular coma, 
no tall.

m=13™.

1940 I (19391). 
The fifth recorded apparition of the short-period Comet 
Giacobini-Zinner. 
Detected according to Cripps' ephemeris by van 
Biesbroeck (Yerkes Obs.) on 15 Oct. as a 15™ ppuls near 4 Ophiuchi. Moved 
south-east through Ophiuchus (October, November),’Serpens and Aquila 
(December). 
Visible only in the evening low above the horizon after sunset, 
thus inconvenient for observations. 
Upon discovery, appeared as a small and very diffuse nebula with central 
condensation; D=15"'. 
In November, very faint because of low altitude. 
References: Obs., 62; Pop. Astr., 47, 48; A.J., 49; B.Z., 21. 
Orbit: Cripps. —B.A.A. Hbk., 1939. 
According to Richter, A.N., 273, H. 2, Hjyg=10™8. 
In Oct., D=0'.7.

2.10). 
1940 Ii (1939m). 
The twelfth recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Faye. 
Discovered above a Capricorni according to Henderson and 
Harris' ephemeris by Jeffers as a small diffuse coma with a nucleus m = 16™. 
Moved east through Capricornus and Aquarius. 
Observed only with large instruments in U.S.A. 
14 Dec., Jeffers 
reported m=15™; D=78". According to van Biesbroeck (Yerkes Obs.), 
5 Nov. and 11 Nov., 
a small circular coma of 16™. 
3 Jan. and 6 Jan. 1940, 
m=15™, blurred nebula; 
almost as bright as Comet Brooks II, yet observed 
with difficulty owing to the low position in the south-west. 
References: B.Z., 21, 22; Pop. Astr., 47; A.J., 49; L.O.B., 503;
